## Thread: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs — 17 msgs

### Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net>`

```
I have seen a fair number of monolithic COBOL programs generated by 4GL's
that are well over a quarter of a million source lines long.  Just a few
weeks ago I corrected a compiler problem that could only be encountered, as
it turns out, when a monolithic program had more than 65K paragraph or
section-names.   The test program that the customer submitted with the
trouble report was 356,117 source lines long.  Now that I know what the
problem is, I could probably demonstrate it with a program as small as, oh,
say, 67,000 lines, more or less ... my regression test actually expands
(through COPY) to 81,130 lines ...

        -Chuck Stevens

Tim Josling <TIMJOSLING@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net...
>
> On a related question, I would be interested in the longest COBOL program
anyone is
> aware of, including copybooks. I know of one that is about 35,000 lines of
code
> (this was split due to copiler limitations once it reached around that
number).
>
> Anyone aware of anything larger?
>
> Working on a free COBOL compiler I am interested to know what is the max
size I am
> likely to come across in practice.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net> <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Jumping in here, not in response to anybody in particular.

Chuck Stevens wrote in message <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
>I have seen a fair number of monolithic COBOL programs generated by 4GL's
>that are well over a quarter of a million source lines long.  Just a few
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


One of the problems here is that nobody has said what constitutes a
"program".  Are we talking about something that starts with identification
Division, or are we talking about a single executable that may contain a
hundred subroutines?

If the latter, I have several systems (single executable, single menu) that
run well over 100,000 lines and run on a PC.  They are not written as a
"single program" simply because that is not the correct way to write a huge
program.  It can always be broken down.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<838dpk$sm1$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net> <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net>`

```
My guess (and it is only a guess) is that at least SOME of the "programs"
that are referenced as over 100,000 lines REALLY are a "single program" -
starting with an Identification Division and not including any "separate"
programs, nested programs, or called programs.

In "ye bad olde days" on IBM mainframes (using pre-85 Standard compilers),
such programs were really NOT that uncommon in large MVS shops.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385914f0.3888119@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net> <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net> <838dpk$sm1$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Wed, 15 Dec 1999 09:57:13 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>My guess (and it is only a guess) is that at least SOME of the "programs"
>that are referenced as over 100,000 lines REALLY are a "single program" -
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agreed.  I haven't nominated the behemoth that I worked on earlier
this year for that reason.

My behemoth is really a couple dozen programs.  We just refer to it as
Report 57.  The begining program is a small "traffic cop" program,
that calls five 50,000 to +100,000 line programs, with about a dozen
smaller programs that are called from any of the calling five.  And
those dozen may call other programs.

The five biggest would only be anywhere from 3500 to 9500 lines of
code uncompiled, but 80 data copybooks and 20 procedure copybooks
cause each program to bloat very quickly.  (BTW, with 80 data
copybooks, 88 levels are a nightmare).

And it continues to get smaller, as procedure division copybook
routines get replaced with called subroutine programs.  But then
again, new code is always added to it.

It's still a real bitch to work with.

Moving to more modular structures has solved some problems, but it
created some new ones.  All calls are static.  You forget to recompile
a key subroutine, in the correct order, and unpredictable results
occur.  Sometimes it crashes, sometimes the data is wrong and you're
beating your head against the wall, trying to figure out why.

And BTW, I heard about the monster at FDR, when I contracted there,
but fortunately, I never worked on it.




Tim Oxler
Teo Computer
http://www.teo-computer.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38592C42.AAC50071@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net> <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net> <838dpk$sm1$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <385914f0.3888119@news.teo-computer.com>`

```
I was just thinking - ask a C++ or Java or even Visual Basic programmer
what the length of one of his programs is.  It will be harder to get an
answer from them than it is for COBOL programmers.  We read about how
many lines Windows 2000 is, but have no idea what that means.  

Is a function call one line?  A copy member?   Is a 5 line statement 5
lines?  Comments?  At least we can FIND our code!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991228233954.22112.00001826@ng-cp1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<838dpk$sm1$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
>My guess (and it is only a guess) is that at least SOME of the "programs"
>that are referenced as over 100,000 lines REALLY are a "single program" -
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>    wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com


Hmmm - I've worked nothing but big iron for  almost 25 years and have never
seen a COBOL program of over about 9,000 lines of code.  I just got done
rewriting one of about 8000 lines down to 3700 (had all logic in it 4 times)
and there's a 8500 line CICS program I'm itching to get my mits on to cut it
down to size.

What I have seen though is a RPG program that used 2 1/2 boxes of cards (that
sure dates it) and all indicators twice. Used 1 digit for the year - when the
decade changed there was major problems. It was a payroll program and it took
out deductions for the next decade from the first checks of the year. The union
was not pleased :).  The decision was made by management to have it converted
to COBOL and someone suggested that it be sent to one of those companies that
said they could convert any RPG program to COBOL.  Four of these companies
advertised to send a sample to check out their work - so we sent 'The Chinese
Dentist'  (program name ended in 2-30 - or as our punster would say "tooth
dirty"). It brought 3 companies to their knees but we did get code (?) back
from the fourth.  None believed it was a real program.  The punster spent the
last year of his career doing the rewrite into COBOL by hand :).

Eileen (in her alter ego - on vacation for the week).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Largest COBOL application suite (was Largest Progran)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6ks882j5k290@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<838dpk$sm1$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <19991228233954.22112.00001826@ng-cp1.aol.com> <Ojpa4.2351$wd2.49292@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
This thread was "longest program (in Cobol)" to answer a compiler guy's
question:
"How much code (in a module) do I have to compile at once?"
We saw answers up to 350,000 lines, although I'm not sure that answer was "a
single module".

We build automated software re-engineering tools (see
http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.htm).
Our question is, "How much COBOL code makes up a single application
system?",
as we are interested in analyzing the entire system at once.   Our tools are
presently designed to
read a million lines into a single NT workstation and process them, but
we've seen
a few application systems that were 5 million lines of COBOL.

Who out there is reading this newsgroup with larger systems?  How large do
they really get?

Ira Baxter, Ph.D., CTO   idbaxter@semdesigns.com     512-250-1018 x140
Semantic Designs, Inc.   12636 Research Blvd #C214, Austin Texas 78759

Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>YukonMama wrote in message
<19991228233954.22112.00001826@ng-cp1.aol.com>...
>>....  I just got done rewriting one of about 8000 lines down to 3700 (had
>all logic in it 4 times)


(PS: Clone detection (see our website) might have found and removed this
automatically).
-- IDB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<838emp$qi3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net> <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net...
> Jumping in here, not in response to anybody in particular.

> One of the problems here is that nobody has said what constitutes a
> "program".  Are we talking about something that starts with identification
> Division, or are we talking about a single executable that may contain a
> hundred subroutines?

In this case we are talking about a single program of 356,117 lines, written
in the ANSI-74 dialect, with no IPC calls, no nested programs, no
separately-compiled pieces (in COBOL74 or otherwise).  The program has
exactly one each of the four DIVISIONs required in that dialect.  The only
"control transfer" statements in the program are GO and PERFORM.   We are
indeed talking about a monolithic single executable, compiled in one "gulp",
without *any* subroutines at all.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3857EB6E.F8AA915E@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net> <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net> <838emp$qi3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
I was thinking of a compilation unit (id idivision to
EOF/end-program), in terms of what a cobol compiler needs to be
able to cope with. I would be interested to hear how long these
really large programs take to compile. I remember one program
that took over an hour on a dedicated system (just the compile. ,
not the link edit)
Tim Josling
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Largest Progran was: what is the avarage length of cobol programs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<838vu4$i7u$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<82gbnr$bb2$1@news.netvision.net.il> <82n91g$ij5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <384F2041.5C7823A8@home.com> <82pii90209t@enews2.newsguy.com> <38512C2B.928D03F9@prodigy.net> <836jh0$ml8$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <8385cv$4mi$1@news.igs.net> <838emp$qi3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3857EB6E.F8AA915E@melbpc.org.au>`

```
This is indeed a single compilation unit.  The program is not intended for a
PC, nor is the code produced compatible with the conventions that obtain in
that environment.   The object code from the compilation of this single
compilation unit is directly executable; no link edit is needed (or even
appropriate, in this case).

Elapsed time for compilation of this particular program seems to be well
under six minutes on a lightly-loaded single-processor Unisys HMP NX4800,
and right around eight on a six-processor Unisys A18 with over 300 active
jobs running at the same priority.

    -Chuck Stevens

Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:3857EB6E.F8AA915E@melbpc.org.au...
```

```
For those who don't know MERANT's (aka Micro Focus') current naming
conventions, "Server Express" most definitely IS a "COBOL product" - and the
person asking the question provided all the "required" information for posts
to comp.lang.cobol.

Having said that, I don't personally know the answer to the question - or I
would be giving it instead of just commenting on this other post.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Server Express - Support for large files greater than 4 gig.

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83al6g$4v8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3856dcc8.0@news.dol.net>`

```

Lou de Freitas wrote in message <3856dcc8.0@news.dol.net>...
>We have recently installed v1.0 of Server Express on a Sun Solaris system.
>We cannot create files greater than 4 gig.  Does anyone know if this
product
>does support seq files greater than 4 gig?
>
>

Yes Micro Focus Server Express 1.0 does support files > 4 gig. One needs to
set up a configuration file extfh.cfg.

A quote from the File Handler Documentation book from Section 6.2.1.1

"The configuration option you need to set in order to create
and access a large file is the FILEMAXSIZE option. This option works for
sequential, line sequential, relative and indexed files"

Chapter 6 in the file handler book tells you all about how to set up the
configuration file.

(I've never actually done this myself, I'm just quoting the documentation).

Regards,
Steve.
```

---

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Royal Doulton - What Language Did They Use?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BkW54.172$A24.4093@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<384e93f9.11397962@news.enter.net> <384EA876.38F6B4A3@home.com> <XjR34.47034$oa2.399220@iad-read.news.verio.net> <82rbas$ah6$1@news2.tor.accglobal.net> <i7d44.47940$oa2.415466@iad-read.news.verio.net> <0wh44.908$uN2.28359@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38551652.6809F7EE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <b4d54.741$Sj6.20539@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <R%f54.10354$lL.108634@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <3855F5B0.2AD4A266@worldnet.att.net> <ucB54.10678$lL.119013@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <3856B609.3BE89380@worldnet.att.net>`

```

>
> I'm asking, not answering. I wouldn't know where to look to consult
the
> Rabbinical debates of the 15th & 16th centuries. Please, what did
they
> decide. (being ignorant in these matters, why might turkeys be
different
> from chickens, which we all know are kosher). I don't know what the
> dietary laws are regarding birds. If one considers the penguin a
bird,
> then it might be kosher.

As I understand the original debates, God said don't eat this bird,
that
bird, etc., but didn't say which could be eaten. So the rabbis tried
to
figure out the rationale God used (sorta like a programmer trying to
deduce (I almost said 'divine') the logic necessary to code a user's
request).

They finally gave up and instituted their own rule (again, similar
to business programming): those birds mentioned
in the Bible as being acceptable in the Temple were OK (pigeons,
quail, etc.) and by extension, domesticated fowl (chickens). All this
was
formulated and fixed into law by the third century. Then came
Columbus and turkeys in the new world.

"Can we eat turkeys?" said the colonists.
"What's a turkey?" said the sages of Europe.
"It looks like a big chicken (kosher), but it has this red thing
hanging down over its beak like a vulture (non-kosher). And
we don't know whether it's a scavenger (non-kosher)?"
continued the explorers.
"Mutter, mutter." said the rabbis, as they turned to the
original program's flowcharts.

Ultimately the rabbis decided a turkey was more like a chicken
than a vulture and said "Go ahead and eat the damn thing."

I don't know whether a specific request has been made in
the case of penguins, but I bet the official rabbinic response
would be "Get a life, fool."

P.S.,
I do know that you can't make a penguin kosher (or a chicken,
for that matter) simply by circumcising it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Royal Doulton - What Language Did They Use?

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839d8d$2mo_003@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<i7d44.47940$oa2.415466@iad-read.news.verio.net> <0wh44.908$uN2.28359@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38551652.6809F7EE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <b4d54.741$Sj6.20539@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <R%f54.10354$lL.108634@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <3855F5B0.2AD4A266@worldnet.att.net> <ucB54.10678$lL.119013@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <3856B609.3BE89380@worldnet.att.net> <BkW54.172$A24.4093@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
In article <BkW54.172$A24.4093@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>,
   "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
+
+>
+> I'm asking, not answering. I wouldn't know where to look to consult
+the
+> Rabbinical debates of the 15th & 16th centuries. Please, what did
+they
+> decide. (being ignorant in these matters, why might turkeys be
+different
+> from chickens, which we all know are kosher). I don't know what the
+> dietary laws are regarding birds. If one considers the penguin a
+bird,
+> then it might be kosher.
+
Whether one "considers" penguins to be birds or not is irrelevant. They *are* 
birds. And chickens are not, in and of themselves, kosher. For a chicken (or 
any other animal) to be kosher it must, among other things, be slaughtered and 
butchered in a prescribed manner. I believe there are also religious laws 
governing who may perform the slaughtering and butchering, and establishing 
standards for cleanliness and for the handling of the meat, all of which must 
be followed strictly for the meat to be kosher. I'm quite sure that a chicken 
cannot be kosher, no matter how it's slaughtered, if it is butchered on a 
table that has also been used to butcher hogs.

I believe you are confusing kosher/non-kosher with clean/unclean. Hogs are 
unclean, and cannot be kosher under any circumstances. Cattle are clean, but 
beef is not necessarily kosher, depending on how the animal, and later, the 
meat, are handled. Processed and handled in accordance with kosher law, 
the milk and cheese of a clean animal (such as a cow) can be kosher, and as I 
said, so could beef -- but a cheeseburger can't.
```

```
I am not sure that oo and dynamic memory allocation need be synonymous.  To
be sure, object creation requires a pointer, and that a new thread be
created.  There are methods of doing that, however, that are much simpler
than the sort of memory havoc that happens in C.
```

```
Re : Constants and prior messages referring to Level 78s. The most
obvious didn't strike me, when I was originally quoting their use.

When using the M/F Dialog Editor (parallel to the M/S Dialog Editor), in
addition to providing a myapplication.rc and myapplication.h (used for
linking) the latter is also "translated' into mfres.cpy which contains
the equivalent of #define, and the controls are hard-coded as LEVEL 78s
:-

	78 IDOK			value 1.
	78 IDCANCEL		value 2.
	78 ID-CUSTOMER-DIALOG   value 400.
	78 ID-CUSTOMER-NUMBER   value 401 etc......

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

### Re: When are we going to get Cobol 9x?

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ph995scim5v2v3ggupg0qrgd5qac3tfdnl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<384BF493.9A64870B@pipeline.com> <82gtkk$9as$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The venerable "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> bestowed
upon comp.lang.cobol on Mon, 6 Dec 1999 12:00:25 -0600 these words:

>Probably why I always told people NOT to call it 9x Standard - because I
>never did believe it would be approved in the 1990's.  I am medium hopeful
>that it will be approved in the 2000's (changing only the last digit) - but
>wouldn't be completely surprised if it never gets approved.

I'm calling it COBOL-20XX.  If I'm wrong, by the time I'm wrong, I'll
be dead.  If I'm wrong and not dead, I'll keep working on COBOL-21XX.
```

- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <3842BDE8.942020E7@NOSPAMhome.com> <822ah5$1gc$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ex14.6774$Ic5.79616@news3.mia> <826dpi$fpk$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <3846C4A5.C1A66591@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <826slb$peq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847487c.537138075@news1.attglobal.net> <827r49$tb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847D524.4FE1ADBA@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <829ct3$cuo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3848725F.D1A0FA7C@att.net> <38488E05.35632487@home.com> <3849017F.B8D940BF@zip.com.au> <384CA162.C7B5367D@home.com> <82ja74$5ej$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <Sva34.2737$Ze7.107325@news3.mia> <82jcc6$7aj$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <3853c3f1.43035700@news1.attglobal.net> <3853F9A2.ADBA8CA6@home.com> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message news:Gtu54.2727
> The question
> is "Do we *really* have to have this feature in COBOL, and are the

I tend to agree with you. But sometimes it is convenient, I made for
instance once a function module in Cobol, working pretty much like cell in a
spreadsheet, you could even nest formulas. If I were to solve this in
another way I would have had to use indexed files, if I wanted to use static
tables, I would have had to make those huge to make sure they would keep
pace.

The linked list I ended up with was two dimensional. Held by an array
outermost.

The first dimension was each line. Each line then had a user defined set of
columns and each column could consist of formulas. Like:

Line 1
..
Line 99 (Table)

For each line there were:

Column 1 .. Column n

And each column then had a formula list:
Formula 1 .. Formula n

Where a formula could point to a new formula in itself.

I chose to limit to possible nesting to three level deep.

Obviously this was/is terrible to debug, so, I am just happy it works :-)

Cheesle
```

```
Yes.  But it is non-trivial.

Do you plan to run it in a DOS box under Windows95/98, or are you running it
in actual DOS mode?

Does the scale have to be legal for trade?  If so, where?

You say "lever" scale.  I have not seen a lever scale in use for at least a
decade.  Do you plan to upgrade it or convert it?  You cannot "read" a
lever.

<8363c2$hl2$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net>...
```

## Thread: NetExpress RunTime Distribution — 17 this month / 19 total

### NetExpress RunTime Distribution

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839cls$1i6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Anyone out here actually distribute MicroFocus NetExpress applications...???

I am very curious how you are distributing the MF Runtime modules (redist
application server)...

For well over a year now, MF has distributed/posted "FixPacks" to NE...These
however do not update the redistribution modules in the redist application
server portion of NE...I have complained several times...I have reported
problems that even today continue to go unanswered my MicroFocus
(MERANT)...I have received responses from them such as - they cannot provide
support for the problem because I do not use the OUTDATED redist application
server runtime...Instead, I try to use current modules from the NE/Base/bin
directory, which are current with the fixpacks...

Today, I asked them...."OK, when will you provide me a current reist
runtime?"

They're formal response is "This remains under study. It should be ready
soon. What will be available is a Service Pack that can be used to update a
Redist Run-time installation. However, this will probably not become
available until after the current problems with Service Pack 1 are
resolved."

How are other developers distributing their products developed with NE to
clients?

kenmullins
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress RunTime Distribution

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839d3m$gvj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<839cls$1i6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Forgot to mention...What this means is that it is not possible to distribute
a product using NetExpress and expect it to be supported by Merant...

kenmullins
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress RunTime Distribution

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83agkj$4ba$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<839cls$1i6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <839d3m$gvj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken,

Could you explain why ? I don't understand how the lack of an automatic
update mechanism indicates that redistributable files are not supported by
Merant, which is what your comment seems to suggest.

I sympathise with your point about the redist directory not being
automatically updated, but the issue is actually more complicated than that.
A system may have more than one development system and/or multiple
applications. Typically those applications contain redistributable files
from the COBOL system, rather than a single redist directory, so in order to
bring everything up-to-date the installation mechanism would need to scan
the entire hard disk for any occurrences of any redistributable files and
prompt the person running the update as to whether an updated was desired on
each occurrence found, as it is quite possible that updates would only be
wanted on certain apps. I would imagine that there's quite a lot of work
involved in the coding and interface of such a mechanism and I'm sure the
group working on the technology want to make sure it works first, hence what
you were told.


Ken Mullins wrote in message <839d3m$gvj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
>Forgot to mention...What this means is that it is not possible to
distribute
>a product using NetExpress and expect it to be supported by Merant...
>
>kenmullins
>
>
```

```
"We are up against the same wall as previously. If a standard install is not
being used Development will not participate in resolving non-standard
installation issues."

Now how can i do a standard install today, when the runtime modules are
outdated more than a year...???

kenmullins
```

```

Gael Wilson wrote:
> 
> Ken,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> automatically updated, but the issue is actually more complicated than that.

Gael,

All that you say may well be true. But the fact is, this is an OLD
problem, brought to the notice of Answerlab, by somebody other than Ken,
and I have to go from memory, well over a year ago.

Somebody didn't quite think this one through - yes there have been  Fix
Packs to adjust/fine-tune existing package, but the thinking missed the
boat on ensuring Redistribution was updated. Classical example, as I
recall, PC_PRINT was updated but not the files in Redistribution.

A recommendation I made, and I hope Newbury, (or whoever), follows
through - use a Project Wizard to select only those files needed from
the Redistribution required for a particular end-user - at the moment
you have to 'chuck' the lot at them, so you don't miss a feature you
need - and it sure as hell doesn't fit on a 1.4 floppy !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>

```
Ken,
      Our product has been developed on NetExpress and we had the same
problem.  On one of the support calls we had I mentioned it to the support
person and he shipped me a copy of a .bat file they use at Merant.
Basically you copy the files from wherever they live (mostly
NetExpress\base\bin and NetExpress\DialogSystem\bin) into your redist
directory.  We have a redist directory on a shared drive and when our app is
built we dump down all of those files.  It isn't small, but we haven't had a
problem yet.  After I load a fixpack I run this .bat file and it copies all
of the files into this redist directory so it constantly stays updated.

bill
```

```
Bill Wood wrote:
> 
> Sadly, this is true.  For example our entire application is written using
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I tried that Bill; all I wanted was OO and single-threading, no Dialog
System, no.... etc. Tried to figure it from the information in the
on-line help - short story - what I did, didn't work; in my case user
has a laptop so he came over and plugged into my desktop and transferred
the whole shebang that way.

Just not satisfactory. Don't get me wrong, overall I still think
NetExpress is a great product - but this Redistribution/Runtime Support
- sheesh ! There is a simple and effective answer, and I'm curious why
they haven't gone that route -  use a Project Wizard to determine what
you want, e.g. :-

	Do you use OO			Yes/No
	Single Thread/Multi		S/M
	Do you use Windows APIs		Yes/No
	Do you use Dialog System	Yes/No
	Do you use Fileshare		Yes/No
	Do you ....... etc.

and whatever necesary permutations on the above to make it fit.

I fail to the see the problem to achieve the above; fairly short
analysis required, followed up by one program to copy and build the
run-time package you want. Merant ?????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3865B99F.9BE31B2D@home.com...
> Just not satisfactory. Don't get me wrong, overall I still think
> NetExpress is a great product - but this Redistribution/Runtime Support
> - sheesh ! There is a simple and effective answer, and I'm curious why
> they haven't gone that route -  use a Project Wizard to determine what
> you want, [snip]

i agree that this feature is much needed; i have forwarded the stream on to
MERANT Product Management for consideration.

technically, the solution is simple. what isn't so simple is the testing
requirements that a multi-personality run-time environment (RTE) imposes (i
assume you'd like the resulting RTE's tested? ;-). hence the RTE is tested
as a whole, although 'whole' in this instance actually means two RTE's
(single-threaded & multi-threaded).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress RunTime Distribution

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8467tc$4qu$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<839cls$1i6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <uxRGwhbS$GA.274@cpmsnbbsa05> <83ii07$rus$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <utBy57uT$GA.287@cpmsnbbsa05> <3865B99F.9BE31B2D@home.com> <gAw94.13524$du4.320843@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```
There also needs to be a way to include only the modules required by your
app...why distribute dialog system dll's, etc when they are not used by the
app? if app is single threaded, why distribute the multithreaded dll?...if
the app is a client side app, why distribute fileshare?...etc...etc...etc...

A wizard sounds like a good ideal to me...maybe have it create a directory
of runtime modules needed upon completion of the wizard...Or at minimum, an
exact list of modules required to distribute...
```

```
Ken Mullins wrote:
> 
> There also needs to be a way to include only the modules required by your
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> exact list of modules required to distribute...

Yes Ken, I think Gazloo misunderstood a bit. Let me just re-state it. Do
away with Distribution Diectory completely, all the files required exist
in the bin directories. Ask the "Wizard" questions and create
'customized' Support/RunTime according to developer's selection. We all
realize that many individual modules will be 'common' to variants of a
customized output - let developer pick his own name if necessary, and he
can select, as examples :- Multi-with-OO, Single-with-OO,
Dialog-System-with-OO, Dialog-System-Multi-Thread etc, etc....  Choosing
as many different versions as he may need, according to an
end-customer's requirements.

It then becomes a process of one program selecting from the bin
directories,( NOT Redistribution directory), and copying to a directory
for each customized RTE selected.

Hope that makes it just a bit clearer.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3866DB1F.99F41EFA@home.com...
> Yes Ken, I think Gazloo misunderstood a bit. Let me just re-state it. Do
> away with Distribution Diectory completely, all the files required exist
> in the bin directories.

ya know, i don't think i did misunderstand; unless of course i've done it
again? ;-)

you're asking for custom RTE's -- i.e. only those files you need to host the
application. i can understand the need (why ship files you don't use), but
as i said, it does present some challenges for MERANT in terms of testing
all the various possible configurations of the RTE's. keeping the RTE whole
keeps it stable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress RunTime Distribution

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<846s6n$d0b$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<839cls$1i6$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <uxRGwhbS$GA.274@cpmsnbbsa05> <83ii07$rus$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <utBy57uT$GA.287@cpmsnbbsa05> <3865B99F.9BE31B2D@home.com> <gAw94.13524$du4.320843@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <8467tc$4qu$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <3866DB1F.99F41EFA@home.com> <LiC94.13801$du4.327653@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Jimmy and Gary,
  The other possibility is to *NOT* test all the variations on packaged
RTEs - but instead to issue a "user-friendly" message if a required run-time
module is missing at a distributed site.  This should mean that the *ONLY*
bug that the end-user-site would have is that they are missing a module (some
answer in the Wizard was wrong - or not current).  This could be corrected by
the "distributor" of the RTE.  Any "bugs" other than this "missing module
one" should be "recreatable" with the full RTE - so MERANT not testing the
variations should make no difference.

NOTE WELL:
  If the end-user reported problem could NOT be recreated by the distributor
of the application - with the full RTE - then you are in deed in a "problem
condition".  However, I think that this is where the "onus" could be placed
on the distributor (to insure that the end-user had all the correct modules)
and get rid of Ken's problem of MERANT just refusing to deal with "different"
or "non-current" installations at end-user sites.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumbest COBOL Article Ever?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38601ef5.31517954@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<19991214001118.05252.00000758@ng-fa1.aol.com> <38569815.23309809@news.enter.net> <836jkr$p71$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3857c957.12293562@news.enter.net> <3857E3FF.F959D3A1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <83ab9f$8ja$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <83bf0j$rke$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Thu, 16 Dec 1999 14:45:45 -0500, donald tees wrote:

>I never saw one without at least a drum.

The first punches I used, IBM 026 and 029 used drum cards for
formatting, columns, etc.  These machines actually had drums on which
said drum cards resided.  I think there was a card verifier (056?)
that had a similar drum setup.

An other machine I later used (I think it was a Univac punch/verifier,
don't remember model) used a similar card that was read into memory.
I also remember some incombatibility with the 026/029, but I think
this was mainly due to the concurrent switch from BCD to EBCDIC.

A later IBM punch (I think the 129) worked similarly; the drum card
was read into memory; there was no drum.


>Rick Price wrote in message <83ab9f$8ja$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>...
>>You had a programmable card punches?   Lucky!

>>Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
>>news:3857E3FF.F959D3A1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...

>>> Huh?  The 60's were just the other day...  Back when I had to know how
>>> to program the card punch to make writing COBOL easier.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumbest COBOL Article Ever?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3860ECB1.682FB094@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<19991214001118.05252.00000758@ng-fa1.aol.com> <38569815.23309809@news.enter.net> <836jkr$p71$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3857c957.12293562@news.enter.net> <3857E3FF.F959D3A1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <83ab9f$8ja$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <83bf0j$rke$1@news.igs.net> <38601ef5.31517954@news1.frb.gov>`

```
WDS wrote:

> On Thu, 16 Dec 1999 14:45:45 -0500, donald tees wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> was read into memory; there was no drum.

AFAIK, the card you just punched was in a read station and you could either
punch a column or duplicate the same column on the previous card.  Before the
129 one could insert a column in an otherwise duplicated card by holding the
card in the read station while punching the new column.  A column could be
removed by pulling the read card by one column.  While the 129 added some nice
features, being able to shift duplicated fields was no longer possible.
```

<a id="t-dd84a12f50"></a>
## Thread: Pointers — 9 msgs

### Pointers

- **From:** "Chip Scardo" <cscardo@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yRP54.4253$t65.121594@news.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
Hi all,

I am quite new to COBOL, but have tons of C, C++, and ASM experience.

I have, for whatever reason, a field like this:

05  ADDR  PIC S9(9) COMP-4.

A call to an assembler program is used to place in this var the address
of a field, in another program:

05  SOURCE PIC X(5)

CALL MYAMSPRG USING ADDR, SOURCE.

Basically, to keep from using the whatever the COBOL equivalent of address
getting is.

Now, I have another field,

05 TARGET PIC X(5).

I need to move the data pointed to by ADDR to TARGET.  (and remember
SOURCE is in another program, so I can't just MOVE SOURCE TO TARGET).

How do I do that in COBOL?

In C, it would be: memcpy(target,addr,5).

Thanks
Chip Scardo
```

#### ↳ Re: Pointers

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<838i25$u4l$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<yRP54.4253$t65.121594@news.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
Please tell us which COBOL compiler (vendor and release) you are using an on
which operating system.  Depending on the answer to that question, the answer
to your question will either be "easy" or "hard".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Pointers

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<838j6f$er3$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<yRP54.4253$t65.121594@news.rdc1.tn.home.com> <838i25$u4l$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Private email received which stated,

"Thanks...
IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0"

    ***

Therefore, the answer is,

1) Change the definition of ADDR to

    05  ADDR Usage Pointer.

2) Keep your CALL statement as,
  CALL MYAMSPRG USING ADDR, SOURCE

3) Add a new entry in your LINKAGE SECTION as follows:
  01 Temp-TARGET PIC X(5).

4) After you call statement add the following two COBOL statements:
   Set Address of Temp-Target to ADDR
   Move Temp-Target to TARGET

      ****

After this statement, your 05-level "TARGET" will contain the contains of
whatever is at the storage location passed from your assembler routine.

NOTE TO C.L.C. readers,
  VS COBOL II does support USAGE POINTER, but SET ADDRESS can only be done on
a Linkage Section item.  No COBOL compiler (that I know of) allows a SET
ADDRESS of an 05-level.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pointers

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83915m$l0k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<yRP54.4253$t65.121594@news.rdc1.tn.home.com> <838i25$u4l$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <838j6f$er3$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <838j6f$er3$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
<Snipped> >

> NOTE TO C.L.C. readers,
>   VS COBOL II does support USAGE POINTER, but SET ADDRESS can only be
done on
> a Linkage Section item.  No COBOL compiler (that I know of) allows a
SET
> ADDRESS of an 05-level.
>

I believe this statement also to be true. However, if the POINTER is
redefined as a fullword-binary field, then you can ADD a displacement
to it ( similar to an Assembler 'A  Rx,=F'DISP' ). But, doing things
like this in COBOL is certainly NOT the norm and you'll probably have
people staring at you like you've grown another head out of your
shoulder :-)

HTH....

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pointers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8399pk$ncf$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<yRP54.4253$t65.121594@news.rdc1.tn.home.com> <838i25$u4l$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <838j6f$er3$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <83915m$l0k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Having worked at Micro Focus when we were trying to get an "explicit"
statement out of IBM about whether arithmetic on redefinitions of USAGE
POINTER items was or was not supported, let me tell you "coder beware".  It
may work today, it may work on the platform that you are currently running
on, but do NOT count on it working tomorrow or on other IBM-supported
platforms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pointers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00

```

  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> Having worked at Micro Focus when we were trying to get an "explicit"

USAGE
> POINTER items was or was not supported, let me tell you "coder
beware".  It
> may work today, it may work on the platform that you are currently
running
> on, but do NOT count on it working tomorrow or on other IBM-supported

> Bill Klein

Bill,

Please don't get me wrong. I'm NOT ( by any stretch of the
imagination ) advocating this technique as it can result in the normal
IBM verbiage "Unpredictable Results May Occur" :-)

I believe that POINTERS on the AS/400 are doublewords, which shoots
this technique in the foot.

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pointers

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991222021230.23435.00001249@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<839erk$vdt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>It
>> may work today, it may work on the platform that you are currently
>running
>> on, but do NOT count on it working tomorrow or on other IBM-supported
>> platforms.

I've noticed, during the long road from OS/VS COBOL to COBOL for MVS and VM,
that the code will change to match the documentation.  If the technique is not
explicit or implicit in the IBM documentation, there is a very good chance that
a future release or PTF will "break" it.

Frankly, I'm not sure whether this is a good thing or not.  It seems I've spent
at least 12 of the last 15 years removing the "elegant" code that someone long
gone installed, and no one can now maintain, or is getting in the way of a new
vendor function.  It does seem the most "bullet-proof" code consists of only
the 15 or 20 most commonly used commands (ok, maybe 25).  But then, I've always
been simple-minded.
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pointers

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3860E30A.9AFE1F8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<839erk$vdt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <19991222021230.23435.00001249@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
Steve Newton wrote:
> 
> I've noticed, during the long road from OS/VS COBOL to COBOL for MVS and VM,
> that the code will change to match the documentation.

Gee, and I always thought that documentation (especially flow charts)
was something you wrote after the coding was finished, and had to turn
something in to close the project.  If you were in a hurry, one would
just grab some other documentation and change the name, otherwise, one
would figure out what the code does, and write the specs to match it. 
Then hide it away where nobody will ever find it until the code has been
changed too much to be recognized.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pointers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991222102959.06424.00000770@ng-fz1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3860E30A.9AFE1F8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard Brazee writes...

>Steve Newton wrote:
>> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>changed too much to be recognized.

Actually, in the IBM world (and others, I suspect), technical writers are often
given the design specs in advance; while they are writing their books the
coders are coding; it's not at all uncommon for the resulting code to not
behave exactly as originally specified (usually in subtle, relatively
unimportant ways); then when users discover how the code actually works and
exploit "undocumented features" or they get away with things the pubs say you
can't do, developers are busy revising to work more closely to the specs.
Moving target time.

Regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
----- Original Message -----
From: Roger Johnson
To: Massimo Morgia
Sent: Tuesday, December 14, 1999 10:50 PM
Subject: Re: Sign maintenance


I appreciate your pricing concerns. I do not know all the pricing history
with Fujitsu but will try to explain the present pricing methodology.
The current price of the Enterprise Edition is $ 3,000 US. Annual
maintenance is $500 per year if paid prior to the expiration date. If a
customer allows his maintenance to expire and then wants to get back on
maintenance, an upgrade fee of $500 is charged in addition to $500 per back
release plus the annual maintenance of $500. Therefore, since you are on
version 4.0 and version 4.2 is the latest version, the above scenario fits
you.
$  500 for the upgrade fee
$  500 for the back release
$  500 for the annual maintenance
$ 1500 total cost to upgrade to version 4.2 and place product under
maintenance for one year from date of upgrade purchase.
I hope the above explains the cost.
```

```
....Fujitsu Bashing snipped ....

I'm no big fan of high prices.  I think ALL of the COBOL compilers are
priced too high.  However, let me defend Fujitsu a bit here.  

First, they are one of the few (perhaps only) vendor that allows you
to purchase the level of tools you need, rather than buying the whole
store.  I use the lowest level - Standard COBOL - which allows me to
do everything I want, and costs me $150.00 a year.  

Second - before 4.2 came out Fujitsu offered everyone, ad Nauseum, the
chance to ADD ON maintenance, even if they bought the compiler WITHOUT
(also something no one else offers).

You are being asked to pay that upgrade and the maint since, plus the
maint for the next year - which will get you v5 - for free.

When you bought the compiler you KNEW it didn't come with maintenance
-- unless you paid for it.  If you now need it, it's time to pay the
piper or live with what you have.

I do agree however, that the new prices are generally too high.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Maintenance BLACKMAIL

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83q5si$1tl$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<837dje$88l$1@lacerta.tiscalinet.it>`

```
Mr. Morgia,

Thank you for communicating your concerns to us. You have pointed out an
inconsistency in our pricing that we are keen to rectify.

Our goal has been to make sure that our customers who are buying
maintenance regularly get the best deal. In the past year we have offered a
number of special deals that would enable customers not on maintenance to
sign-up for maintenance and receive upgrades at a reduced price. We assume
that you either were not registered, were not the person who received our
mailings, or chose not to take advantage of those special offers.

That said, we agree that asking you to pay more than our special price for
those moving to Fujitsu from competitive products is not right. As most of
our existing customers either bought maintenance or took advantage of our
special offers during the last year, you are the first person to point out
the inconsistency in our upgrade-pricing algorithm. Thank you for doing
this. It is not our intention to trap our existing customers into paying
for products or services they do not wish to purchase.

We have therefore reviewed our upgrade pricing and are putting the
following prices into immediate effect. Prices quoted are only for the
Enterprise Edition. Please check our Web Site for the full pricing changes:

Upgrades from V4.0 to V4.2, or V4.2 to V5.0:
With 1 year's maintenance: $1125
Without maintenance:  $625

Upgrades from V4.0 to V5.0 will be:
With 1 year's maintenance: $1295
Without maintenance:  $895

(Above prices will be good for you until March 31st, 2000. Generally, of
```

## Thread: Nested IFs, is there an alternative? — 28 msgs

### Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

- **From:** "michael vasmatics" <vasmati@snet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```

Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> Mike Frederickson wrote:
> >
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> homework assignment).

Ok so much for the pros they had there say, can't figure the BLOW thing if
your checking the value.

So as one student to another consider this, lets keep it simple and answer
the quistion .
"IF" works fine, you just need the right IF so it doesn't look like
spaghetti code. LOOK AT YOURS THEN LOOK BELOW.

Why couldn't you just code, a simple compounded if statement?
IF this can't be done then the pros can tell us why.

IF GRADE = 'A' OR ' B' OR 'C' OR 'D' OR 'F' OR 'W' OR ' I'
    MOVE  'Y' TO GRADE- CORRECT
ELSE
    MOVE 'N' TO GRADE- CORRECT
END-IF.

this seems straight forward and readable, and you can use PIC X,
your testing for the value of grade so if the condition isn't met it wont
pass.
If your testing for it, how could it BLOW,  a simple class  test for
alphabetic wont do at all,
I just got finished with a COBOL class so I'm no expert but I don't see
anything wrong whit a compounded IF statement.
You need to reread the section on a compounded IF condition.

Here is something along this compounded idea I just used in a program I
handed in
for a presort condition to weed out bad records before sorting

600-VALIDATE.
           IF  EMPL-NUM   NUMERIC
               AND
               TER-NUM    NUMERIC
               AND
               OFFICE-NUM NUMERIC
               AND
               YR-SALARY  NUMERIC
               AND
               SS-NUM     NUMERIC
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
               MOVE PAYROLL-REC-IN TO WS-FAILED-REC
               MOVE 'YES' TO BAD-REC
           END-IF.

       610-EX-VALIDATE.
           EXIT.
See its all compounded, IF all these fields are numeric its a good record
release it to sort otherwise its a bad record
so don't release it , write to a bad records report.
These are different fields. BUT, its single dependent, although on more then
one variable but its still  yes or no or 1 of 2 things can happen ,bad input
or good input.
Nested  IF as I understand it is for multiple branching.
LIKE  this:
IF person is a salary  employee, just move pay in to pay out, ELSE he is
hourly go to hourly calculation, IF hourly with more then forty hours
of work calculate regular pay and OT pay ELSE he dint work OT, so just
calculate regular pay.
See how this thing branches depending IF person is hourly or salary and IF
he is hourly did he work OT or not, a simple yes or no wont do.
Depending on the condition, 1 of 3 calculations is used, see the "branch
once and branch again" this has to be nested.
Your stuff doesn't need to be nested its a yes or no, it just needs to be
compounded. One line will do it, and its easy to read.

For data validation you should reread the following statements IF, INSPECT
and EVALUATE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3858FCB3.662E9055@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net>`

```
michael vasmatics wrote:

> > I never use PIC A.  I would rather check myself to see if it is
> > alphabetic than have my program blow.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> your checking the value.

The word is "you're", not "your" - sorry for being off-topic but I have
a personal problem being too easily irritated with that misspelling. 
Back on topic:

You can blow when you reference a PIC 9(5) alphabetic value.  I believe
you can blow when you reference a PIC A(5) numeric value.  And checking
for it being numeric or alphabetic is referencing it.  

I don't know of any upside to using PIC A instead of PIC X.
```

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:3858FCC8.E64BB428@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
>
> You can blow when you reference a PIC 9(5) alphabetic value.  I believe
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't know of any upside to using PIC A instead of PIC X.

I agree with what you stated, and I'm sorry about the spelling mistake, I
am, I admit, terrible at spelling, even simple words.
It leads to people thinking that I must be a simpleton for that I'm sorry.

Since I'm only a student although a very old one, I tried to explain that my
answer may not be the best. I saw nothing wrong with his code except that
the nested IF should have been a compounded IF. I tried to tell him why, I
also tried to tell him what he needed to revisit.
I guess the lesson learned here is for students to read and listen. I just
felt that, If he couldn't figure out the nested IF, how is he going to
figure out LEVEL 88 special names. As for the PIC A, I have yet to use it, I
use PIC X myself but that doesn't make it wrong.
He was expecting an ALPHA field and choking if the value coming in was
correct. I used packed decimal in a program and if a non numeric shows up
the program chokes.
I didn't realize that the answer was to use it as PICK X first, then check
to make sure its numeric, then turn it into a packed decimal
to make it more efficient.
In either case I didn't mean to offend anyone, if I have, for that I
apologies. My intent was to show why a compounded if would be better,
and what other things he needed to read to get a better understanding of how
to set it up his validation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> I agree, let's keep it simple whenever possible.  Class, 88s, 

Huh? No 88-levels? I'm going to disagree, especially since we can both
test and set the 88 from COBOL II onward (revealing my IBM mainframe
orientation). Using the 88's is far more symbolic & self-documenting
than coding:

IF	some_field		=  1...
    or
IF	some_other_field	=  'Q' ...

IMHO, anyway.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch wrote in message <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net>...
>Jeff Baynard wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
What a neat thread this turned out to be...we had examples of compound IF, a
dissertation on the use of English (spelling and grammar), Michael (whose
contributions I ALWAYS read...informative and usually amusing) asked for
"YES" to represent the GOOD and "NOPE" to represent the BAD...What
represents the UGLY, Mike? Oops, sorry, most of the code examples posted do
that just fine...

So here's my contribution (in flawless English and perfect grammar,
correctly spelled, using no joined-up writing and a large crayon...)

1. Can we please stop using "YES" and "NO" or "Y" and "N" in code?

My objections are based on having worked in different countries where the
native language uses "Ja" or "Si"  or something else instead of "YES". Lets
make COBOL international and assign a value of "1" for "TRUE" and "0" for
FALSE ("1" = "GOOD", "0"=BAD, " "= UGLY (indeterminate) for Michael...)
so...

(01  RECORD-FLAG  pic x value space.
        88 GOOD-RECORD    value "1".
        88 BAD-RECORD        value "0".)

IF (all that stuff)
     SET GOOD-RECORD TO TRUE
ELSE
     SET BAD-RECORD TO TRUE
END-IF

Obviously, I can't force anybody to apply this as a standard, but you would
make an old man very happy if you did so...(and maintenance programmmers
from all ethnic backgrounds will call down blessings upon your house...)

2. The original contributor wanted to know about alternatives to nested IFs
(remember that...?)

Well, I don't propose to discuss alternatives here, (don't see why I should
be the ONLY one on topic, and besides, it would involve too many specific
examples and it's Sunday here in NZ and it's clear blue sky and I'm going
fishing and having a few beers this afternoon with some friends, so I
haven't got all day for this...) but I'm happy to post a few simple rules
which may help some of you to know when to use a nested IF.

Pete's rules for nested IFs:

1. If you can avoid using them, do so.

2. There is ONE valid case where you SHOULD use a nested IF... (actually
there are probably others, but this is the only time when I will DEFINITELY
write a nested IF)

If there is a "decision tree", use a nested IF. Here's a simple decision
tree, nested to 3 levels...

IF c1
     IF c2
          IF c3
               a1
          ELSE
               a2
      ELSE
             a3
ELSE
        CONTINUE

Before you get incensed at the "bad code" let me explain. I have
deliberately omitted the END-IFs for clarity. You should read the above as:
(The "c"s are conditions; the "a"s are actions, so c1=Condition 1, a1=
Action 1)

If c1 is TRUE, AND c2 is TRUE, AND c3 is TRUE, do a1.
If c1 is TRUE, AND c2 is TRUE, AND c3 is  NOT TRUE, do a2.
If c1 is TRUE, AND c2 is  NOT TRUE, do a3
If c1 is NOT TRUE, just carry on

So we have a branching tree-like structure of conditions and actions.

Remember that all the conditions above may be "any conditional expression"
so you could include compound conditions in your decision tree. Also, the
"action" can be any valid COBOL construct, including "IF". This brings us to
the next important rule...

3. ONLY nest your IFs into the TRUE branches of your decision tree. This
keeps it simple. While COBOL will support having ELSE...IF, in my opinion,
this is inelegant and best avoided. (I confess there are occasions when I
have done it, but I was young and foolish at the time...). So here's the
code for the tree above...

IF c1
     IF c2
          IF c3
               a1
               ELSE
                     a2 (Note: we avoid putting further IFs here...
                            If you REALLY need to have some tricky stuff at
this point, move it
                            into its own section and PERFORM it...)
           END-IF
     ELSE
           a3
     END-IF
END-IF

OK, that's really all I wanted to say on the subject. Students please note,
and if your lecturer disagrees tell him to mail me...(You just can't get the
help these days...)

Now, wheres that bait box....

Pete.









>IF some_field =  1...
>    or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385C5DC3.3396AE6C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> 1. Can we please stop using "YES" and "NO" or "Y" and "N" in code?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> FALSE ("1" = "GOOD", "0"=BAD, " "= UGLY (indeterminate) for Michael...)

Interesting you should mention this:-) For the last several years ('92
or so, when we moved up to COBOL II), I've been defining switches as PIC
9 and placing them in their own 01-level (no VALUE clauses). In my
initialization logic I say:

	INITIALIZE		SWITCHES

So the default value for all switches is ZERO, which usually means no
error. Other values are from 1 thru 9 and mean whatever I need for the
task at hand. If I have many potential values, the field is PIC 99. When
you're writing display or print programs, any switch = ZERO means no
errors. Any non-ZERO value is a subscript to a table of error messages.
For YES/NO or BON/MAL or JA/NEIN, etc., ZERO is the default and anything
non-ZERO is the opposite.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385D392D.80BA3B77@home.com>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385C5DC3.3396AE6C@worldnet.att.net>`

```


Bill Lynch wrote:
> 
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> initialization logic I say:

My approach is similar. In the case of M/F OO there are classes which
will return boolean isTrue isFalse - so Merant recommend you use a Pic
x(4) comp-5. My 'tests' tend to be 95% numeric, exceptions being as
follows, where user is entering only one character :-

	evaluate inputField
	  when "d" or "D" display "Dual"
	  when "m" or"M"  display "Mixed"
	  when other      display "Screen Mode"
	End-evaluate 

Tied in with that comp-5 approach, (and not knowing implications on
mainframes - as you and Pete have discussed), the comp-5 means that you
don't have to remember how many digits you allocated for a routine - in
your case you said sometimes pic 9, others pic 99. This tied in with
'flagging' errors allows you to go to your error table knowing that you
can always rely on the comp-5 being of sufficient size.

We've been discussing the merits of IFs and EVALUATEs but we didn't 
advise the original questioner on 'hit frequencies'

 	evaluate true
	  when Red   do this
	  when White do this
	  when Blue  do this
	  when other do this
	End-evaluate

Yep. I've written the colours as most of us in the Western democracies
think of them - and as an example it is probably difficult to suggest
which should come first. But that isn't the case with the following, for
a department store group, and the same concept applies to the particular
branch of commerce you are dealing with  :-

	evaluate true
	  when Fashions   do this
	  when ShopinShop do this
	  when Food       do this
 	  when other      do this
	End-evaluate

It's a fair bet 'Fashions' will give you the 80/20 Pareto rule. Don't
know American terminology for 'Shop-within-Shop' but UK version means 
trade-names like Aquascutum, London Fog or in perfumeries, Chanel,
Charlie etc. operating as separate 'shops' within the store. They
probably come out second. Food used to be a factor in department stores,
but with the onslaught of supermarkets, tends to be a minority
speciality department. So based on that knowledge it is possible to make
this type of Evaluate( or IF ) meaningful in terms of hit-rate.

Above doesn't fit the bill ? You can divide it down further :-

	evaluate true
	  when Fashions
	    evaluate true
              when LadiesFashions    do this
	      when ChildrensFashions do this
	      when MensFashions      do this
	      when other	     do this
	    End-evaluate   
	  when ShopinShop do this
	  when Food       do this
 	  when other      do this
	End-evaluate

Now take those tables above for a large supermarket chain, which also
dabbles in 'Dry Goods' and you reverse the order, splitting the Food
down into its own departmental categories. It's 25 years since I was
involved with Food, so we would need one of our supermarket specialists
to give us the Pareto breakdown.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83isvj$bbp$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385C5DC3.3396AE6C@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch wrote in message <385C5DC3.3396AE6C@worldnet.att.net>...

>Interesting you should mention this:-) For the last several years ('92
>or so, when we moved up to COBOL II), I've been defining switches as PIC
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch
Hmmmm....I take no issue with what you've written, Bill, (whatever works for
you is OK) but I would make the following observations:

1. Pic x switches are more efficient on IBM Mainframes. (It generates a
single CLI  (Compare Logical Immediate, where the literal being compared is
a single byte contained in the instruction) instruction to check for 1 or 0.
If you define the switch as numeric, you may end up having a CVB (convert to
binary) followed by a PACK, followed by a CP (compare packed). All in all, a
bit of an overkill for a switch test...)

2. I'm not sure about your practise of using the values of a switch to be a
subscript into a table of errors....I guess as long as the switch is not a
simple ON/OFF (Boolean) then it's OK. It raises the question: "When is a
switch, not a switch...?"

I definitely don't like the idea of zero being the "default"...for me, zero
represents logically FALSE, so it would always be NO. (You can imagine the
trouble I had when I  first learned C and found that NEGATIVE returns
represent this...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385D00F1.DA81A16B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385C5DC3.3396AE6C@worldnet.att.net> <83isvj$bbp$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
(snippage)
> Hmmmm....I take no issue with what you've written, Bill, (whatever works for
> you is OK) but I would make the following observations:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> bit of an overkill for a switch test...)

A CVB in this case makes no sense. It converts packed data (in memory -
a doubleword) to binary (in a register). Worst case would be a PACK
followed by a CP.

> 2. I'm not sure about your practise of using the values of a switch to be a
> subscript into a table of errors....I guess as long as the switch is not a
> simple ON/OFF (Boolean) then it's OK. It raises the question: "When is a
> switch, not a switch...?"

When it may have more than two states. I agree it's not really a switch
in that case, but an error flag (for want of a better term). Maybe I
should have made that clearer.

> I definitely don't like the idea of zero being the "default"...for me, zero
> represents logically FALSE, so it would always be NO. (You can imagine the
> trouble I had when I  first learned C and found that NEGATIVE returns
> represent this...)

Maybe it's my IBM mainframe background, i.e., RC=0 means no errors.

Bill Lynch
```

```
Bill Lynch wrote in message <385D00F1.DA81A16B@worldnet.att.net>...
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>>
>(snippage)
>> Hmmmm....I take no issue with what you've written, Bill, (whatever works
for
>> you is OK) but I would make the following observations:
>>
>> 1. Pic x switches are more efficient on IBM Mainframes. (It generates a
>> single CLI  (Compare Logical Immediate, where the literal being compared
is
>> a single byte contained in the instruction) instruction to check for 1 or
0.
>> If you define the switch as numeric, you may end up having a CVB (convert
to
>> binary) followed by a PACK, followed by a CP (compare packed). All in
all, a
>> bit of an overkill for a switch test...)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry Bill, you are absolutely correct... Please excuse my rambling. It's 30
years since I wrote mainframe assembler and I mistakenly thought that it was
necessary to issue a CVB before issuing PACK. Now you've documented what it
does, those dormant brain cells have revived and I recall how it works (You
issue the CVB AFTER you've PACKED the data, or not...)

The real point is that a PACK (which is a fairly hefty instruction; it was
originally implemented as a macro...) and a CP  is more overhead than a CLI.
That's why we always made our flags PIC X. I realise that these days it
really doesn't matter too much.

Now I'll just go and find Nursie and get my poultice changed....

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83ivs7$bfj$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385ce37b.398426302@news1.attglobal.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <385ce37b.398426302@news1.attglobal.net>...
>On Sun, 19 Dec 1999 11:28:41 +1300, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
><dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>until the next standard) - Initialize to make them false.

It may not matter to you Thane, but there are many programmers around the
World to whom it DOES matter. Some non-English-first-language speakers are
actually offended when they see YES and NO being used in listings that they
have been passed from parent companies and are required to maintain. (Mind
you, that could just be peevishness at having to maintain code that was "not
invented here"...Nevertheless, I have seen this manifested on different
occasions.)

I agree that the actual values used are irrelevant when SET is employed, but
I would argue the validity of using INITIALIZE to set Falsity. The initial
value of a flag should be indeterminate. If you want it to be false, SET it
so and do it explicity so there is no doubt about it. TRUE and FALSE have
equal logical weight. It should be easily apparent from code what state is
being set and where.

Assuming the initial state of a flag to be FALSE is a recipe for disaster,
especially in on-line programs where code may be re-used. (Even COBOL code
can be "serially re-usable" (pseudo re-entrant) and the initial state of a
flag could have been long lost. Think about the case where flags are defined
as EXTERNAL...a number of modules could be inheriting the flag; it's initial
state is meaningless.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> I agree that the actual values used are irrelevant when SET is employed, but
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> being set and where.

Falsity - I like that! I don't see the difference between having ZERO =
False and saying:

SET  SWITCH-IS-OFF  TO  TRUE

     or

INITIALIZE  SWITCHES (where the byte for SWITCH-OFF in a field in
SWITCHES) 

Same difference to me, except you have to code the SET for every switch.

> Assuming the initial state of a flag to be FALSE is a recipe for disaster,
> especially in on-line programs where code may be re-used. (Even COBOL code
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> state is meaningless.

I really don't see your point here. The switches aren't set to anything
until I code the INITIALIZE in the initialization routine. It makes no
matter if the program is being used concurrently or not, every
invocation of the program gets its own WORKING-STORAGE, so no one in
task B can ever trip over any value in task A. I do trust myself (and
you guys, too (includes women)) to get the usage straight within the
task, even if some are EXTERNAL, which I do use in a couple of places.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83j1a2$g8o$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385ce37b.398426302@news1.attglobal.net> <83ivs7$bfj$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385D0331.16263ED0@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:385D0331.16263ED0@worldnet.att.net...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> >
> (snip)
> >
> > I agree that the actual values used are irrelevant when SET is employed,
but
> > I would argue the validity of using INITIALIZE to set Falsity. The
initial
> > value of a flag should be indeterminate. If you want it to be false, SET
it
> > so and do it explicity so there is no doubt about it. TRUE and FALSE have
> > equal logical weight. It should be easily apparent from code what state
is
> > being set and where.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Assuming the initial state of a flag to be FALSE is a recipe for
disaster,
> > especially in on-line programs where code may be re-used. (Even COBOL
code
> > can be "serially re-usable" (pseudo re-entrant) and the initial state of
a
> > flag could have been long lost. Think about the case where flags are
defined
> > as EXTERNAL...a number of modules could be inheriting the flag; it's
initial
> > state is meaningless.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

Bill,
  I was following you OK until you said that you do use EXTERNAL in some
programs.  In such cases, you are NOT getting your own "copy" of the switches
in each program - but a shared copy.  Now, it is still true that you can
reset it in the "housekeeping" routine of each program, but if this is the
case, it doesn't make any sense to use EXTERNAL.  On the other hand, if you
do truly share it across programs (where EXTERNAL is useful), then it is true
that you can never tell what the initial (to the run-unit) setting will be.
This can be avoided by having a "housekeeping" routine for the whole
run-unit - but that only works if there is some program you can be certain
gets executed before all the others in your run-unit.  That is one possible
system design, but I have also seen systems where it really isn't possible to
predict "where you start from".
```

```
I understand that the next standard caters for this.  Perfectly clear
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b%q74.95$i05.5127@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385ce37b.398426302@news1.attglobal.net> <83ivs7$bfj$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz> <385D0331.16263ED0@worldnet.att.net> <385E1E3A.30B63D15@zip.com.au>`

```
It just occurred to me that you can intialize switches to anything if you
are careful about your WS:

01  SWITCH-BANK-1
    05  SB-1               PIC 9 OCCURS 10.
      88 SB-1-FALSE   VALUE  0.
      88 SB-1-TRUE     VALUE  1.

   INITIALIZE SWITCH-BANK-1
    REPLACING NUMERIC DATA  BY 0     << Pick one
    REPLACING NUMERIC DATA  BY 1    <<
```
