###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested IFs, is there an alternative?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JxW64.3354$_f.26567@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<385236D5.16CF7442@shinbiro.com> <385510B4.9AC96F41@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <839hmk$9vq@news1.snet.net> <83eo380vts@enews2.newsguy.com> <385B5592.64801735@worldnet.att.net> <83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote in message
<83h5ei$8c5$1@hermes.enternet.co.nz>...
>
> Michael ... asked for
>"YES" to represent the GOOD and "NOPE" to represent the BAD...What
>represents the UGLY, Mike?

Mirror, mirror, on the wall...... (1)

>Pete's rules for nested IFs:
>
>3. ONLY nest your IFs into the TRUE branches of your decision tree.

Now that is an *excellent* rule: nest the code only if the decision is
nested rather than evaluative (is 'evaluative' a real word?); and  if the
decision seems nested, see if a compound IF can convert it to an evaluative
test.

One thing about that though: pre-ANSI '85 (well,
pre-explicit-scope-terminator) COBOL has left us an astonishing quantity of
"nested evaluation" (I don't like to mix metaphors like that, but if the
shoe fits, you should not throw stones) code to maintain.

However, with COBOL code's unexpected longevity, methinks we should all be
prepared to see this kind of code for a long, long, time.

MCM

1. Or is it OFF the wall?
```

- **From:** jlw@psulias.psu.edu (j.lance wilkinson, (814) 865-1818)
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Grace Murray Hopper

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385A9C99.82CEF268@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<38590215.84B10F80@CapGemini.Co.UK> <19991216152743.05252.00001326@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
The article referred to makes at least one false claim, that GH
invented the term 'bug', which in fact goes back to Edison in the
C19.

It also claims she invented COBOL, without providing any real
evidence. 

I just get the feeling she is one of those people everyone agrees
was fantastic, but noone is quite sure why. I know we are always
on the lookout for feminist heroes, but what is the truth?

This reminds me of the efforts to portray Lady Lovelace as an
equal of Charles Babbage in the invention of general purpose
calculation engines. Lovelace does have one clearly documented
achievement, being the first programmer to write a program with a
bug in it. See the recent article in Scientific American.

Tim Josling


RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
> Simon Elliott, was kind enough to post a reference to
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Grace Murray Hopper

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12c449b07b38284498968d@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<38590215.84B10F80@CapGemini.Co.UK> <19991216152743.05252.00001326@ng-fa1.aol.com> <385A9C99.82CEF268@melbpc.org.au>`

```
In article <385A9C99.82CEF268@melbpc.org.au>, tej@melbpc.org.au says...
> The article referred to makes at least one false claim, that GH
> invented the term 'bug', which in fact goes back to Edison in the
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
   I think it is too late to correct history books.  What has been 
printed can not be easily retracted.  We can only hope that the true 
facts will not be totally lost.  Only time will tell.

   The other night I watched a television program on our local PBS 
station regarding the allied code-breakers of WWII.  Much of the detailed 
information about these units had remained classified until recently.  An 
important conclusion was that they developed and implemented the first-
ever programmable computer to assist them with their work.  Shortly after 
the war, they destroyed these classified machines and all of the design 
documents.  The television program included interviews with living people 
that were involved with these machines, so I tend to believe them.  
   Does this reduce the significance of GMH's contributions?  No, because 
she was one of the instrumental players in getting the idea of higher-
level languages off the ground.

   When I attended one of her talks, I enjoyed her story regarding 
speaking to a congressional committee about project funding.  After 
selling them on the proposed computer project, they asked how much she 
wanted.  When they realized she wanted $30K and not $30M, the congressmen 
had to laugh! (I guess you had to be there, he he)

Pete
```

wrote:

>It also claims she invented COBOL, without providing any real
>evidence. 
>

COBOL was based in large part on Flow-Matic - which GMH did invent.
She participated in an advisory manner to the original CODASYL.  

I think if you read the section in my book, Sams Teach Yourself COBOL
in 24 Hours you will find an accurate accounting of Graces'
contribution to COBOL.  

If you can Tim, just browse the book in the bookstore or your local
library - My information was gathered from first hand interviews of
contemporaries of hers, and people directly involved with the creation
of the original standard.
---

This seems like a spurious distinction to me. I don't see a
separate term 'computer bug'. Bugs in programs and systems are
just like bugs in any other gizmo.

"There is a bug in this program" "I am debugging" "Why do
Microsoft programs have so many bugs".

Seriously, from accounts I have read of the incident, GH did not
claim to in vent the term. The whole thing seemed to be a bu^Dig
joke - a bug that was actually a 'bug'.

No need to shout, by the way, even when dealing with people who
are so obviously less intelligent than yourself.

Tim Josling

<a id="t-b23e1fef4a"></a>
## Thread: Performance enhancement by compiling? — 25 msgs

### Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We have some programs (lots of I-O's) which run half an hour and more.

Now that I read the MF-Books about compiling and linking again, I
recognised that linked programs should run faster than GNT-format
programs. Normaly our programs run in gnt-format.

So I compiled one of those programs to gnt and after I linked the
program to a 'standalone' executable.

I used:
cob -uO progname to get the gnt program
cob -Ox progname to get the linked program

My coboptions are set to:
-C ERRLIST
-C FORM=72
-C OPTIONAL-FILE
-C ANS85=SYNTAX
-C NOALTER
-C COMP
-C WARNING=3
-C NOBOUND
-C BOUNDOPT

I use MF Cobol 4.1 on RM 200 (Siemens) running Reliant Unix 5.43 C4001.

After running both programs (one after one) as the only programs running
on those machine, I saw that the linked programs was a bit (30 secs on 8
minutes runtime at all) SLOWER.

I already replaced most compute statements thru add/subtract/divide
statements....

So my question:
How can I improve speed on programs with lots of I-O (please no hardware
tips)?

Many thanks for all hints.

Stefan Meyer, Triumph International


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<838rg1$8n8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Stefan.Meyer@triumph-international.de wrote:
: We have some programs (lots of I-O's) which run half an hour and more.

: Now that I read the MF-Books about compiling and linking again, I
: recognised that linked programs should run faster than GNT-format
: programs. Normaly our programs run in gnt-format.

: After running both programs (one after one) as the only programs running
: on those machine, I saw that the linked programs was a bit (30 secs on 8
: minutes runtime at all) SLOWER.

: I already replaced most compute statements thru add/subtract/divide
: statements....

Changing the amount of CPU usage does not affect how fast the
disk drives operate (in general terms).  Your Unix system
should be able to tell you how much CPU time has been used
during the run.  This seems to be an insignificant part of the
run time.

You need to look first at optimising your I-O statements.  This
may require changing, for example, the ISAM Key structure, or
replacing SORTs with another alternate key.

Where there is a large file that may need to be read through to
see if processing is required on 'active' records, it may be
an advantage to create another file of 'required actions'.
That is if a flag is set in the master file (in some way) to
indicate that processing is required then have the programs
that set this flag write it to a small 'flag file' instead.
Then it is only necessary to read through the flag file,
deleting records as the processing is done, rather than
reading though the whole master file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cZV54.169$A24.3842@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Of course there's the rule:

The fastest I-O is NO I-O.

Look real, real hard at what I-O operations that might be
removed - for example, if you've gotten a record once,
do you really need to get it again? Do you really need
a record or can you guess at what it might contain?
Instead of getting a record, would anyone care (or
notice) if you just used random numbers?

Anyway, once you find the trick that makes the biggest
difference in your application, please share it with the
group.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3858577A.9A2B7784@home.com>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cZV54.169$A24.3842@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```


Jerry P wrote:
> 
> Of course there's the rule:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> group.

One of the 'tricks' I use to avoid repetitious reads, and a
ws-MyDescrips is used for each file in the routine; sorted records it's
a cinch, but most often used with random :-

01 ws-MyDescrips.
   05 ws-key	pic 9/x
   05 ws-name	pic x(xx)

	initialize ws-MyDescrips
	do some processing
        if     my-key = ws-key
	       move ws-name to .... and continue

	else   move my-key to ws-key
	       read myFile key is my-key
	         invalid key move "^%!@#$$!" to ws-name
			  move ws-name to ......and continue
	         not invalid key
                          move myrec-name to ws-name
	       End-read
	End-if

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83a7j8$gtg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cZV54.169$A24.3842@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
In article <cZV54.169$A24.3842@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>,
  "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
> Of course there's the rule:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> notice) if you just used random numbers?

Yes, I think that someone would care.

As far as I know there are not so many double readings. When I debugged
the code and saw that a record has been read twice or even more, I tried
to avoid this situation (seems to be almost working).

But in some circumstances we really have to read twice.
For example:
When we try to create receipts for delivery from outstanding orders, we
have to check in which order our clients have to be delivered (Depends
on many facts, result is a big sort-file).
Further to see if an order can be delivered, we have to check:
1. How many items can't be delivered
2. The overall percentage must be greater than a client specific
delivery ability.

The first parse is a simulation if everything will be ok. And the second
parse does it.

Unfortunately, we are using C-ISAM files and not a database. So we can't
do a rollback. And of course we don't use FileShare because speed will
be up to 5 (or even more) times slower.

Question: Would reducing the length of a sort-key improve sorting?

In fact the original question was: Is it possible to increase speed by
simply choosing an optimized compile or link run???

> Anyway, once you find the trick that makes the biggest
> difference in your application, please share it with the
> group.

I do my best.

Stefan Meyer




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VMe64.1383$q54.12613@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cZV54.169$A24.3842@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <83a7j8$gtg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> > Of course there's the rule:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> When we try to create receipts for delivery from outstanding orders,
we
> have to check in which order our clients have to be delivered
(Depends
> on many facts, result is a big sort-file).

> Question: Would reducing the length of a sort-key improve sorting?
>

Which brings us to rule #2: Never sort the master file.

In your question, the length of the key - while of some import - may
not be as time consuming to sort as moving a humongous data
record attached to the field. Have you thought about the following:

Instead of:
1. SORT lots of
   SORT-RECORDS = SORT-KEY + (really big (or part of really big) data
record)
2. Process the returned SORT-RECORDS

Try:
1. SORT lots of:
   SORT-RECORD = SORT-KEY + pointer to record in data file
2. a. Return a SORT-RECORD
    b. Use record pointer in SORT-RECORD to get data
    c. Process data

For example, suppose you have 100k ISAM records whose file key is
a customer number (say 10 bytes). Each of these records is 500 bytes.
You want to sort by ZIP code. So, then, to sort the whole file you
have
SORT-RECORD
   SORT-ZIP        X(5)
   SORT-CUST   X(10).
which works out to 15 x 100k = 15 Meg sort file instead of the
500 x 100k = 50 Meg sort file you get by sorting the whole record.

Remember, it's worse than it looks. Internally, the sort routine
may have to move a record in-and-out dozens of times. The bigger
the sort file or the bigger the sort record, the more moves to
temporary storage.

I apologize for being somewhat flippant in my earlier response
(the part about making up the results), but it was possible you
worked for the government; I wasn't sure. Sorry.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83d3d6$kof$1@news1.cableinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cZV54.169$A24.3842@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <83a7j8$gtg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It may be a silly question but when was the last time you did a reorg of
these files by using REBUILD to reduce fragmentation?

Have you considered using compression to reduce I/O? Your CPU utilisation 
WILL
increase, but elaped time should reduce if you successfully reduce file 
size. There
is much you can do with different options in Micro Focus's External File 
Handler. 
DO NOT waste too much time looking at reducing CPU as it rarely pays off. 
You 
sometimes get savings of up to 3-4% after complete re-writes focusing on 
CPU issues,
but can normally get savings of at least 50% just looking at how data is 
retrieved. After
working for 16 years as a performance analyst, I have found that far too 
much time is
wasted on peripheral issues ... The first question I ask is: "when was the 
last database
re-org?" In 80% of cases the answer is: "Huh?"

Michael Burgun
Director
Centrelink Systems Ltd
Bristol, UK
Technical@centrelink-systems.notthis.com

>> In article <cZV54.169$A24.3842@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>,
>>   "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>> the code and saw that a record has been read twice or even more, I 
tried
>> to avoid this situation (seems to be almost working).
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> The first parse is a simulation if everything will be ok. And the 
second
>> parse does it.
>>
>> Unfortunately, we are using C-ISAM files and not a database. So we 
can't
>> do a rollback. And of course we don't use FileShare because speed will
>> be up to 5 (or even more) times slower.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Before you buy.
```

our countries.

#### ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4QO54.234$CW3.6417@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de wrote in message
<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>We have some programs (lots of I-O's) which run half an hour and more.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


First of all, 'hardware tips' may be what you need, as I-O is a function of
hardware; so don't rule these out 'out-of-hand'.

Other than 'reducing the number of physical I-Os' I can think of no other
way to noticeably improve the performace of an I-O bound process. The famous
American bank robber, Willie Sutton, was once asked by a reporter, "Willie,
why do you rob banks?"  Willie gave a good answer: "Because that's where
they keep the money."

You have the same situation: to steal processing time, you have to look
where the most processing time is located.

If you are processing sequentially, use blocking or caching; if the
operating system can't or won't handle that for you, you'll need to write
your own caching/blocking mechanism.  Actually re-engineering a system to
reduce I-Os is not a trivial exercise, and you may want to look at an
outside consultant.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00

- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <4QO54.234$CW3.6417@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <838i97$97j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

Stefan.Meyer@triumph-international.de wrote:

: There's no way to reduce the I-O processes a lot (already done).

Correction: There is no way that you can think of to ...

If, for example, you are using ISAM files then the index file
will be using a lot of I-O.  If this could be made to a 
hashed random file then it may reduce the I-O significantly.
(there may however be disadvantages in other areas).

Have you tried making the file access exclusive for these runs
instead of shared ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3858D53F.976E92E8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <4QO54.234$CW3.6417@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <838i97$97j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <838rti$8n8$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <83a5m3$eoa$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de wrote:
> 
> ..and I will have a full redesign of our application. There are about
> 100 progs accessing those files. And sorry, my predecessor decided to
> code the I-O routines in each prog again (and again...). He was one of
> those 'programers' who never heard something about using libs etc. 

Method 1:

Take one program.  Pull the code outside the program.  Test and
implement.  As you modify other programs call your common module,
updating the common module as required.  Eventually you will have a
single source controlling IO.

Here is the extreme programming bit.  Write a separate program that
simply calls that IO modules interface and comprehensively tests every
function.  When you need to update the code to handle a slightly
different variations for another program you can prove this process
works as designed, implement new tests for the new functions.  Small
steps,  automated tests, test very often, as counter intuitive as it
sounds it is far faster.

Method 2:

Extract and optimize a single program even if it takes a setup cost
more than the saving.  Gradually implement the optimized solution into
other programs and the pay back happens.

Summary:

Think in terms of years not minutes.  Work to a desired goal.  Kent
beck documents that one rewrite he did took 9 years to complete. 
There was a lot of waiting opportunity and inspiration however.

Finally:

Some advice get statistics as you go.  Gradual improvement is not as
impressive as statistically proven figures showing a 20% runtime
improvement over 12 months.

You also asked:
> Question: Would reducing the length of a sort-key improve sorting?

Yes:  Indexing (should/does) really on caching it's own information. 
If you reduce the size of the key you increase the likelihood of that
key being in memory.  Also ISAM generally works on a page basis. 
These pages for VSAM MVS are 4K and possibly similar in you system. 
If you reduce the size of the key, and there data record you increase
the chance that the particular page you are after is already in
memory.

On a large file this increase in chance might be minuscule.

Off beat:  Have you considered splitting the files up.  Recording part
of the record that is regularly read will be faster because you are
loading less data.  May or may not work....  Definitely will increase
DASD usage.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3859028E.AB4F5C25@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <4QO54.234$CW3.6417@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <838i97$97j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <838rti$8n8$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <83a5m3$eoa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3858D53F.976E92E8@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Off beat:  Have you considered splitting the files up.  Recording part
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I remember sorting a large file into a half dozen tape drives, then
merging the tape files together into one.  Nowadays we don't HAVE a half
dozen tape drives, but have plenty of temporary work DASD, so we don't
need them.

Still the sort/merge concept is one which newcomers should be more aware
of.  An external sort can be very efficient, and match-merge programs
minimize I-O.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3858FFAA.6C6BD862@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <4QO54.234$CW3.6417@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <838i97$97j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38589F0C.FAF9CB07@worldnet.att.net> <83an30$r0u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
What makes a file access method efficient when paging is involved is not
necessarily the same as making a file access method efficient when the
whole thing is in memory.

I imagine that's why SEARCH ALL doesn't specify a binary search.  For a
small table, it could be more efficient to do a serial search than to
stop and calculate pointers.

Normally, you calculate I-O's for your critical tasks, I-O's for other
tasks, and then everything else later.  With everything in memory, there
are no I-O's.  

It could be that making the file smaller (by eliminating an index) can
make it fit into memory, with an advantage which is greater than the
loss of the index!!

Relative addressing often requires one I-O to read record 1 to get the
pointer to the next available record (or it also can use chaining), and
then another I-O to get to the record.  This is used when you can't lock
up the file in a multi-user environment.  But what if you can create a
virtual record 1 available for everybody (with periodic background
updating of the file)?

Many operating systems and database management systems are doing stuff
like this transparently to the user.  If it guesses correctly, our
access can be MORE efficient once it moves to production and the
environment decides that it is being used enough to keep it available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3859A329.9EEA2188@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <4QO54.234$CW3.6417@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <838i97$97j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38589F0C.FAF9CB07@worldnet.att.net> <83an30$r0u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de wrote:
> 
> In article <38589F0C.FAF9CB07@worldnet.att.net>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> how to work with the index in memory?

You don't know it's in memory, once it's there references to the index
don't cause any I/O. How you get it into memory depends on your
platform. The for instance I gave above is how you could do it in MVS.

> On the other hand I'm working on data files up to 1 GB of size...

Yes, but how big is the index? Again in MVS, there's also the
possibility of loading your file into memory (yes, the whole thing). An
MVS address space is 2 GB. If that's not enough you can acquire a Data
Space, which is a separate address space for data, only, i.e., you can't
execute code there.

Bill Lynch
```

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00

```
    The runtime needed for GNT may handle memory management slightly better
than the EXE runtime.

    Also check the cobol operations manual.  Some version of MF have options
that can increase IO speed, by
increasing the size of buffer the runtime uses.  But the best first bet is
to open exclusive.  Also try to avoid
networks if possible.

I have looked at the thread and I wonder what kind of operation we are
trying to optimize.

What do you mean with "lots of I-O's"? I am trying to se the picture of what
your programs are doing. I would like at short description like: "we read
file1 and update file2, file3, file4 and file5. File1 is sequential, file2
is indexed with 32 alternat keys.........". I think you get the idea.

I read somewhere, that you by debugging found, that you accessed the same
record more than once, but you were about to fix that problem.

Do you have a statement like this: "write something invalid key rewrite
something". Now what is the most common result of that statement? rewrite?
If so, change to: "rewrite something invalid key write something".

You have said, that files cannot be changed (files are used by many
programs), bu you can evaluate every write or rewrite in the 2 programs in
question.

I will save my breath until you give a short description of the I-O model
for the programs, then i think I might have some ideas.

Regards
Ib

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance enhancement by compiling?

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <NospamSMeyer@Triumph-International.de>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3859F57A.DBF0B11D@Triumph-International.de>`
- **References:** `<838135$s3s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83brol$2ck$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
> I have looked at the thread and I wonder what kind of operation we are
> trying to optimize.
>

The original intended question was: Is it possible to get a better performance
using the best compile/link method?
That's all. In the meantime, some people asked me, what w're doing and of course
how. So I tried to answer and gave some examples.

>
> What do you mean with "lots of I-O's"? I am trying to se the picture of what
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There are a few programs working like:

- Read the program master file (depends on the program)
- If a record should be processed, read all depended records from detail file
- Sort them (by using key-references to the original record)
- Process the sorted records and write them into a third and fourth table,
completing the new record with datas from different other tables (some records
from the master/detail files will be merged into one new master/detail
relation). By the way mark records in Master and Detail files as processed.

This is a short description of what is done. I think lots of programs are
working in that way...

All files are indexed, some of them have a few (not 32) alternate keys with
duplicates.

>
> I read somewhere, that you by debugging found, that you accessed the same
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, when updating a file, I always know, if a record has to be written or
rewritten.


>
> You have said, that files cannot be changed (files are used by many
> programs), bu you can evaluate every write or rewrite in the 2 programs in
> question.
>

Sorry for missunderstanding. I didn't say that files can't be changed.

>
> I will save my breath until you give a short description of the I-O model
> for the programs, then i think I might have some ideas.
>
>

Nice, but I really only wanted to know....see original question.

Regards,
Stefan Meyer
```

#### ↳ Re: SIGs (OT again)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jDc64.7078$o97.64977@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<5W034.371$He.2528@newsfeed.slurp.net> <82i7fr$2qiq$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <8W934.2671$Ze7.106410@news3.mia> <82kjp2$272s$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <8Ot34.2706$Fk.46252@news2.mia> <384e6b3c_1@news3.prserv.net> <l%v34.2946$Fk.48477@news2.mia> <BAZ44.1780$du4.53577@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <83333j$8b0$1@news.cerf.net> <M5v54.2791$DC6.29217@news2.mia> <Yu3i9KAmS4V4Ewwg@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
>Judson McClendon writes
>>A free society tolerates being exposed
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>ntly set free (lack of evidence, despite witnesses).

Denying them the 'right' to physically assault or molest others, no.
Denying them the right to express their abhorrent views, yes.  The basis
of law is that it protect the innocent from harm and punish those guilty
of causing harm.  Assault and murder should be, and probably is, illegal
in every culture.  Free expression is only denied in oppressive cultures,
and in cultures rapidly moving that way.  The way to combat destructive
ideas is to oppose them with truth.  What if it is the truth that is
being suppressed?  The real problem here is that laws or customs that
can be used to suppress 'bad' speech can be as easily used to suppress
'good' speech.  All that has to happen is that those who have the power
to determine 'bad' and 'good' are replaced by those with a different
agenda, or their masters replaced by those with a different agenda.

>>To avoid tyranny, a free
>>society must allow dissenting, even unpopular, opinions to be freely
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>bring about the destruction of the same society.

Only if the population is foolish enough to believe it.  How else could
a bad government be taken down, other than by internal descent or external
force?  The way bad governments fall is through knowledgeable descent of
the oppressed.  The way good governments stand is by the knowledgeable
consent of the governed.  In both cases knowledge is the key.  How can
citizens become knowledgeable, if they only hear part of the story?  Who
is the 'guardian' to decide what they are to hear?  What if they become
corrupt?  Who do you trust to control what you are permitted hear, and
what you are permitted to say?  Whith free expression, Citizens can listen
and believe or reject any opinion they choose.  This is the right of free
people.  If they blindly follow fools, there is no way to justly govern a
population who has abandoned wisdom for folly.  They bring about their own
destruction.  Free speech, and its subset, free press, are essential to a
free society.  This principle is not an opinion or a guess, it is hard,
cold fact, known for thousands of years.  It is used and abused every day
to defend against, and to support, oppression.  Any conquering army, any
new despot, and the even dumbest of dictators, moves immediately to
control all forms of communication.  Why?  Because, if they do not, they
will not survive for long, and they know it.  There is not one tyrannical
government on the planet, nor has there ever been or ever will be one,
whose citizens enjoy completely free speech.  Why?  Just trot over to
North Korea or China and start talking about how bad the government is.
You will soon learn that those governments understand this principle quite
well. ;-)  In any such government you care to name, people are imprisioned
or killed simply for speaking against the government.  Why?  It is very
simple.  If all people are free to express themselves, then public descent
will eventually bring down any tyrannical government.  If people cannot
speak freely, then it is difficult or impossible for descent to be
articulated, coordinated or spread.  Individuals may hate the government,
but they are virtually powerless to do anything about it.  They are afraid
to even tell their friends, because they might be turned in for violating
the law.  There is no way to expose false propaganda, because the people
who know can't tell anyone about it.  If free expression is abridged, even
a little, the precedent is set in the people's minds to provide a vehicle
for any would be tyrant to use for this purpose.  The same applies, even
in a free society.  When a group's free speech is suppressed, those people
have lost their voice, their ability to influence society, or even express
how they feel or what their problems are.  How or when would discrimination
against minorities ever be addressed, if they are denied a voice?

>>  This is the very foundation stone upon which freedom rests.
>>Otherwise, many more opinions, and eventually truth itself, will be
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>shore up the regime as the prime triggers for the soft revolution.

You are looking at the effects, not the underlying cause.  Why do you
think they were moved to withdraw the troops, or any of the other moves
in that direction?  The Soviets were hard, cold realists.  They never
did anything unless they were convinced it was to their advantage.
Joseph Stalin had millions of Soviet citizens murdered and starved
because he found them inconvenient.  One of the reasons they suffered
many early defeats in WWII was because Stalin had recently murdered
most of the commanders in the army, devastating their whole command
structure and morale.  Stalin left quite a lagacy that his successors
did not fail to notice.  Not since Kruchev was one man in the Soviet
Union ever permitted to wield such arbitrary power.  Gorbachev would
never have been permitted such sweeping reforms unless those who
supported him were also convinced.  What convinced them?  Did they,
after almost a century of lies, simply decide to 'be nice'? :-)

>> and the Soviet Union.
>
>I thought the USSR was brought down by Gorbachev refusing to continue to
>lie about productivity and how great life was in  a socialist utopia.

Thanks, you support my point.  Why was lying effective before?  Why was
it no longer effective?  Was it because human nature had changed?  Or
was it because information, and thereby influence, from the West had
shown the people how totally false those claims were?  Why do you think
Gorbachev, and especially the many others who supported him, knew that
the lying was not useless?  It was because those long oppressed people
now *knew*.  Had their free speech not been suppressed, they would have
known all along.  Which is why it was suppressed in the first place, of
course. :-)
```

<a id="t-8dfe28632d"></a>
## Thread: data file extensions differ on Unix and DOS — 4 msgs

### data file extensions differ on Unix and DOS

- **From:** elizrj@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83903a$k4r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello !

I am currently using MF Cobol on my SCO Unix 5, and for development on
my PCs.  I'd like to set up a program to run on the Win95 boxes that
accesses the data on the Unix.  However, when I run a program on the
Unix, it creates different file extensions than on my PC.

The same select statement

Select NEWFILE assign "NEWFILE.DAT"

will create two files on the Unix as
   NEWFILE.DAT
   NEWFILE.DAT.idx

However, on the DOS box, it will create
   NEWFILE.DAT
   NEWFILE.IDX

Is this a problem with Cobol or with the way our Unix Guru set up the
Unix box?   Is there a quick and easy fix for me?

I'd appreciate your input.  Thanks in advance!
Elizabeth Johnson


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: data file extensions differ on Unix and DOS

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83a4pt$o1c$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<83903a$k4r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
    Remove the ".DAT" from all source code.  Sorry.
    This will produce:
NEWFILE               (DATA)
NEWFILE.IDX        (INDEX on DOS)
NEWFILE.idx          (Index on unix)


<elizrj@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:83903a$k4r$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hello !
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83aupl$m5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data file extensions differ on Unix and DOS

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38593285.1241701837@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<83903a$k4r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83a4pt$o1c$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <83aupl$m5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
E. Johnson <elizrj@my-deja.com> schrieb:

>> NEWFILE               (DATA)
>> NEWFILE.IDX        (INDEX on DOS)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
><grumbles and goes off to change a gazillion lines of code>

If you don't create new files, you can ask your admin to link
*.DAT.idx to .IDX.

Using Linux as our host machine you can tell SAMBA to translate file
extensions.

And last but not least you could use FileShare to do your file I/O.

Best regards
```

>I thought that perhaps a few people in the NG might be amused by a spec that
>I just received.  It calls for a two computer network.  On boot, the server
>backs up to the station.  The station, on boot, backs up to the server.  I
>wonder if the nation-wide accounting firm that designed it has thought
>through the sequence of starting *both* computers.

This would be comparable to one of the earliest vehicle traffic
regulations I remember reading about.  It went something like:
  "When two vehicles approach an intersection at the same time,
   each shall come to a full stop, and neither shall proceed 
   until the other has gone."

On second thought, I guess they invented shared file and database
deadlocks ahead of their time.

#### ↳ Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839a5e$sjh$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<pRV54.14584$Mg.233090@c01read03-admin.service.talkway.com>`

```
The draft of the next Standard (and a number of existing vendors as an
extension) already support what is called (in COBOL) "in-line comments". For
example,

   Move A to B *> B is that field
                     C *> C is that other field

All COBOLs already support "multi-line" comments, just put an asterisk into
column 7.  There USED to be another way by calling something a "NOTE" - but I
think that was dropped in either the '68 or '74 Standard.  (I know it was
gone by '74).  If you use the asterisk in column 7 approach, that should work
in all current conforming compilers.

FYI,
  In the next Standard, there will be both fixed-form and free-form reference
format.  For fixed-form reference format, the asterisk in column 7 approach
will continue to work.  For free-form reference format, you just start each
line with the "in-line comment indicator", e.g.

   *> This is the first line of comments
   *>  and this is a second line.
```

they want to use columns beyond 72 (or 80) - but they LOVE being able to use
columns 1-7 for source stuff (and not for un-used sequence numbers).

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Free-form is very common among several existing sets of users.  It isn't that
> they want to use columns beyond 72 (or 80) - but they LOVE being able to use
> columns 1-7 for source stuff (and not for un-used sequence numbers).

We use columns 72-80 for change control comments.  It is a pain, but at
least it leaves columns 1-6 for what we want.  So when I am making
temporary lines (for debugging), I put HJBDEB (HJB are my initials, DEB
is short for DEBUG) in columns 1-6 on lines which I need to clean up
later.  But comments have to go on separate lines, even if I am simply
describing a field or a test.

IF CAMPUS-CODE = '1'    *> Boulder
   ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83be22$7e0$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<pRV54.14584$Mg.233090@c01read03-admin.service.talkway.com> <839a5e$sjh$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <q3di5s0a29j3cjo4d7li99qqod07g43scv@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke <vbandke@ibm.net> wrote in message
 <snip>
> >
> I would love tobe able to put comments onto the same line as, e.g., a data
division entry.
> I would not care that much for procedure division statements, as, if it
needs commenting,
> I put a comment box right before the part to be explained
>
> One thought - why the cumbersome *> combination?  What's the reason for not
taking a
> semicolon (;) - I haven't checked it, but I can't remember a defined usage
of the ; in
> COBOL (But could be mistaken, of course)
>

UNFORTUNATELY, semi-colon is in the current Standard as (virtually) as a
no-op, i.e. it (a semi-colon followed by a space) has the same meaning as a
comma followed by a space (which is that it means NOTHING more than a single
space).  Although the draft does add the "_" (underscore) character for the
use in COBOL words and the "&" (ampersand) as a concatenation operator, there
was a "goal" not to add new characters to the COBOL character set (now called
the "COBOL Character Repertoire").  For those who went thru changes to
COPY/REPLACING between '74 and '85 for the colon, adding new "special"
characters really can have unforeseen and UNDESIRABLE side-effects - which is
why they (we?) ended up using a combination of characters for the inline
comment indicator.

I hope this helps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38593EAB.4AADB66E@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<pRV54.14584$Mg.233090@c01read03-admin.service.talkway.com> <839a5e$sjh$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <q3di5s0a29j3cjo4d7li99qqod07g43scv@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 

> One thought - why the cumbersome *> combination?  What's the reason for not taking a
> semicolon (;) - I haven't checked it, but I can't remember a defined usage of the ; in
> COBOL (But could be mistaken, of course)
> 

Lots of programs have semicolons in them.  They are punctuation similar
to commas, but less likely to be perceived as periods.  They don't do
anything, but can indicate that there's more to follow.

e.g.
MOVE X   TO FIELD1; 
            FIELD2;
            FIELD3.
```

## Thread: COBOL Anniversary Event — 3 msgs

### COBOL Anniversary Event

- **From:** Bill Becorest <bbecores@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38582794.A079846D@acucorp.com>`

```
Hello,

My name is Bill Becorest and I am the director of marketing at Acucorp,
Inc.  I wanted to let everyone know that there is an open invitation to
the 40th Year Anniversary of COBOL Celebration in San Diego on January
21, 2000.  I have included some relevant information below:

Acucorp, Inc., Computerworld Magazine, and the newly formed COBOL
Alliance are hosting this milestone celebration.  Two events are
included on January 21:  Software Technology Summit 2000; and the Grace
Murray Hopper Commemorative Ceremony (alongside the USS Hopper on Navy
Pier).  This is an industry-wide gathering that will include military
officials, government representatives, Fortune 1000 companies,
technologists, CIOs, CEOs, educators, and students.  Alll organizations
and individuals with a vested interested in COBOL are welcome to attend.

The Software Technology Summit will be hosted by CNN's Ann Kellan.  The
Commemorative Ceremony will honor the achievements of Grace Hopper, and
include shipboard tours of the USS Hopper, a multi-mission
AEGIS guided missile destroyer.

There is no cost to attend (aside from personal transportation and
lodging); however RSVPs are required.  To RSVP, call 1-800-262-6585.
You can also RSVP and get more information online at
www.cobol40years.com.  Please feel free to contact me directly for more
information.

Thank you.

Bill Becorest
(858) 689-4430
Director of Marketing
Acucorp, Inc.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839mbk$j6c$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

As I regularly review comp.lang.cobol, I will be happy to forward (or not -

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Anniversary Event

- **From:** Admin Kit User <abecore1@san.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385884F4.F8CF8158@san.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<38582794.A079846D@acucorp.com> <839mbk$j6c$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks Bill,

It's true the initial thinking was to be somewhat selective with the invitations
in order to get the "cream of the crop," so to speak, and to maintain our budget
forecasts.  But from a promotional standpoint, it just didn't seem to make much
sense.  Our aim, afterall, is to gain as much exposure for the language as we
possibly can.  As a result, we have opened the 40th Anniversary event up to all
interested organizations and individuals, with the only requirement that they
RSVP.  So far, the open invitation has done nothing to damage the "prestige" of
the event, and I have to say I much prefer this approach.  Please feel free to
forward any of my comments, as well as the invitation.  Thanks.

Bill Becorest
Acucorp, Inc.

## Thread: LE370 Callable Services - COBOL II — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### LE370 Callable Services - COBOL II

- **From:** Bill Tredinnick <73043.162@CompuServe.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839ca9$708$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
In "COBOL for OS/390 Power Programmimg", David Shelby Kirk says
that LE370 callable services are available to a COBOL II program
without resorting to a COBOL370 recompile. We have the LE370
environment, but I'm having a tough time making the LE370 
callable services available to my COBOL II program?
 

```

#### ↳ Re: LE370 Callable Services - COBOL II

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839n6n$kuu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<839ca9$708$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Easy (but a tad confusing) answer to your question.

1) a *limited* number of LE callable services are available to be called by a
VS COBOL II program.  The ones that are allowed are ONLY the date/time ones.

2) In order to use those few callable services, you *MUST* call them
dynamically.  (This can be done either by CALL identifier format of the CALL
statement *or* by compiling with the DYNAM compiler option).  *HOWEVER*, in
order to make even a single dynamic CALL in a VS COBOL II program, you MUST
use the RES compiler option.  If even one of the programs in your application
is already (or will be) compiled NORES, then this will force your entire
application into "MIXRES" mode - which introduce a WHOLE huge set of "new"
rules when using the LE run-time library.

3) In case it wasn't clear,  your VS COBOL II compiled programs *must* be
link-edited with *and* be run with the LE run-time library and not the VS
COBOL II one (and again, this means that all your existing NORES VS COBOL II
programs will need to be re-linkedit).

   ****

All of this is documented in an IBM APAR.  I don't have the APAR number
handy, but your COBOL "support person" should be able to find it by checking
IBMLINK for "VS COBOL II" and "LE dynamic calls".  (Let me know if you/he/she
has any trouble finding the APAR and I will get the number for you).

I hope this helps - even if it may not be what you really wanted to hear.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: LE370 Callable Services - COBOL II

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991216065321.25986.00001212@ng-cf1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<839n6n$kuu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill Klein writes...

>Easy (but a tad confusing) answer to your question.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>use the RES compiler option.  

Wait, Bill, this is not true. You only use RES to make dynamic calls to COBOL
routines, not LE routines. DYNAM will make your calls to the LE services
dynamic. You are right that only the date / time services are officially
supported (although I've found you can call some of the other routines, but I
wouldn't count on any non-officially-supported routine being callable).


>If even one of the programs in your application
>is already (or will be) compiled NORES, then this will force your entire
>application into "MIXRES" mode - which introduce a WHOLE huge set of "new"
>rules when using the LE run-time library.

Well, this is true, but not dependent on dynamic calls of LE services.

>3) In case it wasn't clear,  your VS COBOL II compiled programs *must* be
>link-edited with *and* be run with the LE run-time library and not the VS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   ****

Again, yes, NORES cautions once again should be raised, but not due to LE
services issues.



>
>All of this is documented in an IBM APAR.  I don't have the APAR number
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe you can find this in the pubs: perhaps LE Programming Guide, perhaps
the COBOL Migration Guide (don't have easy access to my manuals right now).




>I hope this helps - even if it may not be what you really wanted to hear.
>

I'm sure it helped.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: LE370 Callable Services - COBOL II

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83ar4s$fmt$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<839n6n$kuu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <19991216065321.25986.00001212@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Steve,
  I think you are INCORRECT.  If the compiler itself makes a call to an
LE-service (as it does with IBM COBOL for this-or-that), then you don't need
to use RES (of course it isn't even an option with that compiler).  HOWEVER,
if you have a dynamic call to ANY program explicitly coded in your program,
then RES is forced on at compile time.  The compiler does NOT pay any
attention to what the name is of the called program (and as it is dynamic,
theoretically, it could change from execution to execution).

Again, using an explicit dynamic CALL to an LE-service *will* force on RES in
a VS COBOL II program.
```

#### ↳ Re: LE370 Callable Services - COBOL II

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839rej$88e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<839ca9$708$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
In article <839ca9$708$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>,
  Bill Tredinnick <73043.162@CompuServe.COM> wrote:
> In "COBOL for OS/390 Power Programmimg", David Shelby Kirk says
> that LE370 callable services are available to a COBOL II program
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

Exactly what is the error ? Are you attempting to call these routines
from Batch or CICS ? In CICS, use a CALL Data-Name, because CICS
accesses them via the CICS "Dynamic" CALL convention. The target
routine should have a PPT entry. Check them via CEMT. Static-Calls from
CICS won't work.

Have you verified the parameter-list requirements for each routine ?

Site: http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/Shelves/CEE1BK10

is the page for LE under OS/390 2.8. But from what I remember, the
parameter-list requirements haven't changed since the inception of
these routines.

Refer to books CEEA2010 and CEEA3010 at this site.

HTH....

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-b3d827af20"></a>
## Thread: The cost of COBOL compilers (humor) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### The cost of COBOL compilers (humor)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839obj$tci$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up to the most current thread (in comp.lang.cobol) on the cost of
one specific (and several in general) COBOL compilers, I thought that I would
pass the following along.

Just today I was talking to a friend of mine (former co-worker at two of the
COBOL vendors for whom I have worked),  we suddenly came up with the
following "amusing" answer to those who complain about the price of most
COBOL compiler when compared to the majority of "commercial" C (or C++ - or
even Java) compilers.

What you REALLY need to do is to divide the total cost of the compiler (even
the compiler plus run-time) for each language by the number of reserved words
(or even "statement types") supported by each compiler.  Once you do this,
you will see that your COBOL compiler is really a BARGAIN - on a per reserved
word basis. <BG>
```

---

<a id="t-2079a39267"></a>
## Thread: Explicit Storage Management (wasRe: Any Suggestions? — 1 msgs

### Explicit Storage Management (wasRe: Any Suggestions?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839p7o$28c$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The draft Standard *does* introduce USAGE POINTER (and PROCEDURE-POINTER). It
also introduces the ALLOCATE and FREE statements. Finally, it also introduces
"based tables/data-items".

All of this CAN be used in OO programs - or not.  Those who "hate" doing this
stuff explicitly will NOT be forced into using it.  However, those users who
interface with any API's or other facilities that expect them, will find the
support in the new Standard.

As far as those who "hate this stuff" (and/or find it error prone) and end-up
having to maintain someone ELSE's code that uses it, I have no solution for
you.
```

---

<a id="t-117c7b098a"></a>
## Thread: Program Help — 2 msgs

### Re: Program Help

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839bpv$4ij$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<839jmp$9gg$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
In the screen section, ADD-SCREEN, in ADD_SCR.CPY,
change the FROM to USING. This will display the
information and update the fields during the ACCEPT.
As in,

       05  LINE 07 COLUMN 32 PIC X(9)
               using ID-NUMBER AUTO REQUIRED.

instead of,

           05  LINE 07 COLUMN 32 PIC X(9)
                   FROM ID-NUMBER AUTO REQUIRED.

You have a problem with recursive PERFORMs some
of which return control to lower levels and some of which
return control to the beginning of the paragraph.

To allow me to test the program, I changed some of
the PERFORMs to GO TO; others to CONTINUE.
You should modify the logic!

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

Chris P. wrote in message <839jmp$9gg$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...
>Hi,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>information, the screen does not display the member's prior information.
[...]
>Any suggestions would be great-
```

```

example should have been:

       05  LINE 07 COLUMN 32 PIC X(9)
               using ID-NUMBER AUTO REQUIRED.

instead of,

           05  LINE 07 COLUMN 32 PIC X(9)
                   TO ID-NUMBER AUTO REQUIRED.
------------------
Rick Smith

<a id="t-96b66fb20c"></a>
## Thread: How to use a subprogram with cics extension in an non-cics batch program. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: How to use a subprogram with cics extension in an non-cics batch program.

- **From:** "Alan Hynes" <Alan.Hynes@eds.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf468f$a12b4b60$9aa25d94@j01297478.saturn.gm.com>`
- **References:** `<KQ654.1366$b67.9115@news1.online.no> <8332av$o13$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in article
<8332av$o13$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>...
> If you subprogram has EXEC CICS statements in it, then you cannot use it
in
> batch.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I have a cobol2 subprogram that uses som cics extensions. I need to
call
> > this subprogram form a cobol2 batch program running outside cics. Can I
do
> > this, or do I have to rewrite the subprogram, removing the cics
portions?
> >

If this is subprogram is going to continuously run in batch as well as CICS
you 
may want to put the guts of the code and working storage in copy members
and then code the CICS or file access in same named paragraphs (sections)
in the actual programs.  

This way when your program wants to read the invoice file it would
code :
	PERFORM READ-INVOICE-FILE
 in the copy member.  Then the CICS program would have a section like:

READ-INVOICE-FILE SECTION.

	EXEC CICS
		READ DDNAME
	END-EXEC

	(return code check)
	.

and the batch program would have a section like:

READ-INVOICE-FILE SECTION.
	
	READ FILENAME
		AT END (or otherchecks)
	END-READ
	.

This way when you have to modify the logic of the program
you only have to change it in one place (and compile and test
both).  I suspect that finding the copy member in procedure division
would encourage you to look for the other times it is used and
keep the two programs in sync.
```

<a id="t-46d4b60787"></a>
## Thread: How to use a subprogram with cics extension in an non-cics batch program. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: How to use a subprogram with cics extension in an non-cics batch program.

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j7kd5sgkfiv2suve1avbr5827h4ssn78o7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<KQ654.1366$b67.9115@news1.online.no>`

```
On Mon, 13 Dec 1999 14:50:31 +0100, "Steinar Holmeide" <Steinar.Holmeide@SDS.no> wrote:

>hello,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Okay, let's try.  You have a subprogram S which is called by someone, either under CICS or
in BATCH.

The program S needs to be translated by the CICS translator, which inserts 2 arguments at
the PROCEDURE DIVISION USING phrase. You must take care of this in the CALLING program,
which has to provide two dummy arguments

Also, You cannot use the EXEC CICS commands outside of CICS.

Therefore, you need a little program that checks if you are running under CICS.  An
Assembler program that uses the DFHAFCD TYPE=LOCATE,CB=AFCB macro would return that
information.  Then you would have to write the program in a two thronged approach, one
path for the case when you are in CICS (using the EXEC CICS commands), and the second path
when running in batch (not using the EXEC CICS commands).

BTW, if you look at the expansion od the DFHAFCD MACRO, you can do the same thing under
CICS, without having to ressort to Assembler.  It is just a simple matter of following a
control block chain




     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         What does CICS mean? - Can't Implement Consistent Software
```

#### ↳ Re: How to use a subprogram with cics extension in an non-cics batch program.

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385780C2.A8D0E84@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<KQ654.1366$b67.9115@news1.online.no> <j7kd5sgkfiv2suve1avbr5827h4ssn78o7@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 13 Dec 1999 14:50:31 +0100, "Steinar Holmeide" <Steinar.Holmeide@SDS.no> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Why.   I created a batch routine that was statically linked into both
batch and CICS.   It worked perfectly with a single batch cobol
compile.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to use a subprogram with cics extension in an non-cics batch program.

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<839e6q$v0a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<KQ654.1366$b67.9115@news1.online.no> <j7kd5sgkfiv2suve1avbr5827h4ssn78o7@4ax.com> <385780C2.A8D0E84@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <385780C2.A8D0E84@zip.com.au>,
  Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
>
> Why.   I created a batch routine that was statically linked into both
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ken,

When calling a CICS Program from Batch (a program that has CICS
commands in it), addressability must be satisfied for the first two
parameters that the sub-program is expecting (DFHEIBLK and DFHCOMMAREA)
in your CALL statement, such as:

03 DUMMY-ARG PIC X(01).
03 THIRD-PARAMETER PIC X(whatever).

CALL SUBPGM USING DUMMY-ARG, DUMMY-ARG, THIRD-PARAMETER.

The CICS Translator inserts (during the TRANSLATION Stage):

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING DFHEIBLK DFHCOMMAREA.

The sub-program source must specify:

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING THIRD-PARAMETER.

During the TRANSLATION Stage, the Translator will then insert DFHEIBLK
and DFHCOMMAREA just prior to THIRD-PARAMETER, resulting in the correct
parameter sequence.

I'm curious of how it was done without these requirements ? Did the sub-
program call another sub-program to determine the execution environment
or was there intelligence placed in the THIRD-PARAMETER ?

However, if the sub-program does NOT contain CICS Commands, then this
is perfectly legitimate co-usage and none of what I've said is
applicable.

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

As Bill indicated these are clearing instructions for CICS.   What he didn't mention is if you are using CICS 4.x or I believe it is CICS TS 1.3 these are no longer supported funtions.  Dump them, the system will take care of things for you.   If you are using CICS 2.1.2 you aren't Y2K compliant and I wish you well.

Eileen


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

## Thread: COPY handling by Compiler — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COPY handling by Compiler

- **From:** sujay@mahindrabt.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8378u7$bib$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I want to know how COBOL Complier will handle following example.

example-

COPY temp1 replacing ==abc def== BY ==xyz tmp==.

-----------------------------------------------------------

i.e. what I want to know is - we can give more than one word in psudo
text and these words can be replaced by one or more words. How this is
processed by Compiler?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY handling by Compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<837bdi$nt5$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8378u7$bib$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I don't really understand your question.  BASICALLY (a few minor problems)
you can replace ANY number of text-words by any other number of text-words
(including multiple lines on either the replaced or replacing text).  The
words to be replaced must appear "together" in the source text, if that is
your question, i.e. your sample would NOT replace text that had

    abc xyz def

but most certainly WOULD replace text that had

   abc abc def
      by
   abc xyz tmp

Is that your question?
```

---

<a id="t-949c0569bf"></a>
## Thread: How to control a device connected to serial port — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: How to control a device connected to serial port

- **From:** "Charles Goodman" <cgoodman@mbnet.mb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<igE54.1122$pP.26696@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8363c2$hl2$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net>`

```
Sorry about the size of the code snippet, but here is some actual code that
does I-O to the RS232.
Read the manual when looking at the code.

Good Luck

"Tecnopolis. Emilio Escribano" <tecnopolis@sureste.net> wrote in message
news:8363c2$hl2$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net...
> I'm using Microsoft Cobol 4.5 (based on Microfocus Cobol) and I need to
> control a device connected to a serial port. Is it posible?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


045400
045500*    these are the send/receive buffer addresses.
045600 01  PORT1                   PIC 9(5) VALUE 1016.
045700 01  PORT2                   PIC 9(5) VALUE 0760.
045800*    this is the line status register address.
045900 01  STAT1                   PIC 9(5) VALUE 1021.
046000 01  STAT2                   PIC 9(5) VALUE 0765.
046100*    these are the addresses in use
046200 01  COM-PORT                PIC 9(5) VALUE 1016.
046300 01  COM-STAT                PIC 9(5) VALUE 1021.
046400
       01  PORT-NUMBER PIC X VALUE "1".
046500



046600*    this is the character to be received from the RS232 port
046700 01  IN-CHAR          PIC X.
046800
046900*    this is to hold the line status information.
047000 01  LINE-STATUS-X.
047100     05  LINE-STATUS             PIC 9(2) COMP-X.
047200 01  LINE-STATUS-REM PIC 99.
047300
047400 01  BIT-TABLE.
047500         05  BIT-7    PIC X.
047600         05  BIT-6    PIC X.
047700         05  BIT-5    PIC X.
047800         05  BIT-4    PIC X.
047900         05  BIT-3    PIC X.
048000         05  BIT-2    PIC X.
048100         05  BIT-1    PIC X.
048200         05  BIT-0    PIC X.
048300



042700
042800 01  BYTESTREAM-IN-DATA.
042900     05  BY-IX OCCURS 1024 INDEXED BY BIX.
043000         10  BY-IXCOMP PIC 99 COMP.
043100 01  BYTESTREAM-IN-ACTUAL         PIC 9999  VALUE 0.
043200 01  BYTESTREAM-IN-LOOPS          PIC 9999  VALUE 0.
043300
043400 01  BYTESTREAM-OUT-DATA.
043500     05  BOD-X PIC X OCCURS 512 INDEXED BY BODX.
043600 01  BYTESTREAM-OUT-DATA-SIZE     PIC 9999 COMP.
043700 01  BYTESTREAM-OUT-LOOPS         PIC 9999  VALUE 0.
043800
043900
044000 01  BYTESTREAM-IN-WHILE-OUT.
044100     05  BY-IOX OCCURS 1024 INDEXED BY BIOX.
044200         10  BY-IOXCOMP PIC 99 COMP.
044300 01  BYTESTREAM-IN-OUT-ACTUAL         PIC 9999  VALUE 0.
044400



091800
091900 SEND-OUT.
092000     IF BOD-X ( 1 ) = X"0A"
092100         MOVE X"0D" TO BOD-X ( 1 ).
092200     SET BODX TO 1.
092300     MOVE 0 TO BYTESTREAM-OUT-LOOPS.
092400 SEND-OUT-LOOP.
092500     CALL X"87" USING COM-STAT LINE-STATUS.
092600     DIVIDE LINE-STATUS BY 2 GIVING LINE-STATUS
092700             REMAINDER LINE-STATUS-REM.
092800     IF LINE-STATUS-REM NOT = 1 GO TO SEND-OUT-NOIN.
092900     CALL X"87" USING COM-PORT IN-CHAR.
093000     MOVE IN-CHAR TO BY-IOX (BIOX).
093100     IF BY-IX (BIX) > X"7F"
093200         SUBTRACT 128 FROM BY-IOXCOMP (BIOX).
093300     IF BYTESTREAM-IN-OUT-ACTUAL < 500
093400         ADD 1 TO BYTESTREAM-IN-OUT-ACTUAL
093500         SET BIOX UP BY 1.
093600     GO TO SEND-OUT-LOOP.
093700 SEND-OUT-NOIN.
093800     CALL X"F5" USING LINE-STATUS-X BIT-TABLE.
093900     IF BIT-5 = 1 GO TO SEND-OUT-READY.
094000     ADD 1 TO BYTESTREAM-OUT-LOOPS.
094100     IF BYTESTREAM-OUT-LOOPS < 7000 GO TO SEND-OUT-LOOP.
094200 SEND-OUT-READY.
094300     MOVE 0 TO BYTESTREAM-OUT-LOOPS.
094400     MOVE BOD-X (BODX) TO ONE-BYTE.
094500     CALL X"88" USING COM-PORT ONE-BYTE.
094600     PERFORM DISPLAY-OUT THRU DISPLAY-OUT-EXIT.
094700     SET BODX UP BY 1.
094800     IF BODX NOT > BYTESTREAM-OUT-DATA-SIZE
094900         GO TO SEND-OUT-LOOP.
095000 SEND-OUT-EXIT.
095100     EXIT.
095200


106900
107000 GET-INPUT.
107100     IF BYTESTREAM-IN-OUT-ACTUAL = 0 GO TO GET-INPUT-NOOLD.
107200     MOVE BYTESTREAM-IN-WHILE-OUT  TO BYTESTREAM-IN-DATA.
107300     MOVE BYTESTREAM-IN-OUT-ACTUAL TO BYTESTREAM-IN-ACTUAL.
107400     SET BIX TO BIOX.
107500     SET BIOX TO 1.
107600     MOVE 0 TO BYTESTREAM-IN-OUT-ACTUAL.
107700     GO TO GET-INPUT-WASOLD.
107800 GET-INPUT-NOOLD.
107900     MOVE SPACE TO BYTESTREAM-IN-DATA.
108000     SET BIX TO 1.
108100     MOVE 0 TO BYTESTREAM-IN-ACTUAL.
108200 GET-INPUT-WASOLD.
108300     MOVE 0 TO BYTESTREAM-IN-LOOPS.
108400 GET-INPUT-LOOP.
108500     CALL X"87" USING COM-STAT LINE-STATUS.
108600     DIVIDE LINE-STATUS BY 2 GIVING LINE-STATUS
108700             REMAINDER LINE-STATUS.
108800     IF LINE-STATUS NOT = 1 GO TO GET-INPUT-NONE.
108900     CALL X"87" USING COM-PORT IN-CHAR.
109000     MOVE IN-CHAR TO BY-IX (BIX).
109100     IF BY-IX (BIX) > X"7F"
109200         SUBTRACT 128 FROM BY-IXCOMP (BIX).
109300     ADD 1 TO BYTESTREAM-IN-ACTUAL.
109400     IF BYTESTREAM-IN-ACTUAL < 500 GO TO GET-INPUT-LOOP.
109500 GET-INPUT-NONE.
109600     IF BYTESTREAM-IN-ACTUAL NOT = 0 GO TO GET-INPUT-EXIT.
109700     ADD 1 TO BYTESTREAM-IN-LOOPS.
109800     IF BYTESTREAM-IN-LOOPS < 50 GO TO GET-INPUT-LOOP.
109900 GET-INPUT-EXIT.
110000     EXIT.
110100
```

## Thread: back to COBOL from RADs ? — 1 msgs

### back to COBOL from RADs ?

- **From:** sminni@vsnl.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<837pa2$n6a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
4 yrs ago I move to a RAD under Win9x
from MF-COBOL under DOS.

Now I want advice on going back.

Which version is good considering
  Win9x/NT & Linux (incl terminal mode)
  Free deployment
  Interface to RDBMS (ODBC & Oracle8i)
  small code & ease of deployment installation
  Re-usability of Code/Object orientation
    and above all
  Stability

Its a lot but I migrated to RAD with the
dreams of cutting down development time.

But I am now missing
  stability & getting [the RAD] to do
    exactly what I want it do
  in-built file structures spl. Indexed

and...What is the JOB security/
  Prospects of COBOL programmers

Regards
Sanjay Minni nospam_sminni@vsnl.com
http://www.minisoftindia.com
Calcutta India


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: (IBM mainframe people may be interested) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: (IBM mainframe people may be interested)

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38577147.59E57E2A@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<82oso6$kn$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <uDguGCoQ$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa02> <3855DC67.551DCBEC@acm.org>`

```
Doubt you will see PDSE as loadlibs at IPL time.  Takes way too
much to get things started before they can be used.  The IPL is
virtualy complete before a PDSE cam be opened.  Sorry.

(By IPL, I mean geting in the nucleus, starting the master
schedular, searching the PARMLIBs, etc.  Once that is done, there
is still tons of initialization, mostly starting address spaces,
that people consider part of an IPL but which I do not.)

Joel C. Ewing wrote:
> 
> ChrisOsborne wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joel C. Ewing, Fort Smith, AR        jcewing@acm.org
```

## Thread: Y2K — 16 this month / 18 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83b3av$mdd$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Well, I just sent out a last batch of CD's (a screen defaulting to the wrong
century), and a letter to each and every user reminding them to review
backup/restore procedures next week, and to be sure to do at least two
backups between Christmas and New Years.

I took our largest customer, closed December (to-date), redated Novembers
transactions, and set up a machine with January's date, then did a full
months run on all systems. Everything balanced.  All ageing was correct.

I think we are as ready as we can get.  Any last minute
ideas/precautions/caveats that anyone can think of?
```

```

Last minute checks:

External data (Crazy date-formats, validations etc.)

Regards
Ib

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:83dkk9$pe0$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net...
>
> donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> And by extension the 366th day of the year.  (When everyone will once
again
> be saying "This time we can celebrate the REAL change of Millennium - and
no
> computer problem to worry about - but forgetting that the START of Dec 31,
> 2000 may find some YYDDD problems.)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

 And the 20th Century ends on December 31, 2000 as well.

People just still don't understand this new math (which has been going on
for about 30-40 years).

That Last Y2K problem is that a century that ends in 00 and divides evenly
by 400 is considered a leap year, thus 366 days in a year.  1900 was not a
leap year.
```

The system I wrote for my friends Bowling center has been compliant
from the beginning.  I always used 4 digit dates (Written initially in
1991).  However, I am concerned about the current date on the machines
getting off, so I will be sending him a patch that checks for a year <
90 and > 80 - screamin if it finds one.  I'm putting it in the main
menu.

## Thread: ILBOABNO — 14 msgs

### ILBOABNO

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
William M. Klein suggested calling ILBOABNO instead of doing a divide by
zero in an IBM mainframe environment when I want an abend.

I finally got a program where I didn't need to follow standards and
thought I would try it, but CEE tells me that my program can't find this
module.  What else do I need to do?
```

#### ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

- **From:** mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-OTv15omNdbK8@localhost>`
- **References:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
On Thu, 16 Dec 1999 18:03:33, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote:

> calling ILBOABNO , but CEE tells me 
>  
Sorry, meant to include example:
            IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
            PROGRAM-ID. CBL3ABD.

            DATA DIVISION.
            WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
            01  ABDCODE                 PIC S9(9) BINARY.
            01  TIMING                  PIC S9(9) BINARY.
            PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            PARA-CBLMGET.

           **********************************************
           ** 3415 is the abend code to be issued,     **
           ** a timing of zero requests an abend       **
           ** without clean-up                         **
           **********************************************
                     MOVE 3415 TO ABDCODE.
                     MOVE 0 TO TIMING.
                     CALL "CEE3ABD" USING ABDCODE , TIMING.

                     GOBACK.
Courtesy IBM manual CEEA3010, LE Programmers' Reference
 
=Dwight=
X1=L, X2=L & the domain is phonetic
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83bcvc$jb1$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-OTv15omNdbK8@localhost>`

```
If you can be guaranteed that the LE-runtime will be there, then IN DEED,
using a call to CEE3ABD is "preferred" over calls to ILBOABN0.  However, if
you can't be sure whether LE will OR WILL NOT be there, then use ILBOABN0 -
which is there in ALL IBM mainframe COBOL run-times (including LE).

Now to answer your problem,  the name of the module has the letter "O" as the
4th character and the number "0" as the 8th character.  I think you coded
both as the letter.  (Hard to make obvious in quick NG notes - sorry for
misleading you)
```

> misleading you)

Thanks, I tried both and am preparing a message to someone on the
standards committee with the results of my test and with your warning. 
I don't want a maintenance upgrade to change our abort routine!!

CALL 'ILBOABN0'
produces a User completion code of 4039 and a CEEDUMP

Apparently with COBOL for MVS, the preferred method is:
And CALL 'CEE3ABD' USING MY-ERROR, CLEAN-UP

I am not sure what normal cleanup of the enclave or no-cleanup gives
us.  I didn't see a CEEDUPM when I ran with zero, I'll try 1 and see if
I find any difference.
```

#### ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

- **From:** mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-wnBBHxqcCOfz@localhost>`
- **References:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
On Thu, 16 Dec 1999 18:03:33, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote:

> calling ILBOABNO instead of doing a divide by
> zero ... but CEE tells me that my program can't find this
> module.  What else do I need to do?
>  
ILBO is the old way.  LE replaces it with a call to  CEE3ABD, which has two parameters,
the first is a fullword integer <= 4095 that specifies the abend code and the second is a 
zero for just abend, don't clean up the enclave, or a one to do normal cleanup.
=Dwight=
X1=L, X2=L & the domain is phonetic
```

#### ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
