```
In article <38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>,
  Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, post, them!! wrote:
> William M. Klein suggested calling ILBOABNO instead of doing a divide
by
> zero in an IBM mainframe environment when I want an abend.
>
> I finally got a program where I didn't need to follow standards and
> thought I would try it, but CEE tells me that my program can't find
this
> module.  What else do I need to do?
>

Howard,

You could also force a S0C7 by doing the following:

03 WS-PACKED PIC S9(15) PACKED-DECIMAL.
03 WS-PACKED-X REDEFINES WS-PACKED PIC X(08).

MOVE LOW-VALUES TO WS-PACKED-X.
ADD +1 TO WS-PACKED.

He'll try to do an 'AP', get a hardware interrupt and abend.

Compile with 'LIST,NOOFSET' and check the generated ASSEMBLER.

HTH....

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3859644A.9A61F01C@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <83bmh6$ia4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I don't know if that is any better than our current shop standard.  It
depends on compiler options and SOC7 comes from more "natural" errors -
it is useful to have a unique error which yells ABORT to operators but
which only happens when the code catches the error (after doing a
display).

It is hard enough to change a shop standard (especially when operators
are trained to look for divide by zero errors).

WOB Consulting wrote:
> 
> In article <38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>,
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83bpfr$cth$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <83bmh6$ia4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Please NO - don't do this.

Getting a S0C7 is just as "unpredictable" as doing a divide by zero. (As
anyone who has EVER converted from OS/VS COBOL to COBOL for this-and-that can
tell you.)

Please do NOT assume that doing something that is explicitly UNDEFINED will
result (tomorrow or the day after) in getting an ABEND.  Yes, the Assembler
instruction MAY cause an system ABEND today, but there is NO guarantee that
it will with the next release, version, or even maintenance level of the
compiler.

Please DO use the documented (and supported) way of getting an ABEND.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83cba4$8uc8$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <83bmh6$ia4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83bpfr$cth$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Another reason for not forcing a S0C7 is that it becomes difficult to
distinguish between a "legitimate" (unexpected) S0C7 and a forced one.
```

```
In article <83cba4$8uc8$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>,
  "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:
>
> Another reason for not forcing a S0C7 is that it becomes difficult to
> distinguish between a "legitimate" (unexpected) S0C7 and a forced one.
>
On a related note - sometimes it's better to set a return code, close
files, and stop run. Then have Condition Code in a subsequent JCL step,
which executes some Utility ABEND-like program. This is not always
practical, of course.
The advantage is that your output files will contain the most recent
records so you might be able to use that info in figuring out the
glitch. Whereas on an abend, the files will likely be dispositioned
(NEW,CATLG,DELETE) so won't be there to peek at. True - Abendaid should
show the last records, as will the dump, but it's sometimes easier to
view the files.
In addition all your displays will show - I've noticed that sometimes
the SYSOUT buffer does not get flushed to the output JCL-etc listing
when an abend occurs (at least that's my guess at the technical reason).
This technique has been useful in situations when there was a program-
forced abend because some end-of-program control totals were not OK.
I.e., could put the output files thru some investigation using mini-
programs, SORTs, etc.

John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385E486D.45DC5E75@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<38592975.34E9A7BE@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <83bmh6$ia4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83bpfr$cth$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <83cba4$8uc8$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <83dm3l$sth$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
One advantage to this is that you can vary your RETURN CODE
considerably, while the ABEND program gives the standard ABEND.  We have
to use this technique with EasyTrieve jobs which won't ABEND as we
want.  The JCL checks the return code and then forces an ABEND Cobol
program.

john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991222020025.23435.00001248@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<385E486D.45DC5E75@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Maybe I missed something - the reason that COBOL for OS/390 MVS and VM changed
the error handling strategy is, according to my (mis-)interpetration of the
advice from IBM Visual Age Services, is to permit the shop to build an
inventory of "condition-handlers", which will, ideally, deal with the problem,
and then resume execution (somewhat like what the X37 product does for various
out-of-space errors), or, at the very least, produce meaningful messages to
assist in correction.  The LE manuals, rather than the language-specific
manuals, have a better explanation and samples.  And, from a development point
of view, that is one of the major reasons to migrate to LE.
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ILBOABNO

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991222102519.06424.00000767@ng-fz1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19991222020025.23435.00001248@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
Steve Newton writes...

>Maybe I missed something - the reason that COBOL for OS/390 MVS and VM
>changed
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>of view, that is one of the major reasons to migrate to LE.

Wow, the developers of LE would love to hear that.

When I first taught my LE class for IBM Level 2 support for LE at Santa Teresa
(this was before it was moved to Poughkeepsie), the developers were in the back
of the classroom.

Talking with them informally later, they were convinced that what you said was
the going to be the main driving force behind migrating to LE. But my
experience and observation were (and still are) that the driving forces for
moving to LE are:

1) no choice; IBM is not subtle here
2) Date and time services
3) Dynamic storage services for COBOL

I agree that LE condition handling is great and powerful; I just don't see many
shops stepping up to the work it takes to get it going in the first place. (Of
course, Y2K remediation and freezes have impacted this a lot.)

After teaching the course in Copenhagen, I'm an even bigger fan than before for
the LE messaging services; if you need to issue a message to a user in the
language of their choice (say an ATM or kiosk that gives the user a choice of
languages), LE messaging services are very slick.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cookies, Re: +++GET crap

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** wtanksle@hawking.armored.net (William Tanksley)
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.fortran
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn85omcl.i4b.wtanksle@hawking.armored.net>`
- **References:** `<385ACC90.E540C235@yahoo.com> <38597c5a.42431791@news-server> <Vbj64.107136$7I4.2738053@news5.giganews.com> <83dgfm$n4t$1@news.laserlink.net> <945443836.992852@dibbs3.eur.cis.philips.com> <WmC64.2152$M34.24735@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net> <83foct$nur$1@the-fly.zip.com.au> <JgM64.4493$M34.30034@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
On Sat, 18 Dec 1999 07:48:23 -0600, Jerry P wrote:
>Stephen Quinn <squinn@brutecom.com.au> wrote in message

>> Jerry & Richard

>> Try Cookie Pal from http://www.kburra.com/

>I looked at 'Cookie Pal.' It seems to be an ordinary cookie
>manager (accept, reject, delete, filter, etc.) - one of may on the
>market (although apparently a good one).

>I was hoping for a cookie "Spoofer," - one that deliberately returns
>user-adjustable information to confound the cookie-creator.

Um...  No need.  The cookie creator is the one who creates ALL of the
information in the cookies.  Your system is used to store the cookie and
send the cookie, but it plays NO role in the creation of the cookie.  So
the only person who knows how to edit the cookie is the person who created
it, the person on the other side.

A lot of people seem to think that cookies contain info about them.  They
don't and can't (unless you told the other site something about yourself).
The closest they can come is recording the number of times you visit a
site, or (more annoyingly) the locations of the sites from which you view
an advertisement service (doubleclick.net is notorious for this).

If you insist on changing cookies, though, you're free to hand-edit them
-- netscape stores them in "cookies.txt".  I have no clue how to get at
IE's cookies; it does terrible things to make its cookies as hard to deal
with as possible.
```

```
Richard Press <rap@usa.take-this-out.net> wrote in message
news:83dgfm$n4t$1@news.laserlink.net...

> I am deadly tired of all the invasions of my privacy these days.  I
tried
> turning off cookies, but hardly anything on the web works without
them.
> Ditto Java.  Every day, 20 or more spams... I guess the only way to
fight
> back is get a disposable email address and change it frequently.
>

Ain't it the truth.

However, disposable e-mail addresses (Hotmail, Excite, Yahoo,
etc.), along with breeding grounds for spammers (AOL), are
sometimes trashed by ISPs or e-mail reviewers.

One service I've had fun with is found at
www.spamcop.net. You can paste a spam onto a form
and they'll dig out to whom to send replies. For a (tiny)
fee, they can interpose themselves in your mail stream
and automatically a)filter the spam, b) bitch to the ISPs
responsible.
```

- **From:** wtanksle@hawking.armored.net (William Tanksley)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.fortran

- **References:** `<385ACC90.E540C235@yahoo.com> <38597c5a.42431791@news-server> <Vbj64.107136$7I4.2738053@news5.giganews.com> <83dgfm$n4t$1@news.laserlink.net>`

```
On Fri, 17 Dec 1999 09:14:11 -0500, Richard Press wrote:
>What's bugging me is that, using Outlook Express, when the message first
>appeared in the preview panel it automatically triggered my browser it went
>to their web site.  I'm concerned that it carried with it my email address
>and heaven knows what else about me.  Plus, what is this crap in the subject
>line?...

I highly recommend turning off your preview panel as soon as possible.
There are a large number of security holes in VBScript, one of which has
been used to load a demonstration virus.  Don't risk discovering a real
virus that way!  Turn on autopreview to replace it -- it's (currently
thought ot be) safe, since it only displays text.
```

```
Richard Press wrote:
> 
> What's bugging me is that, using Outlook Express, when the message first
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
If you check the source code you'll likely find some
javascript like the following. The random numbers in the subject 
line are designed to defeat spambots by making the message
appear unique. Turn off preview or use a different browser.

<html>
<body onload="window.open('http://www.?????.com/')">
</body>
</html>
```

```
In article <geS64.3338$lM5.28779@news1.online.no>,
Eirik Mangseth <eirik@otp.no> wrote:
>Then you might be so kind to remove comp.lang.eiffel as well?

I'm sorry the rest of you had to get this huge thread.
comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects has been around for years but has
become newly popular now that the vendor of Visual Objects has lost
their Compuserve forum.

Many of us are new to Usenet and aren't used to the type of
self-moderation that's required here.  I've noticed that practically
/all/ of the followups to this spam are from c.l.c.v-o regulars.  This
posting is intended to point Visual Objects programmers in the right
direction with regard to some newsgroup management issues, and I hope
it also steers the thread out of the other newsgroups.

I'm setting followups to just c.l.c.v-o in the hopes that this will
deter more cross-posted off-topic stuff.

VO people, please do a couple of things to help keep Usenet useable:

0. Make sure you read and understand everything in the newsgroup
   news.announce.newusers.  It takes maybe an hour, once in your
   life, and will make much more of this make sense.

1. Don't follow up to spammed advertisements.  If you SIMPLY MUST
   respond on the newsgroup, check the headers and post ONLY to the
   newsgroup you actually follow.

2. Note that c.l.c.v-o is a little unusual for a programmer's
   newsgroup in that we explicitly _do_ welcome advertisements
   that are brief, strictly relevant to Visual Objects, and not
   too frequent.  This is *not* the norm in other comp.* groups
   so be careful.

3. If you're looking for Marijo, she's not here.  I (humbly) know
   about as much as anybody else does about newsgroup administration
   issues on comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects.  So if you don't quite
   understand how all this works after following step #0, please ask.
   I don't mind getting questions from Clipper and VO people once in
   a while about how to use Usenet.

And one tip for everyone else, in case it comes up again soon: Visual
Objects is not Visual Basic.  Totally different product, language,
vendor.

Thanks.  Let's be careful out there!
```

## Thread: Character >>> Binary conversion — 4 msgs

### Character >>> Binary conversion

- **From:** Bob Sharp <trackbowler@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83abq8$jl7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi


I need to read through a VSAM file sequentially.

We have a VSAMIO function but it has a  "SetSequential"
instruction that has me flummoxed.

Anyone know of it and how it is used ?
Any code examples ?

Another (simpler ?) possibility is increamenting the key
and READing next record (KEY  greater than), but how
do I convert some character parts of the key in order
to add the X'01', and then converting back ???


a fast response would be greatly appreciated as I need to
get this job finished before the Xmas holidays.


regards

      {8^)|





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Character >>> Binary conversion

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83b9fs$92o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<83abq8$jl7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <83abq8$jl7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Bob Sharp <trackbowler@my-deja.com> wrote:
> Hi
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

To increment, take each target byte, load it into the low-order of a
halfword, add +1 and restore it back.

03 WS-HWORD PIC S9(04) BINARY.
03 WS-HWORD-X REDEFINES WS-HWORD PIC X(02).

MOVE LOW-VALUE TO WS-HWORD-X (1:1).
MOVE WS-TARGET-BYTE TO WS-HWORD-X (2:1).

IF  WS-HWORD-X (2:1) IS LESS THAN HIGH-VALUE
    ADD +1 TO WS-HWORD
    END-IF.

MOVE WS-HWORD-X (2:1) TO WS-TARGET-BYTE.

========================================================================

HTH....

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Character >>> Binary conversion

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<740AF63FC4913E29.76DD662A5F1C4709.B642CF3C9F7C9999@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<83abq8$jl7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 16 Dec 1999 09:36:08 GMT, Bob Sharp <trackbowler@my-deja.com>
enlightened us:

>Hi
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

You didn't state your platform so I'm guessing that it is IBM
mainfrmae since you mentioned VSAM.

To read a VSAM file sequentially you only need to open it as input (or
I-O) with access sequential (or dynamic) defined in the select
statement.  Then you just READ the file being sure to use the AT END
statement.  There is no need to set a key for a sequential read.

If, however you need to start reading at a particular place, then you
set a key to a value, do a Start Browse followed by a READ NEXT.
You'll need to check your file status or use INVALID KEY and AT END
clauses.  From that point, every READ NEXT will read the next
sequential record.

HTH.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I don't suffer from insanity, I enjoy every minute of it. 


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

```
Programmers were rewarded for being wordy and penalized for being
terse.  I wonder if that's when people started having a line with only
```

<a id="t-3955b62106"></a>
## Thread: pointers — 7 msgs

### pointers

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83ave8$kc3$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Sorry about the lack of quoting, but every time I quoted your message, my
news server rejected it as having a line too long (???).

With regard to pointers, and the presence/lack thereof in OOP Cobol.

***** This is not a pointer
01 my-object        usage object reference.
*****However, it's sole purpose is to point to something.
*****There is absolutely not purpose it can fill outside of pointing to
something

It might not smell as sweet, but it is still a rose.
It can still produce ever expanding memory creep.

How is it not a pointer?
```

#### ↳ Re: pointers

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83b8n4$rln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<83ave8$kc3$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

: ***** This is not a pointer
: 01 my-object        usage object reference.
: *****However, it's sole purpose is to point to something.
: *****There is absolutely not purpose it can fill outside of pointing to
: something

: It might not smell as sweet, but it is still a rose.
: It can still produce ever expanding memory creep.

: How is it not a pointer?

When C programmers complain about a lack of pointers they
usually mean the ability to use and manipulate low level
memory addresses directly.

That is they have been taught that, for example, it is much
more efficient to step along a character array by incrementing
a pointer than to use an index.  As they learnt this in the
'Advanced C' course they think that is an advanced language
feature (and thus Cobol is not an 'advanced' language), when
in fact it is the programmer that must be 'advanced' enough
to cope with low-level grovelling in the bits.

Modern high-level languages (which eliminates C on two counts)
generally avoid pointers because they make it difficult to
have reliable working programs.

Though object references are memory addresses (they need not be)
they are not able to be manipulated in any way and thus are
not pointers as in C.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: pointers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38592B43.F4A992D3@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<83ave8$kc3$1@news.igs.net> <83b8n4$rln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
High level languages leave the details to the compiler.  Thus people who
know the environment can write the compiler to optimize without having
to guess too much what exactly the programmer intended.

It is usually safe to optimize a single command as much as we want.  In
Cobol that single command can be a COMPUTE.  In C it is more likely to
be an increment.  A COMPUTE is complex enough that there is plenty of
optimizing available.  You can't optimize an increment.

Sure a top programmer will be able to optimize better than the compiler
- knowing precisely the specifics of his needs.  But shops are full of
average programmers whose job is to make workable and maintainable code
without obscure tricks.  So put the optimization in the background.

Java is cheaper to maintain than C, partially because memory leaks are
hard to debug.  Let someone else design a way to clean up memory - sure
it will cost some in run time efficiency - but which is more expensive,
run time costs, or maintenance costs?

Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> When C programmers complain about a lack of pointers they
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: pointers

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83avof$10c0$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<83ave8$kc3$1@news.igs.net> <83b8n4$rln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <83b8n4$rln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
[...]
>Modern high-level languages (which eliminates C on two counts)
>generally avoid pointers because they make it difficult to
>have reliable working programs.

The problem is not pointers, it is dynamic memory allocation.

COBOL uses pointers for parameters to CALLed programs.
We just don't call them pointers. COBOL works reliably with
these pointers because the memory is statically allocated in
the CALLing program.

C programs could be written to behave as COBOL programs
by using static allocation; but it is far more common, in C, to
use dynamic allocation.

In any case, it is possible to have very reliable programs with
dynamic memory allocation, if one uses accessor functions
to handle the allocating and freeing of memory and to return
pointers, of the appropriate type, to allocated data.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: pointers

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83bmkh$uvo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<83ave8$kc3$1@news.igs.net> <83b8n4$rln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <83avof$10c0$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:

: Richard Plinston wrote in message <83b8n4$rln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
: [...]
:>Modern high-level languages (which eliminates C on two counts)
:>generally avoid pointers because they make it difficult to
:>have reliable working programs.

: The problem is not pointers, it is dynamic memory allocation.

No, the problem is programmers that introduce bugs by
omission or commission.

Objects use dynamic memory allocation, and, in say Java,
this does not cause problems.

: COBOL uses pointers for parameters to CALLed programs.
: We just don't call them pointers. COBOL works reliably with
: these pointers because the memory is statically allocated in
: the CALLing program.

In practical terms these are just memory addresses.  In 
technical terms they are <<references>>.  While pointers, references
and objects are all (generally) memory addresses 'pointers'
can be manipulated to have any arbitrary address.

: C programs could be written to behave as COBOL programs
: by using static allocation; but it is far more common, in C, to
: use dynamic allocation.

While there are many problems that are associated with dynamic
memory: not checking malloc result, using freed pointers,
accessing beyond allocated size, memory leakage; these are
not the only problems with pointers.

Dynamic memory can be safe as is shown by many languages, and
by OOCobol.

Static allocation does not eliminate pointer problems because
pointers can still be used, for example to step down an array.

: In any case, it is possible to have very reliable programs with
: dynamic memory allocation, if one uses accessor functions
: to handle the allocating and freeing of memory and to return
: pointers, of the appropriate type, to allocated data.

Exactly, but these tend not to be referred to as 'pointers'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: pointers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83c6te$1jiu$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<83ave8$kc3$1@news.igs.net> <83b8n4$rln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <83avof$10c0$1@news.hitter.net> <83bmkh$uvo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <83bmkh$uvo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>: The problem is not pointers, it is dynamic memory allocation.
[...]
>Objects use dynamic memory allocation, and, in say Java,
>this does not cause problems.

OK, the problem is "programmer-controlled" dynamic
memory allocation, the absence of which makes pointers
unnecessary, except for communicating with other
languages.

Modern high-level languages assume the control of dynamic
memory allocation by providing the means for programmers
to allocate memory resources in a more controlled environment.
These languages, by removing dynamic memory allocation,
from the direct control of programmers, tend toward higher
reliability in the programs created using those languages.

The only languages that come to mind Java, Visual Basic,
and the OO extensions to COBOL. Did you have other
languages in mind?

From the Java Language Specification, page 1:

"Java is a relatively high-level language, in that details
of the machine representation are not available through
the language. It includes automatic storage management,
typically using a garbage collector, to avoid the safety
problems of explicit deallocation (as in C�s free or C++�s
delete). ... Java does not include any unsafe constructs,
such as array accesses without index checking, since such
unsafe constructs would cause a program to behave in an
unspecified way."

From this statement, it appears as if Java controls dynamic
memory allocation for reliability; but avoids pointers for
security reasons. Though not stated, the "unspecified"
behavior can include malicious programming such as
introducing a virus into the computer; but could also include
reliability.

>: COBOL uses pointers for parameters to CALLed programs.
>: We just don't call them pointers. COBOL works reliably with
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>can be manipulated to have any arbitrary address.

Can we agree that a pointer to type T is an address and
the value at that address is a reference to type T?

>: C programs could be written to behave as COBOL programs
>: by using static allocation; but it is far more common, in C, to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>not the only problems with pointers.
[...]
>Static allocation does not eliminate pointer problems because
>pointers can still be used, for example to step down an array.

In this case, a pointer may work reliably in all cases but a size_t
subscript could be subject to intermittent failures. It depends
upon the use. I guess I don't see the problem with using a pointer.

For static allocation, the problems with pointers are not
significantly different than using a subscript with a value that is
outside the bounds of the array, where bounds checking of the
subscript is not done.

>: In any case, it is possible to have very reliable programs with
>: dynamic memory allocation, if one uses accessor functions
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Exactly, but these tend not to be referred to as 'pointers'.

It is almost always a pointer to a structure of some type.
I don't know what else to call it.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
PN79703, <is the relevant APAR but,> it is also in the LE Programming
Reference, from Release 6 on. LE Release 6 was shipped with OS/390 R2, so
every OS/390 customer should have a new enough copy...

 Here is the text of the APAR:

Make the following changes to the manuals:

COBOL for MVS & VM Programming Guide  SC26-4767-01
  In the Release 2 book on page 451, change the NOTE: to read:

  "Note: There is no way to get 4-digit year dates using
   OS/VS COBOL programs even if they are running under Language
   Environment.  You can use DYNAMIC calls from VS COBOL II
   programs to Language Environment Date/Time callable services.
   You cannot use STATIC calls from VS COBOL II programs to
   any Language Environment callable services."

   The list of LE services that can be dynamically called
   from VS COBOL II (any release) programs is:

         CEECBLDY      CEEISEC
         CEEDATE       CEELOCT
         CEEDATM       CEEQCEN
         CEEDAYS       CEESCEN
         CEEDYWK       CEESECI
         CEEGMT        CEESECS
         CEEGMTO       CEE3CTY

COBOL for MVS & VM Migration Guide    SC26-4764
  In Appendix A, "Commonly Asked Questions", change the answer
  to the first question under the heading "Language Environment
  Services".


  > Can VS COBOL II programs CALL Language Environment callable
    services?

   "You can use DYNAMIC calls from VS COBOL II programs to
    Language Environment Date/Time callable services.
    You cannot use DYNAMIC calls from VS COBOL II programs to
    other Language Environment callable services, nor can you
    use STATIC calls to any Language Environment callable
    services from VS COBOL II programs."

LE/370 Programming Reference SC26-3312-00 (Release 3),
LE for MVS & VM Programming Reference SC26-3312-01 (Release 4)
                                  and SC26-3312-02 (Release 5)
  Add the following note in 3 places after the bulleted list
  of languages which can call LE callable services:
    Release 3, at the beginning of chapter 2, page 29,
    Release 4, at the beginning of chapter 3, page 42, and
    Release 5, at the beginning of chapter 3, page 49

 "NOTE: You can use DYNAMIC calls from VS COBOL II programs to
        Language Environment Date/Time callable services.
        You cannot use DYNAMIC calls from VS COBOL II programs to
        other Language Environment callable services.
        You cannot use STATIC calls from VS COBOL II programs
        to any Language Environment callable services.

        The list of LE services that can be dynamically called
        from VS COBOL II (any release) programs is:

             CEECBLDY      CEEISEC
             CEEDATE       CEELOCT
             CEEDATM       CEEQCEN
             CEEDAYS       CEESCEN
             CEEDYWK       CEESECI
             CEEGMT        CEESECS
             CEEGMTO       CEE3CTY "
"
```

```
The program in question was generated by a 4GL, and in this particular
environment it is an exceedingly rare circumstance for modifications to be
made to the 4GL source; more typically debugging is done at the 4GL level.
IOW, the program source isn't something the "application programmers" deal
with in the first place, except in extremely rare circumstances.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
In article <38568a79$0$16152@fountain.mindlink.net>, Robert
Annandale <snarfle99@hotmail.com> writes
>Hello,
>
>To those of you who have Linux/AcuServer experience
>I appeal to you for help.
>

Robert,

Have you contacted Acucorp support with your problem (we're
usually pretty friendly!). 

You might want to try starting Acuserver with the command:

acuserve -start -le /tmp/trace.log -t3

This will give you a trace (in /tmp/trace.log) of all attempted
accesses. Be aware that it can generate a *lot* of information, so
be prepared to restrict access while you test the remote
accesses. The trace should give you as clue as to what is going
on. If not then forward the trace (with a problem description) to
<support@acucorp.com>. They will either deal with the problem
directly or forward it to the appropriate place.

If you don't get what you need this way (and I really believe you
will), please feel free to email me (neaton@acucorp.com) and I
will do my best to sort the problem out.

Please note that 'reply to' on this message will hit my private email
address which gets monitored sporadically. 'Join Acucorp and
see the world' they said. They were not kidding........   ;^)


Best regards

Nigel
```

## Thread: mf Variable File Formats — 17 msgs

### mf Variable File Formats

- **From:** "Lou de Freitas" <Luiz.deFreitas@dol.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net>`

```
With a MF created executable, I am having a problem creating a variable
length file defined by the use of occurs x times depending on y within the
fields that define the 01 level of the record.  The problem is that the
records are created with the max size even though the occurs depending on
has a value less than the max.  I confirmed this by viewing the created file
in hex and confirmed that this is a problem.

Anyone have a work around until MF gets me a corrected compiler?

Does it on WB  (W95) and Unix (Sun Solaris) versions of MF.

Thanks,

Lou
```

#### ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

- **From:** E. Johnson <elizrj@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83dvuj$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net>`

```
Hey Lou

I had this same problem.  I was trying to keep varying sized notes out
for various things on our Win95 POS system.  Then I realized I had set
up the FD wrong.  Here's what I had originally:

       FD NOTES
       01 NOTES-REC.
           03 NOTE-KEY.
               05 NOTE-TYPE                PIC X.
               05 NOTE-2-TYPE              PIC X(03).
               05 NOTE-NUMBER              PIC X(10).
           03 NOTE-FIELD.
               05 NOTE-ARRAY               OCCURS 1 TO 2000 TIMES
                                             DEPENDING ON NOTE-LENGTH.
                   07 NOTE-CHAR            PIC X.
           03 NOTE-LENGTH                  PIC 9(4).
           03 NOTE-REC-LENGTH              PIC 9(4).

But it was missing a statement.  Here's what worked:

       FD NOTES
               RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE
                   FROM 19 TO 2018 CHARACTERS
                   DEPENDING ON NOTE-REC-LENGTH.
       01 NOTES-REC.
          .......... etc. .......

If you don't include that clause, the system will write the max length
out for each record.  Give it a shot.  Of course I may have done it
wrong, but then I do most things wrong and somehow get them to work in
spite of it ;)

Elizabeth


In article <385a6113.0@news.dol.net>,
  "Lou de Freitas" <Luiz.deFreitas@dol.net> wrote:
> With a MF created executable, I am having a problem creating a
variable
> length file defined by the use of occurs x times depending on y
within the
> fields that define the 01 level of the record.  The problem is that
the
> records are created with the max size even though the occurs
depending on
> has a value less than the max.  I confirmed this by viewing the
created file
> in hex and confirmed that this is a problem.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Elizabeth,

I was interested in your example because it covers an area I've queried
before. My record has a max-table of 10 x 24. Under ideal conditions I
would like records to expand to the maximum or contract to what is
currently required. I think with what you've illustrated, you've set the
size for notes to be currently what you are outputting.

Question - do you ever read those notes in and increase/decrease their
size ? From some responses I got, it looks like it could be tricky. The
nearest I've gotten to-date is to use Micro Focus data compression. And
unless there is a concise technique, I really don't want to mess around 
playing suck-it-and-see and screw-up my datafiles in the process !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83e62q$i4k$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net> <83dvuj$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol E. Johnson <elizrj@my-deja.com> wrote:

:        FD NOTES
:        01 NOTES-REC.
:            03 NOTE-KEY.
:                05 NOTE-TYPE                PIC X.
:                05 NOTE-2-TYPE              PIC X(03).
:                05 NOTE-NUMBER              PIC X(10).
:            03 NOTE-FIELD.
:                05 NOTE-ARRAY               OCCURS 1 TO 2000 TIMES
:                                              DEPENDING ON NOTE-LENGTH.
:                    07 NOTE-CHAR            PIC X.
:            03 NOTE-LENGTH                  PIC 9(4).
:            03 NOTE-REC-LENGTH              PIC 9(4).

: But it was missing a statement.  Here's what worked:

:        FD NOTES
:                RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE
:                    FROM 19 TO 2018 CHARACTERS
:                    DEPENDING ON NOTE-REC-LENGTH.
:        01 NOTES-REC.
:           .......... etc. .......

How could that _possibly_ work ?

The length field follows the varying part.  It needs to know 
the length of the array/record before it can find where the
field is that tells it the length.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** E. Johnson <elizrj@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83qtb4$pro$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net> <83dvuj$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83e62q$i4k$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <DlC64.2151$M34.23809@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
In article <DlC64.2151$M34.23809@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net>,
  "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
> Richard Plinston wrote
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So glad you two informed me that this won't work.  I would have gone on
using it with no problems showing up for yet another 2 years.  God,
what would I have told my users?? Just because it LOOKS like it works,
and ACTS like it works, doesn't mean it really works so I have to take
it out of the code until it conforms to something other people say
should work.  WHEW!  Big relief for me.

Seriously tho, I use this in many places.  It holds various notes for
customers, vendors, orders, etc.  It is a dynamic file indexed to the
customer code, the vendor code, the order number, etc.  In 2 years, I
have had absolutely no problems with it ... unless you count the first
couple weeks of installation when I didn't include the varying phrase
and it grew to 6 meg.  After correcting that, the file remains at about
1 meg after 2 years.

There is some finagling (sp?) you have to do to get the notes into and
out of the array.  And since I use dialog, I use a panels call to move
the notes to and from the note field.  All in all it works pretty
simply with only about 13 lines of code.

Sheesh guys, I'm not an expert or anything.  I can't tell you cobol
standards and quote textbook examples of logistical code.  I can,
however, tell when something works or not.  Please give me a little
credit ... but not a lot cause I may get a swelled head and demand a
raise.

~Elizabeth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FKd84.19$ix6.1235@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net> <83dvuj$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83e62q$i4k$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <DlC64.2151$M34.23809@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net> <83qtb4$pro$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
At issue:
.
FD NOTES
       01 xxx.
           03 yyy.
               05 aaa                                PIC X.
               05 bbb                                PIC X(03).
               05 ccc                                 PIC X(10).
           03 ddd.
               05 NOTE-ARRAY               OCCURS 1 TO 2000 TIMES
                                             DEPENDING ON NOTE-LENGTH.
                   07 NOTE-CHAR            PIC X.
           03 NOTE-LENGTH                  PIC 9(4).
           03 NOTE-REC-LENGTH              PIC 9(4).


RECORD VARYING FROM integer-5 TO integer-6 DEPENDING ON data-name-1

"DEPENDING ON  - If this phrase is present, you must store in
data-name-1 the size of the record before any WRITE or REWRITE
operation on the file. After any successful READ operation,
data-name-1 contains the size of the record just read. Data-name-1
must be an unsigned integer data item defined in the Working-Storage
section."

I think I've found a way in which your code could work.

Since your compiler does not flag NOTE-LENGTH as being absent from
Working Storage (my compiler generates an E-Level diagnostic with the
above code), I assume your compiler is reserving space for maximum
record lengths (as if NOTE-ARRAY were defined in Working Storage).
If that is the case, you are not getting variable length records, they
just seem like it.

Whatever seems to 'work' is guaranteed to fail on a more conforming
compiler.

E. Johnson <elizrj@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:83qtb4$pro$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83ru12$26m$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net> <83dvuj$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83e62q$i4k$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <DlC64.2151$M34.23809@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net> <83qtb4$pro$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <FKd84.19$ix6.1235@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
One more time,
  PLEASE look at using the following type of code INSTEAD


  FD NOTES
        Record Varying in Size from 23 to 2022
           Depending on WS-File Size
      .
         01 xxx.
             03 yyy.
                  05 aaa                                PIC X.
                  05 bbb                                PIC X(03).
                  05 ccc                                 PIC X(10).
              03 ddd.
                  05 NOTE-ARRAY               OCCURS 1 TO 2000 TIMES
                                              DEPENDING ON NOTE-LENGTH.
                      07 NOTE-CHAR            PIC X.
              03 NOTE-LENGTH                PIC 9(4).
              03 NOTE-REC-LENGTH       PIC 9(4).

    ....

Working-Storage Section.
   ...
01  WS-File-Size Pic 9(04).

  ...

Then MOVE the length of each file to be written to WS-File-Size before doing
your WRITE statement.  After each READ WS-File-Size will contain the size of
the record that was read.

I have NOT changed the location of NOTE-LENGTH, but unless you really have a
system that can handle that, I *strongly* recommend that you put the "object
of the ODO" *before* the ODO itself - and not after it.
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

```
    With MF cobol, this code should work just fine so long as you avoid the
ODOSLIDE compiler directive.
Without this directive (the default), NOTE-LENGTH would be placed after a
FULL length array.

    With ODOSLIDE, this arrangement might be unusable.

    Sorry about delay - thought that Outlook would
send it automatically.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83u2fc$1e9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net> <83dvuj$4cu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83e62q$i4k$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <DlC64.2151$M34.23809@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net> <83qtb4$pro$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <83qtb4$pro$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  E. Johnson <elizrj@my-deja.com> wrote:
> > >
> > > How could that _possibly_ work ?
>
> So glad you two informed me that this won't work.  I would have gone
on
> using it with no problems showing up for yet another 2 years.  God,
> what would I have told my users?? Just because it LOOKS like it
works,
> and ACTS like it works, doesn't mean it really works so I have to
take
> it out of the code until it conforms to something other people say
> should work.  WHEW!  Big relief for me.

I may be instructive (for you) to examine what of this code _does_ work
and what does _not_ work.

You have a varying record containing a varying array followed by the
array size and then the record size.

The compiler should object to this, the fact that it does not does not
mean that the code is correct, only that _this_ compiler doesn't care.

The program will be treating the twoi length fields as if they were in
Working-Storage.  That is the record will be written out to the length
specified, and when the record is read the _system_ will be setting the
length read.  The two lengths will not, however, be written out as part
of the actual record.  These two lenths are in character positions
2000+ of the record.  When a record is written (assuming lengths are
set expecting the fields to be written) then the record will contain
the initial data, the text in the array and the next 8 bytes of the
array and _not_ the lengths.

When the record is read back in the record length will be set as for
any variable record even if this length was in (as it is supposed to
be) Working-Storage.  The text array length will _not_ be set as it
is outside the record size (except with a 2022 sized record).  You are
probably recalculating the array size from the record size after each
read and never knew why this was often wrong.

So what is _not_ working is the array length is not being written, the
record length is not being written (but is recreated), you are wasting
8 bytes on every record and are having to recalulate the array length.

You are also risking a new compiler forcing you to recode and
restructure the file.

>
> Sheesh guys, I'm not an expert or anything.  I can't tell you cobol
> standards and quote textbook examples of logistical code.

I had noticed.

>  I can,
> however, tell when something works or not.

Obviously not.  have you actually looked at the data file itself and
noticed the last 8 bytes of every record is rubbish left over from a
previous record ?


> Please give me a little
> credit ... but not a lot cause I may get a swelled head and demand a
> raise.

I don't think that you risk that.  You appear to program by 'trial and
error', with the emphasis on the 'error'.  This is a poor approach that
is expensive and time consuming.  What is even more worrying is that
you seem to think that the code is actually 'working' and have no idea
what parts are or are not or why.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83drsd$sod$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net>`

```
Rather than RECORD CONTAINS, use the (new with '85 Standard) RECORD VARYING
IN SIZE DEPENDING ON option in your FD.  It will require you to put the
size-to-be-written into a working-storage field, but should (I think) solve
your problem.
```

```
This var file has only one occurs depending on sections in the record and it
always gets greated with the max size of 560

          SELECT NAME-ADDR-OUTFILE        ASSIGN TO
          'vfcreat2.dat'
                               ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVVARON.
       FILE SECTION.

       FD  NAME-ADDR-OUTFILE
           RECORDING MODE IS V
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM 144 TO 560
           DATA RECORD IS VAR-LOAD.
       01 VAR-LOAD.
          02 VAR-INPUT-REC.
             03 VAR-SERIAL-NBR                   PIC 9(9).
             03 VAR-DATA-FOR-COUNTRY             PIC X(3).
             03 VAR-LANGUAGE                     PIC 9(3).
             03 VAR-INSTITUTION                  PIC X(4).
             03 VAR-LOCATION-NO                  PIC X(12).
             03 VAR-ACCT-KEY.
                04 VAR-PROD-CODE                 PIC X(5).
                04 VAR-PLAN-CODE                 PIC X(5).
                04 VAR-ACCT-NBR                  PIC X(30).
             03 VAR-CIF-KEY-DSPLY                PIC X(20).
             03 VAR-CIF-KEY-BINARY               PIC X(20).
             03 VAR-SSEID-NBR                    PIC 9(15).
             03 VAR-SSEID-XREF-LINE              PIC 9(2).
             03 VAR-DATE-PROCESSED.
                04 VAR-DATE-PROC-YYYY            PIC 9(4).
                04 VAR-DATE-PROC-MM              PIC 9(2).
                04 VAR-DATE-PROC-DD              PIC 9(2).
             03 VAR-CHANGE-OR-DELETE-SW          PIC X.
                88 VAR-IS-A-CHANGE                       VALUE 'C'.
                88 VAR-IS-A-DELETE                       VALUE 'D'.
                88 VAR-IS-A-ADD                          VALUE 'A'.
                88 VAR-IS-A-SCRUB                        VALUE 'S'.
             03 VAR-RECTYPE                      PIC X.
             03 VAR-NBR-LINES-IN                 PIC 9(2).
             03 VAR-NBR-LINES-OUT                PIC 9(2).
             03 VAR-LINES.
                04 VAR-NAME-ADDR-INFO
                   OCCURS 0 TO 8 TIMES
                   DEPENDING ON VAR-NBR-LINES-IN.
                   05 VAR-NAME-ADDR.
                      06 VAR-1CHAR               PIC X.
                      06 VAR-RMNG                PIC X(49).
                   05 VAR-LINE-CODE              PIC X.
                   05 VAR-SEX-CODE               PIC X.
             03 VAR-CRLF                        PIC X(2).

This var file has two occurs depending on sections and it always gets
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mf Variable File Formats

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83fo9i$e0r$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<385a6113.0@news.dol.net> <XqD64.9436$o97.85846@news3.mia> <385b7392.0@news.dol.net>`

```
Having  VAR-CRLF at the end of the record descriptions
makes the length of these records fixed due to the ANS
rules for ODO. (But see below for using ODOSLIDE.)

For the first record description, decrease the minimum and
maximum sizes by 2 and eliminate VAR-CRLF and the
record will be variable.

For the second record description, do the same as above;
but note that the record will be variable based upon the
second ODO, the first ODO will always have 8 slots
allocated.

If the proper precautions have been taken for moving data
into the both records, and every other ODO in the programs
that use these files, you could use the ODOSLIDE compiler
directive. This directive causes ODO to behave as in older
IBM mainframe compilers.

Also get rid of the RECORDING MODE IS V. RECORD IS
VARYING is the ANS 85 replacement for RECORDING MODE.
------------------
Rick Smith

it
>always gets greated with the max size of 560
```

<a id="t-bbca65d5e5"></a>
## Thread: Who has had Y2K problems yet? — 14 this month / 26 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Who has had Y2K problems yet?

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
It's Friday 12/17/99 (or is that 12/17/1999?) and today I spent half my day
remediating 4 production programs that were apparently 'missed', installing
the Standard Sliding Window.  The jobs were not canceling, but the users
started calling about weird reports.

I'm thinking that when Jan 1st rolls around, things won't crash much at all.
What will probably happen is a bunch of stuff gets screwed up and nobody
will know until the users start calling.

I think we will all be busy for a long time after the new year.

Jeff
Giant Food Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who has had Y2K problems yet?

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#kFnMfRS$GA.292@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
At the turn of 1998 to 1999, my last firm experinced 6 date related abends
on the mainframe.  The common date program that calculated system run-dates
accidentally calculated either a day early on the 31st of December, or a day
late on the 3rd and 4th of January, for reasons still unknown.  Since the
problem stopped occuring, no one investigated.  As all data from systems
from that firm have now been transitioned to another firm's systems, that
particular problem won't occur again.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who has had Y2K problems yet?

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70E6DDB02DC50756.A3500C6E514DCAFF.FE6DCC6C83B908AE@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
We had a small number of bugs reported after installing our Y2K
remediated software back in May.  Nothing serious, and all easily
fixed.  I am sure that other bugs (hopefully just as minor) will surface
after year-end.

However, our very first Y2K crisis came in May of 1998.  Our General
Ledger system uses a calendar file to keep track of fiscal period dates
for each of our companies, all of which have fiscal years that do not
start in January.   The financial calendar system knows about "previous"
"current" and "next" fiscal years.  In May 1998, one company was rolling
into the next year which meant that the "current" year became May '98 -
April '99 and NEXT year would become May '99 - April 2000.  Bang!  The
software couldn't cope.

I am embarassed to admit this caught us completely by surprise.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who has had Y2K problems yet?

- **From:** bberman@netbox.com (B Berman)
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385c3d7d.12756114@news.ntplx.net>`
- **References:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Dec 1999 21:03:40 -0500, "Jeff Baynard"
<union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

>It's Friday 12/17/99 (or is that 12/17/1999?) and today I spent half my day
>remediating 4 production programs that were apparently 'missed', installing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>will know until the users start calling.

I started seeing problems in the first week of December, in one system
that I inherited this past summer (guaranteed compliant they told me).

One customer, who transmits orders to us from Europe, started
complaining about wierd Specified-Ship-Dates on his Order Review
screen, for orders with ship-dates in January 2000.  Upon
investigating, I found that the order is transmitted with a ship-date
in format DDMMY (single-digit year).  The program that processed the
order converted the date properly and stored it in the database OK,
but the inquiry program could not handle the conversion back to YY.
Ironically, the problem will go away once we roll into January.

The program compared the ship-Y to the current year, and if less,
added 1 to the current decade (if 0 < 9, add 1 to decade(9) allowing
truncation).  Unfortunately, this only worked for the first
transaction in the batch, as the current decade was not reinitialized
in working-storage for subsequent transactions.  The first order
showed a ship-YY of 00 on the screen, the second 10, the third 20,
etc.

Because of the Y2k "code freeze" that has been in effect since Nov. 1,
I am not allowed to fix it until sometime in late January, when the
problem will have gone away until the next decade change.  I can only
field calls from the user, and verify that the order database is OK.
I wonder what they did 10 years ago ?  Did anyone even notice it then
?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who has had Y2K problems yet?

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83n0l6$5l2$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com> <385E5A3C.213433FD@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:385E5A3C.213433FD@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> We had lots of programmer hours spent doing Y2K work instead of more fun
> stuff.  Does that qualify as a problem?

We had a real live Y2K problem on Friday (Dec 17).  The kicker is the
program appeared, at a glance, to be Y2K compliant.  It had date windowing
and everything!  I didn't personally work on it, but here's my
understanding...

We bill customers once a year for a certain item.  We need to notify them in
advance that they will be charged.  (20 days or so prior, I'm not sure on
the exact number.)  So a program goes through each account, checking for a
bill date.  If it's 20 days before that date it generates an entry in NACHA
format (where the day is YYMMDD).  Notices are created on the run date, and
the file itself is intended to be warehoused until the actual bill date.
Program 2 reads the warehouse file daily, and if the date of the entry is
equal to or earlier than today then that entry is sent on to the posting
programs to actually charge the customer.  Here follows some of the code:

ACCEPT CURRENT-YYMMDD FROM DATE.
IF CURRENT-YY > 90
    MOVE 19 TO CURRENT-CC
                            BILL-DATE-CC
ELSE
    MOVE 20 TO CURRENT-CC
                            BILL-DATE-CC.

* After reading a NACHA record
MOVE NACHA-DATE TO BILL-DATE-YYMMDD

IF BILL-DATE <= CURRENT-DATE
   ...write out the NACHA record to the posting file

So what's the problem?  Well, the bill date for this run is 000103
(1/3/2000).  But, because the windowing was based on the *current* date and
not the *bill* date we ended up with BILL-DATE being 19000103.

Yikes!

A simple fix to the program, of course, but we had to go and reverse all the
entries!

(Name of business removed to protect the guilty!)
```

```
I first ran into the Y2K  problem back in 1982.  Not much was done except
that all new date fields had a full year value instead of the two digit
dates.  The existing date fields were not touched.  Too much of a hassle or
cost to fix at the time.  Famous last words repeated over and over again at
work and all over the world.

Tim
```

#### ↳ Re: Who has had Y2K problems yet?

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83kaov$789$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
In article <83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com>,
  "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:
> It's Friday 12/17/99 (or is that 12/17/1999?) and today I spent half
my day
> remediating 4 production programs that were apparently 'missed',
installing
> the Standard Sliding Window.  The jobs were not canceling, but the
users
> started calling about weird reports.
>
> I'm thinking that when Jan 1st rolls around, things won't crash much
at all.
> What will probably happen is a bunch of stuff gets screwed up and
nobody
> will know until the users start calling.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

IMHO, the ONLY way to EVER store dates is in a Y2K-Friendly Julian-
Format. Julian-Dates handle year and century turnovers much better that
Gregorian (unless the format is CCYYMMDD). Plus, date calculations in
general as much simpler. When stored as packed-decimal, only 4-bytes
are used as opposed to their Gregorian counterpart, which requires 5-
bytes no matter how you slice it (unless you play games with the data).

Standardized Julian to Gregorian routines are plentiful. If shops that
had taken on Y2K decided to pursue and implement Julian as their
standard format, this silly idea of sliding-century windows (band-aid)
would not have to be re-visited.

However, by the time that rolls around, we'll all be dust in the wind
and someone will be bringing up the 22nd-Century date-dilemma :-)

Just my .02 cents.

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who has had Y2K problems yet?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8u694.14818$Dj6.172502@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com> <83kaov$789$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
WOB Consulting wrote:
>
>IMHO, the ONLY way to EVER store dates is in a Y2K-Friendly Julian-
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>bytes no matter how you slice it (unless you play games with the data).

The only problem with this is that you display or print dates many
times more often than you calculate with them.  Most dates are never
used in calculations.  If you store dates and display dates in the
same format (ccyymmdd), you never have to convert.  Even if you
display and input dates ac MMDDCCYY, that conversion is much simpler
than to/from Julian format.  But each to his own. :-)
```

<a id="t-118d64834f"></a>
## Thread: Maximum size of tables was Re: Any Suggestions? (Extreme Programming) — 1 msgs

### Re: Maximum size of tables was Re: Any Suggestions? (Extreme Programming)

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethis@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mk3l5s8507qbofepjp5v79rt21hklhprp6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<82ja74$5ej$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <Sva34.2737$Ze7.107325@news3.mia> <82jcc6$7aj$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <3853c3f1.43035700@news1.attglobal.net> <3853F9A2.ADBA8CA6@home.com> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38562FE6.754CB3FE@istar.ca>`

```
Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>Actually with modern day virtual memory systems, you may only be
>wasting 4 - 8K if you don't hit the maximum occurs value.  If the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>doing a SEARCH ALL.

At least on IBM mainframe applications the is really no reason to code
OCCURS DEPENDING ON in any working storage table. Space will have to
be allocated off of a BL cell for the maximum possible storage size of
the table no matter what. Remember that when you define a variable
table, by definition the space is dynamic and has to becapable of
holding the maximum number of entries regardless of what's actually
used.

Where I used to work, ODO's were used a lot in files to 'save' space
with repeated data.  Even though it usually had only minimal effect
due to poor knowledge by the developers about how their data would
actually look in the fiels after a couple of years of use.  (a
favorite example of mine  was two years of payment history with a
average number of entries of about 18 out of a max of 24 and a
standard deviation of about 2). This kind of file also caused cpu
usage by the applications to skyrocket if the data was referenced in
the buffer because of the offset recalculations on every reference of
data elements. It was very difficult to pursuade programmers to move
the data to working storage definitions that did not have the ODO's in
the tables.  When we did, the resource reductions in some programs was
spectacular. 

I would be dubious of any significant savings when using SEARCH ALL.
This format of the SEARCH verb uses a binary search rather than
sequential and except in extreme cases the number of probes into the
table to find an element should not be that great.
                         
                         		Scott Peterson


If you're born again, do you 
have two bellybuttons?
```

## Thread: object cobol — 16 msgs

### object cobol

- **From:** "Philip Limitone Limitone" <p.limitone@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A0U64.3186$_f.21561@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
hello group,

i am a student and we just finsihed a unit in my COBOL class having to
with object oriented COBOL.
my professor just glanced over the unit since it was nearing the end of
the semester, but my question is:

is object cobol prevalent in the "real" world? or, are most programs
written the old fashioned way?

just wondering....

thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: object cobol

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O5t5gnbS$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<A0U64.3186$_f.21561@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
We are currently converting a text-based application to a Windows
application and in doing so we are having to reengineer/rewrite large
portions of the code.  Our code was started 17 years ago back when the "old
fashioned" way was the only way so it has no object oriented code.  In our
new code we have tried to take advantage of the OO technology whenever
possible.  I can tell you that it is difficult to get into a mindset where
you think to use objects first, but once you do we have found it to be a
most valuable tool.  I think a lot of programmers are starting to use it,
but I wouldn't view a lack of knowledge of OO COBOL as a deficiency in your
education.

bill


Philip Limitone Limitone wrote in message ...
>hello group,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: object cobol

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385ce53e.398877582@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<A0U64.3186$_f.21561@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Since no two vendors support the proposed COBOL standard for OO, it is
completely and utterly non portable between compilers at present.
Mainly because of that, and not because of any deficiency in OO or
COBOL, it has been slow to be adopted.  Cautious optimism abounds.
However, you will find many here who have adopted it within the
confines of their particular compiler of choice, who are using it for
production code.
```

```
applying oo concepts at the system design level.  A system that is 
designed for re-usable components results in much smaller cobol programs, 
and more subroutines than usual, so they can be re-used by the next 
potential business application.  The objects just happen to be coded in 
non-oo cobol!
```
