###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12c81d48b4b063c098968f@news.transport.com>`

```
Pete, a question...
After several failed OO projects at several different clients, I finally
recognized a pattern to the problems - that of managing the inventory of
objects.  In one particularly noxious example, an application with a "budget"
of some 3000 objects was killed when management found out that over 15000 had
been created.  There were more than 20 for the customer name field.  It
appeared that it was easier for most of the programmers to write their own
object than find the one (or several!) already created.  How is your shop
handling this problem?  Does it involve whips, chains, and electrical devices
for the transgressors?
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2A584.57987$eh2.1181011@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12c81d48b4b063c098968f@news.transport.com> <19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
Does your project plan recognize the object dependencies in developing a
program? One of my pet peeves is shops that continue to plan in a 'job shop'
mentality when they really need to think about 'software manufacturing'
including critical path analysis. Often planning consists of identifying
individual programs and then assigning them to the worker bees to be
written. If management doesn't know how to manage a project with a high
degree of interdependency then you cannot blame the worker bees for doing
whatever is necessary to deliver their piece.

Once the delivery date starts slipping there is pressure to move that
completion estimate to 100%. Try explaining to some middle manager why 90%
of the programs are at 95% complete pending completion of a single object
that somebody else is writing. They do not want to hear it. Management needs
to be taught a new way to plan deliverables.

The next issue is indexing all those objects. If a programmer perceives that
it would take them longer to find an object that suits their purpose than
writing a new object, they will write a new object. Trying to figure out
which object to use, or even if an object can be used in a given situation
is a lot like playing 'Jeopardy!'. You've got an answer but you have to come
up with the question, or context, in which that answer applies. It would
help if documentation was extensible so that every time an object is used,
the user provided a brief description of the context in which it was used.
That way you could index the question rather than the answer. If you can't
find it, you won't use it.

So, you've found the (almost) perfect object. In the real world that object
is only perfect for the set of conditions that it was developed to handle.
Making it generic takes effort. Designing the 'perfect' object is an
exercise in diminishing returns. Assume that you're going to start with an
imperfect solution and have to adapt as development proceeds. So now you've
got a programmer trying to complete an assignment using unstable components.
That programmer needs to know that any problems that are identified will be
resolved in a timely manner. Any lack of confidence or unacceptible delay
will result in the developer solving their own problem by growing a new
object. A successful project needs a respected technical architect.

Review programmer's work early and often. It's time-consuming but it's a
valuable learning tool for everybody. The architecture is improved by the
feedback, the programmers learn about how the objects were intended to be
used. Object programming is a team exercise. Everybody depends on everybody
else. One project I worked on involved one-on-one startup sessions between
the keepers of the objects and the programmer for each program. They
identified the most logical starting point, objects to handle particular
data domains, common business processes, etc.

Plan to maximize reuse. Leverage commonality whereever possible. If the
analysts aren't working from the same set of program archetypes then the
programmer's will be left to sort out the mess. The best object library in
the world is of little use if it cannot be applied to the problem, as
defined by the analysts. Develop program archetypes, data domains, and
common processes before ever partitioning the work between analysts.
Establish confidence that the archetypes and object library satisfy the end
users, analysts, and data gods by implementing a small part of the system
using them. Make the analysts stick to the plan. If they are allowed to
introduce their own variations then the programmers may be forced to
introduce new objects to deal with those variations.


"Steve Newton" <stevenln@aol.comnospam> wrote in message
news:19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com...
> Pete, a question...
> After several failed OO projects at several different clients, I finally
> recognized a pattern to the problems - that of managing the inventory of
> objects.  In one particularly noxious example, an application with a
"budget"
> of some 3000 objects was killed when management found out that over 15000
had
> been created.  There were more than 20 for the customer name field.  It
> appeared that it was easier for most of the programmers to write their own
> object than find the one (or several!) already created.  How is your shop
> handling this problem?  Does it involve whips, chains, and electrical
devices
> for the transgressors?
> Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
> Still the best
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12caa00fd6498a43989694@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12c81d48b4b063c098968f@news.transport.com> <19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com>, 
stevenln@aol.comnospam says...
> Pete, a question...
> After several failed OO projects at several different clients, I finally
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Our programming unit that is making the best use of oo objects has a 
manager involved that uses whips and chains!  This individual 
monitors/reviews new code from his team with a passion, and there are 
frequent whippings with wet noodles when people don't re-use.

Without someone who loves this oo stuff taking the reigns like this, I 
wouldn't give it much of a chance.

And actually, I am concerned about what will happen to the quality of 
their pool of software once this individual is gone.  I expect everyone 
would fall back into the quick-and-dirty methods of re-creating the wheel 
in his absence, because only he has an instantly-recallable catalog of 
available objects in his head.  Without an instant-recall guru guy 
around, people will find that searching for appropriate objects will 
actually slow their project down.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38610B80.C1A3BE50@home.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12c81d48b4b063c098968f@news.transport.com> <19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```


Steve Newton wrote:
> 
> Pete, a question...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Still the best

I found your comments intriguing. Working as a loner, with no peer
support I've developed OO techniques - doing it 'My way'. Now let me
play heretic - I get the impression from various sources that I've read
that OO is a 'pure science, the methodology 'demanding' that the code be
written in a specific way to obey the rules. (This is an area Ken Foskey
and I are going to have 'fun' with - where we are communicating
privately).

Your 1,500 - 3,000 objects makes me shudder, but I can understand how
this happens, because everything gets split down to the nth degree
creating sub-objects. To my mind this is theory which has gone nuts.
What I've written so far seems to work satisfactorily - and frankly
until someone can prove that my approach will trip me up at some future
date, then I am quite happy to continue 'bending the rules' if
necessary. (A corollary, Dijkstra and 'Structured' - DON'T use GO TO -
some very proficient programmers still use GO TO in a very tight loop).

Regardless of what you are doing, it starts at the DB or flat-file
system. OO in no way negates your need to consider the design of file
systems to obtain the desired hieracial structure of data. Get that
right and OO becomes a bonus tool.

In his message, Oscar T. Grouch, touched on reusability - without doubt
a very powerful tool. Although concentrating on getting the current
application out to the user, as I'm coding, I've mentally noted
repetition of areas of code - and these are 'natural' candidates to be
included in generic classes. As an example, mulling away in the back of
my mind, and Ken Foskey made an observation to me about it - Creating
Dialogs using NetExpress OO/GUIs and the M/F Dialog Editor - there
definitely is a case for writing a generic class 'MakeDialog', firstly
identifying the ID of the specific dialog and then passing its creation
parameters to 'MakeDialog', regardless of whether the control is an
entry-field. pushbutton, listbox etc... How generic it can become, I'll
have to find out my experimentation.

Encapsulation - now there's an over-used word; read the texts, 'You
don't need to know what a method is doing, it is hidden'. Like me,
anybody reading that for the first time thinks, "How the hell can you
use it, if you don't know what it's doing ?". Agreed you don't need the
method's code but as an absolute minimum you must have a list of sending
and receiving parameters. Therefore, in a large installation you have to
decide on one of two approaches :-

a) Constantly update a reference list of methods with parameters
b) Access to the source code of classes by all programmers

We all know about documentation - (a) above will probably fall flat on
its face - not regularly being updated. As regards (b) - possible
problems with confidentiality, security.

Back to your 1,500 - 3,000 objects - break the rules <G> As a last
observation - filing systems haven't really changed since I
designed/used manual systems nearly some 50 years ago in the RAF - all
we have done is automate them.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386AA679.73F7D410@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12c81d48b4b063c098968f@news.transport.com> <19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38610B80.C1A3BE50@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> In his message, Oscar T. Grouch, touched on reusability - without doubt
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> have to find out my experimentation.

Generic routines are impossible to build.  Write a few and then pull
the generic structure out.  Do the simplest thing that can possibly
work and refactor (rewrite working code) until it is better code.

Someone once said that I cannot see the consistent part until I have
written it three times.  This is true, every generic component I have
created was flawed in some way.

> Encapsulation - now there's an over-used word; read the texts, 'You
> don't need to know what a method is doing, it is hidden'. Like me,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> a) Constantly update a reference list of methods with parameters

There must be a copybook of the object signature that you include in
all programs that use it.  This signature should not contain the code
as well,  Optimal signatures do not contain any private information
(working storage),  This is an 'interesting' compiler design issue.

> b) Access to the source code of classes by all programmers

Open source, Great idea.  100% agree.

All object will be supported by a comprehensive testing framework that
exercises all functionality.  If the class must be modified ("Oscar T.
Grouch" listening) it can be 'guaranteed' that the revised class will
not break the existing programs.  (If the guarantee is broken then
upgrade your test cases).

The class might be close but not perfect.  Inherit the functionality,
and modify the imperfections.  In a method has some part of the
functions you need break the method into two and reuse the part you
need.

> We all know about documentation - (a) above will probably fall flat on
> its face - not regularly being updated. As regards (b) - possible
> problems with confidentiality, security.

Document by writing very clear code firstly.  Document a high level
overview of the system.  Pass around the information on the system as
much as possible by sharing the work on the system around.  One of the
nemesis of today's programming is code ownership.  I suffer from this
badly, I need to develop the code and no matter what hand it on to
others to improve.

> Back to your 1,500 - 3,000 objects - break the rules <G> As a last
> observation - filing systems haven't really changed since I
> designed/used manual systems nearly some 50 years ago in the RAF - all
> we have done is automate them.

When the duplicate functions are located they should be removed. 
Entropy has set in, clean up or you system will die!

Oscar also wrote:

> Review programmer's work early and often. It's time-consuming but it's a
> valuable learning tool for everybody. The architecture is improved by the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> data domains, common business processes, etc.

Consider pair programming.

For a new and fresh look at old problems read up in
http://www.XProgramming.com


Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** E. Johnson <elizrj@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83r153$t0b$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12c81d48b4b063c098968f@news.transport.com> <19991222010502.23435.00001242@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
All of our new code is done like this.  We don't use OO per se, but we
build callable modules.  For example, we have module that we can call
to come back with a single product code or a range of product codes.
That module is then responsible for all lookups, validations, adds (if
allowed by a flag sent in with the call), ranges or single picks
(again, a flag) etc.

However, like you say, it's hard to keep track of what modules you've
built and how to use them.  To this end, we have gone to great efforts
to document the calls, what to send in, what comes out, etc.  They've
proven to be very very useful as we build the new system.  Having one
module to change/correct/maintain has eased the transition.  The added
benefit is the consistancy to our end user.

~Elizabeth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: object cobol

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991220015659.29404.00000045@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<A0U64.3186$_f.21561@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Since you are a student, your professor may not have told you that you will
spend most of your work time working on "old fashioned" COBOL, since that is
the current inventory.  Contrary to what I was taught 15 years ago, most
program life-cycles are counted in decades, not years.  It is simply not
economic for most business users to re-write the entire portfolio every 7 to 10
years.  Thus, you will, if my experience is any guide, find yourself working on
code that was written in  the 80's or even the 70's.

However, if your future employer is going to use the new technologies, like
tying the "legacy applications" to the Internet, or make extensive use of EDI
in most of the current implementations, you will need to know some OO, simply
to write the interfaces, and maintain the connections.  Press your instructors
next semester to spend some time on it - or, go out and find it yourself (what
most of us in the profession have to do on a ongoing basis).  There are lots of
good resources out there, I like Digital Think (www.digitalthink.com) but there
are many more that do a good job of providing targeted instruction.
Good Luck
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

---

<a id="t-f99105cdff"></a>
## Thread: COBOL II with COBOL 370 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: COBOL II with COBOL 370

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83gj1a$824$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<83evor$nnl$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Yes,
  If you mean a user-written COBOL/370 (which is actually obsolete and
replaced by IBM COBOL for MVS & VM - or IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM) program
which uses intrinsic functions, then you can statically call such
user-written programs from VS COBOL II.

HOWEVER, if your VS COBOL II program is compiled with the NORES compiler
option, you will need to use the LE library when you link-edit it.  If it is
compiled with RES, you shouldn't have any serious problem. (Read the COBOL
for this-and-that Migration Guide for general rules on mixing VS COBOL II and
COBOL for this-and-that routines.)

If you mean one of the LE routines (for doing "intrinsic functions") then the
answer is that you cannot call them (statically or dynamically) from a VS
COBOL II program.  Only the date/time routines can be directly called - and
the CALL must be dynamic - from a VS COBOL II program.

(CC'ing the reply to comp.lang.cobol - which is better than alt.cobol for
this type of question.)
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
> [among much else...]
>One of the things in C/C++ and many other languages that contribute most
>to broken code and horrible debugging problems is the use of pointers.


When I first read your message, I realized I had entered the realm of one of
my hot buttons and decided to let that button cool down for a few days
before responding.

And a good thing I did.

As subsequent posters have stated, "pointers" and "dynamic memory
allocation" are not, of and by themselves, problems; rather, it is the
improper or sloppy use of these tools which cause problems - regardless of
language platform (C/C++ mentioned as an example). My real point is, to
paraphrase Churchill's requests to the US for military aid at the onset of
the Second World War, is, "Give us the tools and we'll finish the job."

I just don't see any problem ADDING capabilities to a programming language;
I consider it highly unlikely dynamic allocation or enhanced pointer usage
would supplant 'traditional' COBOL programming techniques. But for those
tough lies behind a tree, it would be nice to have in the bag a tree-iron
and not be forced to use a three-iron.

Michael Mattias
Racine WI USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Any Suggestions? (Extreme Programming)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00

- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <822ah5$1gc$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ex14.6774$Ic5.79616@news3.mia> <826dpi$fpk$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <3846C4A5.C1A66591@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <826slb$peq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847487c.537138075@news1.attglobal.net> <827r49$tb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847D524.4FE1ADBA@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <829ct3$cuo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3848725F.D1A0FA7C@att.net> <38488E05.35632487@home.com> <3849017F.B8D940BF@zip.com.au> <384CA162.C7B5367D@home.com> <82ja74$5ej$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <Sva34.2737$Ze7.107325@news3.mia> <82jcc6$7aj$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <3853c3f1.43035700@news1.attglobal.net> <3853F9A2.ADBA8CA6@home.com> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <WdM64.1146$_f.3759@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> [among much else...]
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the Second World War, is, "Give us the tools and we'll finish the job."


Okay, are you saying then that C/C++ programs are much more 'bug prone'
and difficult to debug because C/C++ programmers are less capable?  If
you are, quickly put on your asbestos pants. ;-)  But the disparity does
exist.  Since both C/C++ and COBOL programs are frequently written in
the same environments, that disparity is caused by either the C/C++
language being more bug prone, or C/C++ programmers being more bug
prone, or both.  Take your pick, I won't argue with either. ;-)

The fact is that you are *partially* correct in your reasoning.  What
you are not taking into account is that not every feature that can be
implemented in a language is equally easy to learn and use correctly.
You can not claim, for example, that C/C++ programmers never make the
mistake of typing or reading '=' for '==', and vice-versa.  This
confusion simply does not exist in COBOL, because any place you can
use '=', a '==' would be a syntax error, and vice versa.  Two of the
subtlest, arcane and difficult to master features of C are pointers,
indirection, and all the subtle variations of their possible uses.
Anyone who says they mastered this with no difficulty, that they do
not make errors using it, is not being honest.  Up to now COBOL has
avoided this completely.  Adding these features will provide similar
opportunities in COBOL, and it is not logically defensible to insist
otherwise.  The question is not 'if it will', but 'is it worth it'.
This is a matter of opinion, but considering where COBOL works best,
I don't think so.  Leave those features to languages frequently used
for tasks for which they are necessary.  A Big Mistake for any tool
is to try and make it a 'be-all, end-all' solution for every problem.
A frequent error being made these days is 'over enhancement'.  It
only complicates use of the tool and makes it less convenient and
reliable for tasks it is best suited for.  Since COBOL is the very
best, most economical and reliable tool we have for core business
applications, why compromise COBOL's strengths by adding bug-prone,
little needed features, when there are other languages available for
the tasks that need them?  My $.02. :-)
```

- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <3ex14.6774$Ic5.79616@news3.mia> <826dpi$fpk$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <3846C4A5.C1A66591@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <826slb$peq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847487c.537138075@news1.attglobal.net> <827r49$tb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847D524.4FE1ADBA@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <829ct3$cuo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3848725F.D1A0FA7C@att.net> <38488E05.35632487@home.com> <3849017F.B8D940BF@zip.com.au> <384CA162.C7B5367D@home.com> <82ja74$5ej$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <Sva34.2737$Ze7.107325@news3.mia> <82jcc6$7aj$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <3853c3f1.43035700@news1.attglobal.net> <3853F9A2.ADBA8CA6@home.com> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <WdM64.1146$_f.3759@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <nT874.11689$DC6.169673@news2.mia>`

```
<snippage of much PROs and CONs on POINTERS and DYNAMIC MEMORY allocation>

Judson,
  Are you aware that many (most?) COBOL's that I know of have supported USAGE
POINTER for over 10 years now - and some are close to that on USAGE
PROCEDURE-POINTER.  Add to that the fact that CICS (with COBOL on IBM
mainframes) has required you to use POINTERS (previously BLL-cells, but that
is what they really were) since LONG before that?

Yes, some of those programs have bugs - but I don't think any more or less
than "similar" applications written using other techniques. Personally, I
would guess that there are more errors (at least in the development stage)
using COBOL STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT than there are with USAGE POINTER,
SET ADDRESS OF, EXEC CICS GETMAIN, etc.

I think that the bottom-line is that "more complex" language features require
better training, more testing, and maintenance by those who UNDERSTAND the
same features as those who coded them in the first place.

Personally, I am HAPPY that these are being added to the Standard so that
"portable" coding techniques can be used - thereby INCREASING the chance of
proper maintenance.  I also, just don't see how anyone can want COBOL be
"eliminated" as a viable language in today's world of multi-language APIs -
which all (or almost all) seem to assume that your programming language has
these features.
```

- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <826dpi$fpk$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <3846C4A5.C1A66591@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <826slb$peq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847487c.537138075@news1.attglobal.net> <827r49$tb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847D524.4FE1ADBA@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <829ct3$cuo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3848725F.D1A0FA7C@att.net> <38488E05.35632487@home.com> <3849017F.B8D940BF@zip.com.au> <384CA162.C7B5367D@home.com> <82ja74$5ej$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <Sva34.2737$Ze7.107325@news3.mia> <82jcc6$7aj$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <3853c3f1.43035700@news1.attglobal.net> <3853F9A2.ADBA8CA6@home.com> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <WdM64.1146$_f.3759@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <nT874.11689$DC6.169673@news2.mia> <83jfca$g16$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>Judson,
>  Are you aware that many (most?) COBOL's that I know of have supported
>USAGE POINTER for over 10 years now ...?


Yes, mainly from posts in this newsgroup.  But I have little information
on how wide the usage is, or how successful.

>Yes, some of those programs have bugs - but I don't think any more or less
>than "similar" applications written using other techniques. Personally, I
>would guess that there are more errors (at least in the development stage)
>using COBOL STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT than there are with USAGE POINTER,
>SET ADDRESS OF, EXEC CICS GETMAIN, etc.


Without actual data, that is hard to determine.  I suggest that the
manner of implementation (syntax and semantics) has a great deal to
do with the difficulty.

>I think that the bottom-line is that "more complex" language features require
>better training, more testing, and maintenance by those who UNDERSTAND the
>same features as those who coded them in the first place.


Bill, it is a well established and easily demonstrated fact that the
more complex the operation, the more likely an error, other factors
being equal.  We humans are simply built that way.  In fact, even a
machine is more likely to make an error on a long operation than a
short one.  That same better training and testing would provide
similar improvements in quality without the extra features.  All the
training would cost a great deal of money and time.  If that extra
cost is necessary to achieve parity in quality with current (COBOL 85)
methods, then that is a liability.  You and I come down on different
sides because we view the issue with different perspectives on the
benefits vs costs. :-)
```

```
<385f0501.84403950@news1.attglobal.net>...
> On Mon, 20 Dec 1999 15:55:48 -0600, "Judson McClendon"

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> API.  

CICS programmers have been using pointers for a long time.  Though it were
only called BLL cells before, and were moved around in PIC 9(8) COMP
fields.  You had to remember to use SERVICE RELOAD before referencing the
structure again.  To the traditional batch programmer it was a mystery, to
those with assembler (or PL/I, or C) background it was a piece of cake.  VS
COBOL II was a relief.

Gunnar.
```

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
  <snip>
> >
> >CICS programmers have been using pointers for a long time.  Though it were
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >those with assembler (or PL/I, or C) background it was a piece of cake.
VS
> >COBOL II was a relief.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

What COBOL product have you used (OS/VS COBOL - or a newer one)?  If it is
anything POST-OS/VS, then you are correct that you haven't used (needed to
use) BLL-cells.  If you ever used OS/VS COBOL and EVER used anything in
LINKAGE SECTION, then you DID use BLL-cells, whether you knew about it or
not.

Of course, if you used CICS on Non-S/370-S/390 environments (OS/2, Windows,
HP, etc) then you wouldn't have used them there either - but MVS (OS/390) and
kVSE did use them.
```

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in article
<38622de8.291514320@news.freewwweb.com>...
> Maybe I am the exception - I am a CICS programmer, and I have never
> had occasion (in 15 years) to use BLL cells.  I started on 1.5 and
> have worked through 4.1.

OK, I was referring to CICS on MVS with OS/VS COBOL.  I've been a CICS
systems programmer since CICS/VS was introduced and it was macro-level
those days.  It was no way to avoid the fiddling with BLL cells in COBOL. 
I was not a COBOL programmer, I just supported the guys that had to cope
with it.

I think the way CICS (mis)used the BLL cells in COBOL is an interesting
story.  These are the base locators for the Linkage Section.  Each 01-level
was addressed by a BLL cell (more than one if the structure was more than
4K).  The content was meant to be set at entry to a subroutine and remain
constant until GOBACK.  OS/VS CICS required a first structure that
described the very collection of BLL cells and set the content of this
first BLL, so the program was able to manipulate the BLL cells.  Thus we
got support for dynamic storage.

The code generated by the compiler generally assumed that BLL cell content
was constant.  So to ensure that an updated BLL cell was used when
referencing a Linkage Section structure, you had to code the SERVICE RELOAD
compiler directive.

It is not difficult to understand that this was a mess to a traditional
COBOL batch programmer.  It had to be done in a similar way in assembler,
but this was everyday life to an assembler programmer.  PL/I already had
facilities to support based structures, so the PL/I CICS programs were
cleaner than with COBOL.  C was not supported by CICS in those days.

The introduction of pointers with VS COBOL II changed all this.  COBOL
programs can now be written in PL/I style (though the syntax is different).

Gunnar.
```

```
One issue about "SERVICE RELOAD" and OS/VS COBOL (or DOS/VS COBOL) that may
be missed by some is that IF you used "CAPEX" (later CA-Optimizer), you did
NOT need to code the SERVICE RELOAD statement because that "add-on" to the
IBM compiler handled these linkage pointers "better" than IBM's own compiler.
Other than that, I still can't figure out how Thane avoided them.
```

```
Well, I can see a couple of ways <G>.  

I rarely used EXEC CICS LINK.  Instead we used XCTL or RETURN with
different transaction ID's.  We made use of the communications section
quite often.  

However, I did have some subroutines executed via EXEC CICS LINK - and
I know I didn't use SERVICE RELOAD.  I think the modified translator
provided by Software Pursuits handled that.  

The DOS/SP 2.1 shop never used EXEC CICS LINK, and in fact never used
RETURN with a transaction ID.  Their code was very simplistic, but
suited their needs.
```

- **References:** `<832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <WdM64.1146$_f.3759@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <nT874.11689$DC6.169673@news2.mia> <83jfca$g16$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <FBx74.847$Fc6.8352@news4.mia> <385f0501.84403950@news1.attglobal.net> <01bf4cb5$2e08e670$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <38622de8.291514320@news.freewwweb.com> <3E8CB67BF5FCBF3E.76466B2A492809DE.E12DAEFB266FFD19@lp.airnews.net> <3862d4a2.334202275@news.freewwweb.com> <83uqth$ukk$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <3866274f.552008712@news.freewwweb.com>`

```
Somewhere we changed subjects.  I started saying (or meant to say) that I
couldn't believe that you hadn't used BLL-cells.  Others started talking
about the SERVICE RELOAD statement.  If you had any "items" in your linkage
section, then I think you were using BLL-cells -even if you didn't use
SERVICE RELOAD.  This had (has) nothing to do with EXEC CICS LINK
statements - but does happen when you use CICS "locate" rather than "move"
mode.  (I.e. when you use the "name of a bll-cell" in *any* EXEC CICS
statement - rather than the name of a working-storage section.  The were also
used whenever you use EXEC CICS GETMAIN.)
```

```
On Sun, 26 Dec 1999 18:10:17, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> Somewhere we changed subjects.  I started saying (or meant to say) that I
> couldn't believe that you hadn't used BLL-cells.  Others started talking
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I have avoided GETMAIN like the plague. <G>  Thinking about this 
further, I think I might have had occasion to modify a program that 
used BLL cells, but I never created one.  

Let me give you a little history.  When I started we were using a 
really terrible third party tool called Accolade to create our CICS 
programs.  When moving from 1.5 to 1.6, Accolade would not make the 
transition.  I went to CICS school at IBM so I could port these 
programs.  Based on there extensive use of SWA and TWA, I decided it 
best to just scrap and rewrite them.  We needed some method of 
duplicating the Accolade menus and security features (No RACF with 
MVT/VSE).  I designed and coded a security and menuing system that 
made use of the communications section and XCTL.  In addition, IBM was
hot on the SAA and CUA bandwagon at that time.  I made all the 
applications CUA compliant.  We had some fairly complex applications, 
but none of them were heavy "Transaction serving" systems.  They were 
really interactive file maint systems.  Our user base was around 100 
or so most of the time.  I may have never gotten into the performance 
areas where using memory internally, or sharing memory areas would 
have helped me much.  Looks like I missed out! <G>
```

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf510d$cf54f760$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <WdM64.1146$_f.3759@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <nT874.11689$DC6.169673@news2.mia> <83jfca$g16$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <FBx74.847$Fc6.8352@news4.mia> <385f0501.84403950@news1.attglobal.net> <01bf4cb5$2e08e670$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <38622de8.291514320@news.freewwweb.com> <3E8CB67BF5FCBF3E.76466B2A492809DE.E12DAEFB266FFD19@lp.airnews.net> <3862d4a2.334202275@news.freewwweb.com> <83uqth$ukk$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <3866274f.552008712@news.freewwweb.com> <845lva$qe3$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WdTI3zkJD9pM@localhost>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WdTI3zkJD9pM@localhost>...
> I have avoided GETMAIN like the plague. <G>  Thinking about this 
> further, I think I might have had occasion to modify a program that 
> used BLL cells, but I never created one.  

Disclaimer: I only know Cobol on IBM mainframe.

All Cobol programs that use Linkage Section use BLL cells.  Each 01 level
has a corresponding BLL cell.

The BLL cells are initialized from the parameter list (procedure division
using parm1 parm2 ...) when the program starts.  After that the content of
the BLL cell is not supposed to change in a batch program.  CICS introduced
a new way of using Linkage Section, dynamic storage areas required change
of base locators (BLL).  The point is that the compiled code must recognize
when a BLL has changed, so that it addresses the correct data area.  The
purpose of SERVICE RELOAD is to inform the compiler that it must now reload
the base register from the BLL cell before using it to address data.

This has nothing to do with the operating system (as one other poster
indicated), it is a property of the compiler.  In VS COBOL II, using
pointers and SET ADDRESS statements, this is not an issue; the compiler
will know when to reload base registers.

Maybe I can shed some light on why you didn't have to use SERVICE RELOAD
with OS/VS COBOL (or was it OS COBOL Version 4).  There was a compiler
option for OPTIMIZE.  I think that when not using that compiler option the
compiler would always reload the base registers before using them, so
SERVICE RELOAD was not neccesary.  But this was a long time ago, so I am
not sure.

Gunnar.
```

```
Gunnar Opheim wrote:

> Disclaimer: I only know Cobol on IBM mainframe.
> 
> All Cobol programs that use Linkage Section use BLL cells.  Each 01 level
> has a corresponding BLL cell.

I have used COBOL in many environments, but half of my experience has
been with IBM mainframes.  Only a couple of months were CICS.  From your
post, I guess I have been using BLL cells, but outside of this thread I
have never heard of them.  Is there some reason I should have heard of
them?  Should they mean anything for me?
```

## Thread: Grace Murray Hopper — 5 msgs

### Re: Grace Murray Hopper

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385C59AA.64DF33F4@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<384FE08D.2FE6C33C@home.com>`

```
Ah ha, so that's where the error was made.  I'd give her Flow-Matic, and
much else, but designing the Univac I ?  Never heard that, and wonder
what E. M. would say.

Warren Simmons

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> From today's paper's millennium column :-
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Grace Murray Hopper

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385C671F.1CE9ECCF@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<384FE08D.2FE6C33C@home.com> <385C59AA.64DF33F4@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Following up on my recent sceptical posting, GH does seem to have
some real achievements to her name

1) According to her, the first compiler. One of the first for
sure.
2) The idea of cobol (an english like language that non-engineers
can program). 
3) flow-matic 
4) the idea of code reuse (still encountering stiff resistance
among programmers) and some practical steps towards making it
possible.

Still following up GHs exact role in these things...

But she has been credited with so much she did not do, and to
have received so many honours, you have to wonder, why? Perhaps
because she was a woman in a male dominated field, perhaps the
force of her personality, or charisma, who knows.

Tim Josling


Warren G. Simmons wrote:
> 
> Ah ha, so that's where the error was made.  I'd give her Flow-Matic, and
> much else, but designing the Univac I ?  Never heard that, and wonder
> what E. M. would say.
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Grace Murray Hopper

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385D2AA9.D067DCB1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<384FE08D.2FE6C33C@home.com> <385C59AA.64DF33F4@worldnet.att.net> <385C671F.1CE9ECCF@melbpc.org.au>`

```


Tim Josling wrote:
> 
> Following up on my recent sceptical posting, GH does seem to have
> some real achievements to her name
> 
Tim,

You previously made the point that perhaps because of the feminist
movement, people tend to look for heroines. I don't think that's the
case with Grace. Like Madame Curie in her time, Grace did make it on her
own, well before Women's Lib, probably due to her intellect and yes,
very likely, forcefulness of personality.

A parallel, though later, is Adele Goldberg who goes back to Xerox PARC
days and is the 'lady' of Smalltalk; like Grace, I get the feel she is
an 'individualist'.

PS: I wondered when Warren Simmons was going to chip in; he certainly
had enough conversations with the good lady. 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Grace Murray Hopper

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385D4238.7AA6D744@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<384FE08D.2FE6C33C@home.com> <385C59AA.64DF33F4@worldnet.att.net> <385C671F.1CE9ECCF@melbpc.org.au> <385D2AA9.D067DCB1@home.com>`

```
Her list of awards took a couple of pages, and in addition to the first
compiler (A-1 in her terminology), and B-1 (Flow-Matic) she did the
first glossary of computer terms.  I think when the Pittsburgh DPMA made
her "man of the year" that told it all.

Warren Simmons
> PS: I wondered when Warren Simmons was going to chip in; he certainly
> had enough conversations with the good lady.
> 
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
I think if you research admiral Hopper's comments on feminists.  You'll find
that she couldn't stand them.  She claimed that being a women was never a
impediment in her various endeavors.

Cheers,
Ralph
```

<a id="t-00b97fba2c"></a>
## Thread: 8 bit C boolean in MF Cobol 4.038 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### 8 bit C boolean in MF Cobol 4.038

- **From:** Alan Dreyer <chieflilal@triton.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385BF23D.AA37D012@triton.net>`

```
I writing a program in Cobol that does calls to the Win API.  I need to
know how to define the equivalent of a C 8 bit boolean value in MF Cobol
4.038.  Any assistance would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks.
Al Dreyer
```

#### ↳ Re: 8 bit C boolean in MF Cobol 4.038

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MFe84.874$ra.23803@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<385BF23D.AA37D012@triton.net>`

```
Well, I'm not a C/C++ guy, but an eight-bit value should be a PIC X(01). (If
you use COMP-X you can get numeric values in it pretty easily).

But...

Which Win32 API call uses an 8-bit value? Even within structures I think
most everything is aligned at least 16 bits.

If you post the name(s) of the Win32API functions you are contemplating,
perhaps someone will have specific COBOL code you  can adapt.
```

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 16 Dec 1999 03:06:05 -0500, William Lynch
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
All due credit to your dad young man. But you know, wouldn't have hurt
if he'd also taught you how this language is written. True, we don't
encourage 'shouting' on the Web, but a few well-placed capitalized words
wouldn't hurt, say like Radio Shack.

Still, given the current crap that is disseminated from schools, the
ability to spell and write aren't a big deal - just so long as you
express yourselves; oh yes, and it isn't even necessary to be courteous
or diplomatic to get an idea across.

No, my own son is no different. I throw the Sports section on the floor
and read the rest of the paper - he picks up the Sports section, it's
the only part he reads; the only news he picks up are 'sound bites' on
the magic one-eyed monster. Down the road, he's going to be puzzled and
frustrated as to why he didn't make it in the corporate hierarchy.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

```
Bill Lynch wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Note I used the word 'merger' not 'take-over' - the latter is bad enough
with the blood-letting that goes on called 'rationalization' - but
unfortunately that's the name of the game.

One reason, cited in the newspaper article regarding mergers, was - so
often what is forgotten is the corporate philosophy of the respective
partners. Close to home it was talking about a merger between two
successful companies, TCPL (Trans Canada Pipelines) and Nova. To date
the shares have taken a nose-dive and they are about to lay-off in
excess of 1,000 staff.

Let's make it more personal; you and I each run successful software
companies, let's say 30 staff each. You are into an area I covet and
your approach/software appeals to me. I make a marketing ploy to
interest you in my offer. I'm not looking to benefit you, but me, even
though I make it sound that way to you. We eventually agree, although we
both have different objectives from this merger, and it takes place.

Silly 'staff' things like you give your folks free coffee and cookies -
me, I'm the tight-wad and demand they put a dime in the box for each
coffee. Stupid, but these small things can turn into grievous irritants.
(Pause : In reality I don't have this problem, 'cos I just go from my
bedroom/office and make a cup of coffee for moi <G>).

Ignoring the staff aspect - who's the boss ? One of us has to take
'junior' position and that also applies to our departmental heads -
here's where you hit the first level of dissatisfaction - right at the
top of the organization.

At the true business level, what about our respective standards
regarding system design and programming. You are not unhappy if your
people use GO TO in a restricted tight loop. Me - the phrase 'GO TO' is
absolutely verboten, and this goes on ad nauseum through are programing
approach. 

Move on to our customers, I am a little more lenient when the customer,
just as the project is being finished, asks for a couple of
enhancements, entailing say 3-4 weeks more work, (I've covered this by
'upping' the original price). You work to a tighter time scale, giving
the end user a 'fairer' price, but you are adamant, "A contract is a
contract" and if the customer wants that they are going to have to pay
more money.

Take all these staff or organizational differences and increase the
staff to 2,000 or 3,000 then you've got real problems. In short order
the good people from both of the partners will leave. Sadly, the only
way a merger really works is when one partner is the dominant, because
of cash resources, and 'merger' is used as a substitute word for
'take-over'.

My own examples to the problem - hope that helps explain it.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Sample source code is one area that the Standard is (almost) definitely NEVER
going to get into.  It is "believed" that these are both error-prone and
non-portable.  Do you really use the ANSI (or ISO) Standard document as your
"programmer's guide"?  If so, let J4 know this (as they have explicitly
indicated that the Standard is for compiler vendors and NOT targeted at
end-users).  Comments on "end-user" use of this document should be addressed
to the J4 chair at:

  doncobol <at> mediaone.net

P.S.  What compiler do you use?  Doesn't that vendor provide you with some
type of programmer guide?  If it isn't good enough, I suggest that you
contact them as well (or instead of) J4.
```

```
was published by Micro Focus), but the BEST place to start is with the IBM
Migration Guide.  On the web you can see this at:

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYMG201/CCONTENTS

which for the web-less is:

 Title: COBOL Migration Guide
  Document Number: GC26-4764-04
```

```
Bill Tredinnick writes...

>1)Looking for a good article on differences between COBOL II
>   and COBOL 370/MVS
>
>2)Looking for good articles on converting OS/VS COBOL and
>   COBOL II to COBOL 370 or COBOL for MVS.

How about a class? We have a two-day course, Beyond COBOL II,  that covers:

* COBOL II features
* COBOL/370 features
* Intrinsic functions
* Introduction to Language Environment
* COBOL for MVS & VM features
* COBOL for OS/390 & VM features
* Introduction to Object COBOL on OS/390
* Millennium Language Extensions

Includes hands on labs.

Check our website for details (www.trainersfriend.com); follow link to topics
then to COBOL, then to Beyond COBOL II; this is a one page description; at the
bottom of the page is a link to a one page set of course objeectives; at the
bottom of this page is a link to the detailed topical outline.

Regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting to COBOL 370/MVS

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991220014741.29404.00000042@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<83h3uu$jql$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Actually, the best "articles" on this are the IBM-supplied Migration guides. 
They are available on BookManager (check the systems programmer for the site to
find out how to access - and then look for bookshelf CEE1BK02, and manual
IGYMG102 - Compiler and Run-Time Migration Guide).  These are also available
from the IBM Web Support Site
www.ibm.com/software/s390/le/ and select the library button on the left.  There
may also be a downloadable PDF.
  I suggest these, rather than something written for Entrprise Journal or such,
because they contains almost everything the IBM Labs knows about doing the
conversion, and include some issues you might not have considered, such as the
different ways the programs will fail in the new environment (you may not get
an abend when you expect to!).
Be aware that the major difference is that you are moving from a MVS/ESA
environment to a Language Environment under OS/390 (I presume).  This has large
implications to link-edit and runtime language support.
The differences between COBOL/370 and COBOL II are less significant, from a
source code standpoint, since both are ANSI 85 implementations.  The major
differences are the way the operating system supports the language.
If you have a lot of OS/VS COBOL to convert, you may want to investigate an
IBM-supplied conversion tool to do that.  It is called CCCA (COBOL and
Command-level CICS Conversion Aid).  It automates much of the tedious work of
replacing reserved words (all EXHIBITs become DISPLAYs, and so on), and it does
a pretty good job of converting BLL Cells, should you have any CICS programs to
do.  I've used it on a couple of conversion projects, and it paid for itself
very quickly.  Its nicest feature, aside from being very customizable (you can
build a conversion for any text string, if you are in an acquistion or merged
environment, for example), is that it explains very clearly when if finds a
conversion it can not do!
You can find the manual for it in the COBOL language bookshelf (sorry, I don't
have the name handy).
.  Also, be aware that Visual Age Services will, for a fee, send an expert to
advise, or even a team to do it, if requested.
Good luck, and send me an e-mail if you have specific questions these books
don't appear to answer
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
[...]
>And there is also an often overlooked serious penalty to that approach.
>One of the things in C/C++ and many other languages that contribute most
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>other logical bugs combined.

The implementation of pointers in COBOL is much closer
to PL/I than it is to C.

>  Up until now, COBOL has been free of this
>particular problem, significantly contributing to COBOL's well deserved
>reputation for reliability.  I am very concerned about this issue and
>the future of COBOL.

To see the future of COBOL with pointers, look at more
than 27 years of pointer use in PL/I. Does PL/I have a
reputation for unreliable programs?

>  If these features are implemented widely in COBOL,
>before long, COBOL programmers are going to be shooting their feet off,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>personally I don't think so.

COBOL needs a means for implementing dynamic data
structures. Two paths are being offered: OO and dynamic
memory allocation with pointers. Some of the features
for implementing dynamic data structures within OO
(collections) will not be available, in the standard, for
about five years after approval of the current draft.

>  After many years of writing code in COBOL
>and many other languages, I believe COBOL's main advantage, and why it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>more from the added complexity and subtleties,

The added complexity, to the language, is ALLOCATE,
FREE, POINTER, BASED [ON], and additional formats
for SET.

The subtleties are really no different than working with
temporary files. If you create a temporary file, don't
lose the reference to it. If you create a linked list using
dynamically allocated memory, don't lose the reference
to the head of the list. The difference between temporary
files and dynamically allocated memory is that memory,
that is no longer required must be freed by the program.
This is similar to deleting every record, in a temporary
indexed (or relative) file, by its key (when the data is
no longer needed).

> and I believe the
>overwhelming majority of COBOL applications really doesn't need them.

I have suggested the same of OO, which is more complex
than dynamic memory allocation and pointers.

It is unfair to imply that prior lack of reliability in C and C++
programs necessarily implies the same lack of reliability
in COBOL programs. This is because, during the late 80s
and throughout the 90s, there was a large demand for C
and C++ programmers. This demand was so large it could
only be filled by inexperienced programmers (rookies).
Rookie programmers in any language will make mistakes.

Again, the implementation of dynamic memory allocation
and pointers, in COBOL, is much closer to PL/I than C.

If experienced PL/I programmers use dynamic memory
allocation and pointers well, then experienced COBOL
programmers will use dynamic memory allocation and
pointers well.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
<SNIP>

> The subtleties are really no different than working with
> temporary files. If you create a temporary file, don't
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<SNIP>

    We also need to consider the compilers. Whether it is
a matter of cuture or skill, the choices that the compiler
writers make effect us.  When you do a memory allocation
in MF cobol, a stop run, or a cobol runtime error will
automatically release ALL allocated memory, even if the
program has lost track of it.

    Is this true with C/C++ ?  Most of the magazine articles I
have seen (not recently) comparing various C compilers focused
on speed, not maintainablilty or reliablity of the program.  Adding
this addtional layer to the software costs speed.  I feel that it is
well worth it.

    We have seen the same thing with cars.  Writers compare
2 or 3 cars, and give us the 0-60 acceleration.  Now we have
cars that are sluggish unless you drive like an idiot (or reviewer,
same thing).
```

```
>     We also need to consider the compilers. Whether it is
> a matter of cuture or skill, the choices that the compiler
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> well worth it.

With virtual memory your program run's in it's own address space. 
When your program ceases then the address space disappears as well.

This really is the responsibility of the operating system.  It must
realize that the program has ceased and free up all resources as a
result.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any Suggestions? (Extreme Programming)

- **From:** Ralph Jones <leclay@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3868DF3E.64104482@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <826dpi$fpk$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <3846C4A5.C1A66591@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <826slb$peq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847487c.537138075@news1.attglobal.net> <827r49$tb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3847D524.4FE1ADBA@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <829ct3$cuo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3848725F.D1A0FA7C@att.net> <38488E05.35632487@home.com> <3849017F.B8D940BF@zip.com.au> <384CA162.C7B5367D@home.com> <82ja74$5ej$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <Sva34.2737$Ze7.107325@news3.mia> <82jcc6$7aj$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <3853c3f1.43035700@news1.attglobal.net> <3853F9A2.ADBA8CA6@home.com> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <83iiqo$1s0n$1@news.hitter.net> <83n4d3$54i$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <385F6B5F.285A906B@zip.com.au>`

```
C has no defined memory allocation in the language.  The ANSI malloc and
it's derivatives are separately compiled functions (sub-routines).  The
major C compilers will automatically free runtime memory allocations when
the program ends for what ever reason.

In C++ it is strictly up to the programmer to free all dynamic memory
using object destructors.  This is the Achilles heel of C++.

C++ has be been grafted onto C, kind of like grafting a Mack truck on top
of a 2 seat Honda Del Sol.

Reliability is up to the programming team, not the language.

True, C tends to be a write-only language.  However.  most C programs are
broken down into much smaller files than a monolithic COBOL program and as
a result, a C program becomes somewhat readable.

After doing C for a couple of years I finally came across a readable C
program.  It was code generated by the GNU fast lexical analyzer, flex.
After that I tried to code C using flex style.  Flex was written by
Vern Paxson.

Cheers,
Ralph Jones


Ken Foskey wrote:

> Russell Styles wrote:

…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
Ralph Jones wrote:
> 
> C has no defined memory allocation in the language.  The ANSI malloc and
> it's derivatives are separately compiled functions (sub-routines).  The
> major C compilers will automatically free runtime memory allocations when
> the program ends for what ever reason.

This is not really valid.  All applications will have the library
functions except washing machines.

> In C++ it is strictly up to the programmer to free all dynamic memory
> using object destructors.  This is the Achilles heel of C++.

You can use NEW and DELETE as well as malloc.  The OO concept enables
a cleaner implementation of the clean up.  If you code your allocation
code in the constructor (the routine that does an initialize of a new
instance) and your deallocation in the destructor (the routine that
does the clean up of a disappearing instance).

You can create an instance of an object that lasts for a routine
(paragraph).  You do not code for creation and deletion in the same
way that working storage just appears and disappears if you use the
recursive compiler directive in Cobol.

> C++ has be been grafted onto C, kind of like grafting a Mack truck on top
> of a 2 seat Honda Del Sol.

Hmm...   Kind of,  there are far better environments.  To learn OO on
top of a procedural language is very difficult, it is better to lean a
pure environment.  This is my concern with OO cobol.   Learn the OO
concepts very well first, then be surprised when you do not really
understand.   Small talk appears to be the language of choice, though
this is strongly debated.

> Reliability is up to the programming team, not the language.

Yes,  I trust my vendor to give me reliable compilers.  In 15 years I
have found one error with the cobol compiler for MVS and one
'documentation' change (I still consider it a bug).

> True, C tends to be a write-only language.  However.  most C programs are
> broken down into much smaller files than a monolithic COBOL program and as
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Vern Paxson.

It is MUCH easier to write crap code in C than cobol.  I have seen
many write only programs in Cobol as well, nothing replaces a good
style guide.  I personally have my own guidelines, I now DO NOT remove
any letters from words in variables in code unless it is a corporate
standard.   Craft takes time to develop and we all constantly learn.

> Cheers,
> Ralph Jones

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any Suggestions? (Extreme Programming)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84gfv3$u15$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <83iiqo$1s0n$1@news.hitter.net> <83n4d3$54i$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <385F6B5F.285A906B@zip.com.au> <3868DF3E.64104482@ibm.net> <386A9B4A.E00D935E@zip.com.au>`

```
: Ralph Jones wrote:
:> 
:> C has no defined memory allocation in the language.  The ANSI malloc and
:> it's derivatives are separately compiled functions (sub-routines).  The
:> major C compilers will automatically free runtime memory allocations when
:> the program ends for what ever reason.

This is simply not true.  When a program terminates the _operating_system_
will normally recover all memory used by the process, the C compiler
will not do so at all, the C runtime library may or may not bother
to relase the whole heap back to the OS, it certainly will _not_
issue any 'free()'s automatically.

On some systems there may be memory allocations that survive the
program termination, depending on how the allocation was done and
which OS and library is used.

:> In C++ it is strictly up to the programmer to free all dynamic memory
:> using object destructors.  This is the Achilles heel of C++.

Simply not true.  As above, when a program terminates the operating
system may recover all allocated memory regardless of how it was
allocated or if it was released or destructed (or it may not
recover certain types of allocation).  Destructors do not have
to be explicitly called by the programmer.


:> Reliability is up to the programming team, not the language.

The language provides the tools (or does not) that the programmers
can use.  For example Cobol does not allow a condition to create a
side effect whereas C does.  When a complex condition is short-
circuited the side effect may be missed.  This may make the program
less reliable because the C language allows this, whereas Cobol
disallows it and prevents the programmers making mistakes of this
type.

:> True, C tends to be a write-only language.  However.  most C programs are
:> broken down into much smaller files than a monolithic COBOL program and as
:> a result, a C program becomes somewhat readable.

Not all Cobol is in monolithic programs.  Many Cobol systems are
written using smaller code modules that are statically or
dynamically linked.  However, size of source files has no bearing
on 'readability'.  Some people can cope with very large and 
complex code blocks and find them perfectly readable, others
cannot cope and can only deal with 'bite' sized blocks without
getting confused.
```

- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <HuV44.16154$Dk.242231@news1.mia> <38545683.EA71B1B3@home.com> <38547457.88200718@news1.attglobal.net> <832pt1$b81$1@news.igs.net> <w0854.182$Sj6.9231@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <833qc5$r7r$1@news.igs.net> <9Je54.949$Sj6.26677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <Gtu54.2727$DC6.28547@news2.mia> <83iiqo$1s0n$1@news.hitter.net> <83n4d3$54i$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <385F6B5F.285A906B@zip.com.au> <3868DF3E.64104482@ibm.net> <386A9B4A.E00D935E@zip.com.au> <84gfv3$u15$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote in message <84gfv3$u15$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>: Ralph Jones wrote:
>:>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>to be explicitly called by the programmer.


Almost. MS-DOS, 32-bit Windows (except as below) , every *nix I've worked
with and IBM MF OSs clean up.

16-Bit Windows does not clean up memory allocated with GlobalAlloc until
Windows terminates. This includes 16-bit software running under Win 9x
32-bit OS. There is a single 16Mb (max) memory area allocated to "be shared
by all 16-bit applications." GlobalAllocs from this pool must be explictly
released or they remain allocated until all 16-bit applications terminate,
which on Win 9x never happens because GDI is 16-bit. 16-bit apps running
under NT is different.

MCM
```

```
In article <834o8s$af8$1@news.cerf.net>,
  "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:
> > I know the file status codes, I don't know that your WFS-FILE-EOF
> > is actually "10".  You may know this because you have used it for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> more predictable????

"10" as a file status is guaranteed in Cobol to have a specific
meaning.  It should be unnecessary to look it up.

'FILE-IS-AT-END' _may_ have a value of "10" as a 78 or a PIC XX, or it
may be an 88 level (and thus have a different usage) or it _may_ be a
PIC X field that is designed to be set as a flag when AT END is taken.

If you never doubt what it means then you are not being defensive about
your code and there will be tears.

Richard


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz wrote:
> 
> 
> "10" as a file status is guaranteed in Cobol to have a specific
> meaning.  It should be unnecessary to look it up.

True, and I understand your complaint against user defined variables
replacing the '10'.

But this is a flaw in the implementations of COBOL.  COBOL was designed
to be in English, status codes should be in English.  This would be
simple to implement, and should have been implemented long ago.
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00

```
Ditto to all error messages.  We are now at the stage the the error numbers
refer to lists in a computerized document, the lists refer to page numbers
in a computerized document, and nothing links them together.  Errors should
be hypertext pointers to where I need to go.

We have far to much historic idiocy in the language.
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> > no, i write in lower case for a social reason.....
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > habit. and hitting shift is a pain.

I used BBSs in the 300 BPS era, and teletype in older eras.  It didn't
take any longer to transmit an upper case character than it took to
transmit a lower case character.  In fact, the slowness of normal
transmittal meant that there was relatively more time for me to make
what I was writing clear.

Of course, it is no great feat to type faster than I think.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Entry Level Jobs (was: Re: Frustration)

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<83mq0v$7i2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19991212003209.04439.00000186@ng-ce1.news.cs.com> <eMlSniMR$GA.280@cpmsnbbsa03> <830uce0gt3@enews3.newsguy.com> <99bd5s0q6lglsptff18ffuo3pd7mk8p8hf@4ax.com> <38589D6D.EB52985E@worldnet.att.net> <o4ei5sscegt4okaq1024plmbdkrlurfmn8@4ax.com> <385AE01F.7D5D0BF3@home.com> <kqpo5s8ld7cbf43ovmh470qrsfstpnillu@4ax.com> <385D6C5D.C4CF5A68@home.com> <385E46B7.58AC1477@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Hell, I *wrote* a BBS in Cobol.  Just to prove it could be done.  I designed
the entire thing using screen sections, then I wrote a time-sharing hook
(this was on a PC under DOS) and triggered once per second.  It scanned the
screen, compared it to the previous screen, and transmitted an ASCII Escape
sequence to update the remote terminal. That was written in assembler, as
were the Xmodem upload/download routines. It ran in batch mode under
Binkley, had E-mail, etc.  Ran in 512k 10 megs.

All the files were Isam.  I made a lot of Sysops Cobol fans when they found
out how easy it was to add a birthday index to a user file and personalize
birthday messages.

You could do the same thing today using nothing but Cobol.  With the kind of
processor power we have today, it should be relatively trivial.

```

<a id="t-fdd11a9690"></a>
## Thread: Detecting OPEN files ? — 3 msgs

### Detecting OPEN files ?

- **From:** nospam-dtfsdf@frontiernet.net (Douglas Flowers)
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385c8559.10026668@News.frontiernet.net>`

```
Is there a way, (within a Cobol program) to detect if a file has been
opened by another user before attempting to OPEN the file ?   I have a
program which uses "COBOLASSIGN"  (Tandem Cobol) to dynamically assign
a filename(from a file of sourcecode  filenames) in order to open that
file and and inspect the sourcode, line by line for a particular text
string. When the program attempts to open a file that has already been
opened by another user with LOCK, my program ABENDS. Normal error
trapping code after the OPEN has no effect... the program just ABENDS
with a system message about the file being open by another user. Is
there another form of OPEN that can recover from this situation
without ABENDing  ??


Thanks, 


Doug Flowers
Phoenix, AZ


- when replying via E-mail, remove "nospam-"  from my address
```

#### ↳ Re: Detecting OPEN files ?

- **From:** PAL3000 <pauldub*REMOVETHIS*@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uFxeOOFjAtsOV4Y=WS4dLDA9s0lG@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<385c8559.10026668@News.frontiernet.net>`

```
Normal functionality is to return a status code of locked when you
attempt to open a locked file.  Your problem may be in the program's
declaratives section where there may be code that is doing the abend
when the file is locked.  

It may also be in the "COBOLASSIGN".  Can you code an OPEN WITH LOCK
to check if locked before doing the COBOLASSIGN?

Paul


On Sun, 19 Dec 1999 07:13:13 GMT, nospam-dtfsdf@frontiernet.net
(Douglas Flowers) wrote:

>Is there a way, (within a Cobol program) to detect if a file has been
>opened by another user before attempting to OPEN the file ?   I have a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>- when replying via E-mail, remove "nospam-"  from my address

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out 
*REMOVETHIS* from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: Detecting OPEN files ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83p2m8$v5l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<385c8559.10026668@News.frontiernet.net>`

```
Douglas has already received a private email response (from Don Nelson of
Tandem - aka Compaq) but I thought that I would post the reply here - for
anyone else who needs/wants the reply,

" You can use a call to FILE_GETINFOBYNAME_ to find out (it is a Guardian
call).  You can also specify a declarative that mentions the specific file
and the program will continue to operate even if the OPEN fails.  All of this
can be found out by going through the normal Compaq help channels. "
```

## Thread: translante cobol files to txt of mdb files — 6 msgs

### translante cobol files to txt of mdb files

- **From:** "Perfect" <nikolaas.coenegrachts@student.kuleuven.ac.be>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<945645582.679982@marvin>`

```
Hi,

Could somebody give me some info how I can translate cobol data files to a
format wich can be accesed in MS access.

Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: translante cobol files to txt of mdb files

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-12-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385D70E6.822FF07B@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<945645582.679982@marvin>`

```
I'm afraid there is no such thing as "a Cobol datafile". The way these
files are structured is most of the time compiler and / or operating
system depended. For instance indexed files may have their indexes as a
part of the data file itself, or as separate files. So it al depends on
what compiler was used when the files were created.

Regards,

Dirk

Perfect wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: translante cobol files to txt of mdb files

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ud1gzrxS$GA.282@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<945645582.679982@marvin>`

```
What I would do is this.

1: Take the COBOL Source code and find the FD (or WS) record definition for
the file.

2: Create a new version of the original FD with all non-display fields
translated into display fields.  For easy Import into MS Access, add a
constant tab, comma, tilde, or whatever character inbetween the fields of
the file.

3: Write a new COBOL program using that new file definition, MOVEing the old
FD fields into the new FD fields, and then "write" out the records to a .txt
file.

4: Open MS Access and use the Import feature.  If you added a delimiting
character between the fields, MS Access will automatically parse the fields.
You'll have to go through each field and change them from TEXT to whatever
else they need to be in the final MS Access product, but you'll be well on
your way to having your data ready for use.  (If you didn't add a delimiting
character, on Import, you'll have a lot of work to do defining the size of
each field, but that's just typing work, not really a big problem.)

5: If you don't hae the original COBOL Source code (the file definition, in
particular), you are in bad shape.  Take the object code, and disassemble it
and try and work out the original file FD from the assembly language.




Perfect <nikolaas.coenegrachts@student.kuleuven.ac.be> wrote in message
news:945645582.679982@marvin...
> Hi,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: translante cobol files to txt of mdb files

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385DBD39.8395F0@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<945645582.679982@marvin>`

```
Perfect wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you

Yes, Siber Systems sells a number of Data File converters
that do just what you want -- they convert Cobol data files
to CVS (comma-separated values) and DBF formats. Both of these 
formats are gladly accepted by MS Access. Currently we can read
FSC and MF data files. RM and ACU data file readers are in works.

More on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: translante cobol files to txt of mdb files

- **From:** stevenln@aol.com (StevenLN)
- **Date:** 1999-12-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991220012211.29404.00000041@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<945645582.679982@marvin>`

```
Do accomplish the task, assuming that the "COBOL Datafile" (previous poster is
correct that there is no such thing - Cobol does not have an intrinsic file
structure) is located on an OS/390 or VSE host, or possibly an AS/400 (ok, I am
an IBM big-iron bigot), is to use one of the reporting tools generally
available in the environment, such as FOCUS or EasyTrieve, to write a
comma-separated-value file (*.CSV), download that to the PC or LAN, and then
open it with that file type in ACCESS.   Of course, you can also do this, with
much more manual coding, in COBOL.  If you choose that route, check out the
MOVE...CORResponding option to easy the coding and make it more amenable to
layout changes in the future.
```

#### ↳ Re: translante cobol files to txt of mdb files

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<385FF790.1CA5@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<945645582.679982@marvin>`

```
Perfect wrote:
> 
> Hi,
> 
> Could somebody give me some info how I can translate cobol data files to a
> format wich can be accesed in MS access.


You could try a generic parser/converter like ParseRat 
(http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm)
```

```
US-FL-JACKSONVILLE/ COBOL/MVS, DB2  REQUIREMENT # 2875

For more detailed information please contact Kathy Diez at (813) 286 - 7771
or e-Mail to Kdiez@cmctpa.com  visit our website at http://www.cmcits.com
or fax to (813) 286-2832.  When responding, please include job reference
number stated above.

Description: (20 consultants or contract to perm openings)
Looking for seasoned candidates with COBOL/MVS and DB2. Candidates must have
at least 5 years experience using these skills with companies known to have
significant IT environments. Prefer candidates who also have experience with
JCL, SDSF, Endevor, Expediter, batch processing and TSO. Secondary: UNIX
MicroFocus Cobol, Oracle, Diamond Claims Process System.

Candidates will engage in analysis/design/test and other activities to put
applications into production in a COBOL/DB2 platform. Department is focused
around design, creation and maintenance of corporate data infrastructures.
Duties will include production of high-quality deliverables under a
disciplined methodology in support of the full systems development life
cycle for the corporate data infrastructures. Candidates must work in a team
environment and take personal responsibility for their deliverables.
Candidates will work side-by-side with technical system resources during all
phases of the project. Candidates will likely be asked to work peak periods
of ovetime, including weekends. Candidates unwilling to work any requested
overtime need not apply. In addition, since weekend work may be required,
candidates should be prepared to make themselves available in Jacksonville
as needed. Relocation is expected, but will not be required for contractors
unless availability becomes an issue.  Date Needed: 1/4/00.  Project End
Date: 7/1/00.

Compensation:
Submit salary or hourly requirements for immediate attention.
CMC is always looking to enhance its permanent staff with talented
 professionals.  We also are open to W-2 and Corp to Corp status
professionals.

Resume:
Please submit resume in standard Word format.  Must have an  AM and PM
contact phone number, current Email address and availability time window.

US CITIZENS, AND GREEN CARD HOLDERS ONLY, NO BROKERS OR H1-B
PLEASE !
```
