###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Predict the future

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84jgdm$67u$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3860E184.3BF2D8B4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <s6hsstll5k2102@corp.supernews.com> <84avvo$c98$1@news.cerf.net> <386AB4A1.1B11F612@zip.com.au> <84g5h0$scc$1@news.cerf.net> <386BB31C.A2852B3C@home.com> <84ibup$gc0$1@news.igs.net> <386CBACB.C5E60FD4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <386CBACB.C5E60FD4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>...

>Or an improvement in capacity of x10.  Mobile capacity may do that.
>There have been arguments that PL/I failed to take over COBOL because it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the same benefit.

Yes, that is true.  But what do you mean by "Mobile capacity"?

I do see a place for web retailing, particularly in small towns that
traditionally have lacked "a decent bookstore", for example. What I do not
see is them replacing the Walmarts of this world.  Not only do most people
want to 'kick the tires first", but it is a delusion to think that there are
any savings. What is cheaper, and more efficient do  of:

1.  Take 500 orders over the net, and ship 500 televisions to 500 locations,
transferring all information over wire.

2. Deliver 500 televisions to a pile on a floor and hold a boxing day sale.
Everybody carries their television away from the pile, and all money is
transferred over wire.

Net commerce is only suitable for very specific things.  Books are about the
largest thing that ship cheaply enough to make it practical.

Certainly there are opportunities.  For example, I do not see the recording
industry lasting much longer.  Once we hit 100 gig disk drives, all music
will be sold producer-to-disk, direct, cash going the other way.  A lock on
*that* market would make Microsoft look small. Radio, recording companies,
television five years later, and movies about the same time.  They will all
become part of the "net" as the
cable companies convert to isp's.  You will download everything, and play it
jukebox style.  PC stereo equipment will be a huge new market.

But it will be sold at the future shop.  And therein lies the rub.  Many
people may choose to "order" from the local future shop over the net. The
existing companies have no net presence.  The net companies have no delivery
mechanism.  I see some tremendous changes in the market place as each
scrambles to buy out the other.  By the end of it all, it might be a moot
point over whether net commerce takes over retailing, or retailing takes
over net commerce.  I tend to think the latter will be true.  Take a look at
the ownership of "chapters" for example.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Predict the future

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84gi6m$uku$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3860E184.3BF2D8B4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <s6hsstll5k2102@corp.supernews.com> <84avvo$c98$1@news.cerf.net> <386AB4A1.1B11F612@zip.com.au> <84g5h0$scc$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle <cheesle@online.nospamplease.no> wrote:

: Reliability is the big issue, can't argue with you there. However, great
: improvements has taken place over the last years. Todays Win NT 4.0 SP6 is
: way more robust than NT 3.5 was in its time. As for Win2000 time will show.
: Beta looks good.

NT is still way down the list compared to what Mainframes, Netware
and Unix have been doing for years.  Typically mainframes only
get 'rebooted' when scheduled, Netware and Unix stay up for
months or years.  Netware has been upgradable in flight for
many years and so is not rebooted even when a new version is
installed.  Even Linux servers stay up for months at a time.

NT, it seems, is best scheduled to have reboots on a regular
basis for various reasons before they are needed.

SP6 is not recommended, revert to SP5.

Win2000 is almost all _new_ code.  It is a 1.0 release.  It
is also several times larger and more complex due to MS's
technique of making things 'simpler' by adding several
layers of complexity to hide underlying complexities (eg
Wizards, PnP, and other automatic mechanisms that get in
the way and stop me doing it the way _I_ want).

: Sounds like a good point, however not entirely agreed. I believe the Linux
: "support" will come as a Windows Terminal Server client. Cheaper, and means
: that your application is there today.

Windows TSE is a very poor multi-user system.  The underlying
Windows OS was never designed to have multiple concurrent users
and, more importantly, the Windows applications never catered
for multiple concurrent users in a rational way.  TSE was
merely a way of driving Citrix out of business when MS saw
revenue going to someone else.

EinFrame at least made an attempt to overcome the underlying
inherent problems of application sharing that TSE hasn't dealt
with.

Also, using Linux as a TSE client is _not_ cheaper due to the
way MS charges for NT/2000 and client support licences.  Even
though Linux may be free it will cost _more_ to use TSE than to
just put Win95 all around.  MS _needs_ the revenue.  If cheaper
is what you want don't use MS at all.

: Interesting, but you don't think that this will delete competition? If
: everyone were using agents to check the price next door, would'nt all end up
: on the same price?

Only if you think that price is everything.


: Yepp, I am just waiting for the big bang. It is totally insane pricing of
: these companies, and I feel sorry for those that eventually will loose in
: this big raffle.

No more so than the insane price of MS which is allegedly based
on false reporting of revenue and costs whereby the employees
are effectively being paid by stock options and the cost of
the payroll is reduced by the stock takeup.  Effectively,
so it is alleged, MS is printing more stock and using this as
if it were revenue.  If this were accounted for properly then
MS accounts would show an increasing loss over the last few
years.  They are being accused of fraud.
```

'money,'

## Thread: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject) — 8 msgs

### Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991222002544.23435.00001226@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<5dfq5sspsbl4e8kc6fgn8o9rugtr7k3655@4ax.com>`

```
>there is some advantage to changing
>transform replacing to inspect replacing, having end-reads and end-ifs
>and all. but i sure as hell don't know what it is. and when i code,
>(since i learned cobol 2, as opposed to 74) i don't know where to put
>the periods, and if the code drops down to the next line

Ah, youth!  If you've ever had to add a new function to a program using 10 or
12 "ALTER...GO TO"s, or debug one with multiple statements on each line and GO
TOs pointing every which way at 2AM, you'd be a lot more forgiving of us
old-timers appreciating a few sign-posts.  Be thankful you don't realize what
an improvement they are, or how much they are missed in languages like C.
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

#### ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83qocu$8k8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<5dfq5sspsbl4e8kc6fgn8o9rugtr7k3655@4ax.com> <19991222002544.23435.00001226@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```


Steve Newton <stevenln@aol.comnospam> wrote in message
news:19991222002544.23435.00001226@ng-fx1.aol.com...
> >there is some advantage to changing
> >transform replacing to inspect replacing, having end-reads and end-ifs
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ah, youth!  If you've ever had to add a new function to a program using 10
or
> 12 "ALTER...GO TO"s, or debug one with multiple statements on each line
and GO
> TOs pointing every which way at 2AM, you'd be a lot more forgiving of us
> old-timers appreciating a few sign-posts.  Be thankful you don't realize
what
> an improvement they are, or how much they are missed in languages like C.
> Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
> Still the best

Well put.  And if you're in doubt about where to put the periods, just
remember that by using scope terminators (end-if, end-read, etc.), you need
periods in only 2 places in the procedure division -- 1 before and 1 after
each section/paragraph name.  (And consistently putting the former in the
same column (12 for instance), they're easy to spot AND provide a line
spacer for readability at the same time).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83qv2s$up8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<5dfq5sspsbl4e8kc6fgn8o9rugtr7k3655@4ax.com> <19991222002544.23435.00001226@ng-fx1.aol.com> <83qocu$8k8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
However, do NOT remove any periods from a program that uses NEXT SENTENCE -
without completely understanding what it is doing (already).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38611211.EFC71C2D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<5dfq5sspsbl4e8kc6fgn8o9rugtr7k3655@4ax.com> <19991222002544.23435.00001226@ng-fx1.aol.com> <83qocu$8k8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <83qv2s$up8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> However, do NOT remove any periods from a program that uses NEXT SENTENCE -
> without completely understanding what it is doing (already).
> 

And be similarly careful if you have a pre-compiler inserting code into
your program.  It may have NEXT SENTENCE which you won't see until you
look at your compiler listing.

In fact - don't remove periods or do any type of cosmetic code changes
because you read the new theory/style.  That is insufficient reasoning.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386A93E7.8A3CA7F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<5dfq5sspsbl4e8kc6fgn8o9rugtr7k3655@4ax.com> <19991222002544.23435.00001226@ng-fx1.aol.com> <83qocu$8k8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <83qv2s$up8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38611211.EFC71C2D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> William M. Klein wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> because you read the new theory/style.  That is insufficient reasoning.

Moderately agree, carefully indented paragraphs is no substitute for
actually understanding what you are doing.

Code that is crafted (i.e. conforms to what is generally perceived as
best practice) will invariably be good code.  I personally believe
that using continue and removing full stops has made my code cleaner
and easier to understand.  The compiler helps find those misplaced
periods (full stops).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84gei0$t9k$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<5dfq5sspsbl4e8kc6fgn8o9rugtr7k3655@4ax.com> <19991222002544.23435.00001226@ng-fx1.aol.com> <83qocu$8k8q$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <83qv2s$up8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38611211.EFC71C2D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <386A93E7.8A3CA7F@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

: Moderately agree, carefully indented paragraphs is no substitute for
: actually understanding what you are doing.

And is far from having the most important aspect: getting the
compiler to understand what you are doing.

Never trust someone else's indenting, nor your own, it may fool the
reader, but it will never 'fool' the compiler.

OTOH, if you don't know what you are doing, do it neatly   ;-)

: Code that is crafted (i.e. conforms to what is generally perceived as
: best practice) will invariably be good code.  I personally believe
: that using continue and removing full stops has made my code cleaner
: and easier to understand.  The compiler helps find those misplaced
: periods (full stops).

Absolutely, the more bugs that the compiler can find the less
the programmer has to search for.  I try to program to the
point that a clean compile means that the program will work,
however, I do know the limitations of this.  Putting in every
END- statement possible does allow the compiler to give
more warnings - thus achieving less bugs.

Using silly tricks, such as mixing NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF,
denys the compiler the ability to warn of introduced bugs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comments in Source Code (was Re: (no subject)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84ibam$g2v$1@news.igs.net>`

## Thread: MicroFocus Cobol Data Corruption Problem — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus Cobol Data Corruption Problem

- **From:** "Mirek" <mirekn@mksoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z3b84.370$9O1.14583194@nnrp1.tor.metronet.ca>`

```
Hi,

I am trying to figure out a way to fix corrupted MicroFocus Cobol
data files. I have not found any tools or applications for doing such a
task. I was wondering if anyone knows of such utilities or possibly
a way to write such a tool in C/C++/VB. I am not a Cobol programmer thus I
am not familiar with the file format of the Cobol ISM files. Any
help would be greatly appreciated.

Mirek
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Data Corruption Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83rnbf$156$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<z3b84.370$9O1.14583194@nnrp1.tor.metronet.ca>`

```
I think that all versions of Micro Focus (now MERANT) products include a
REBUILD function.  If you enter that command from a "dos prompt", I believe
it will give you some help.  One warning, be VERY careful that you don't use
the option that "rebuilds" a file from itself to itself - unless you already
have a backup of the file.
```

<a id="t-b86045b37d"></a>
## Thread: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
We're still converting some VS COBOL programs to OS/390 COBOL and have run into a situation that isn't making any sense.   Of course looking up the documentation hasn't been a joy either.

There is a field defined as:  PIC ----------------V--B (that's 16 hypens before the V and 2 after)

It is redefined as:  PIC ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZV9B (that's 16 Zs before the V).

The error is that the '9' is out of place.  The redefine was changed to put in a VZ9 but it doesn't like that either, putting in a VZBB makes it happy but the user wants to see a zero if the field is zero.

Any suggestions?


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83u2hk$n7o$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen,
  I checked the IBM LRM and it explicitly says that you may NOT use a "V"
before a floating "-".  See,

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYLR204/5%2e3%2e11%2e1
?ACTION=MATCHES&REQUEST=picture&TYPE=FUZZY&SHELF=&searchTopic=TOPIC&searchTex
t=TEXT&searchIndex=INDEX&rank=RANKScrollTOP=FIRSTHIT#FIRSTHIT

(which is the IBM LRM "picture precedence table".

Therefore, although the old IBM compiler might have allowed it, I don't think
it was ever legal/supported.
```

#### ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83tq93$193d$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
The format of these PICTURE clauses, appear to
indicate that the sole purpose of the redefinition is to
provide the "0" the user wants to see and that the
references in the procedure division are inside an IF;
that is,

    if field-1 equal 0
        move field-1 to redefined-field-2
    else
        move field-1 to field-2.

If the above is correct, the simple solution is to change
the redefintion to B(17)9B.
------------------
Rick Smith

Eileen Preston wrote in message ...
>We're still converting some VS COBOL programs to OS/390 COBOL and have run
into a situation that isn't making any sense.   Of course looking up the
documentation hasn't been a joy either.
>
>There is a field defined as:  PIC ----------------V--B (that's 16 hypens
before the V and 2 after)
>
>It is redefined as:  PIC ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZV9B (that's 16 Zs before the V).
>
>The error is that the '9' is out of place.  The redefine was changed to put
in a VZ9 but it doesn't like that either, putting in a VZBB makes it happy
but the user wants to see a zero if the field is zero.
>
>Any suggestions?
```

#### ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83u45q$2lg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Try 'PIC ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZVZ9B'. You must account for all defined
positions (16 + 3 = 19) in a redefines.

In article <s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>,
  "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
> We're still converting some VS COBOL programs to OS/390 COBOL and
have run into a situation that isn't making any sense.   Of course
looking up the documentation hasn't been a joy either.
>
> There is a field defined as:  PIC ----------------V--B (that's 16
hypens before the V and 2 after)
>
> It is redefined as:  PIC ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZV9B (that's 16 Zs before the
V).
>
> The error is that the '9' is out of place.  The redefine was changed
to put in a VZ9 but it doesn't like that either, putting in a VZBB
makes it happy but the user wants to see a zero if the field is zero.
>
> Any suggestions?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83tp2n$8hv$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
What are you TRYING to do?
  Normally using "floating hyphens" (minus signs) are used in DISPLAY fields,
and "V" is used in internal fields.  Therefore, I would suggest that you
change the "V" to a "." (period or full-stop).  If you really do NOT want any
"display" decimal point in our output when there are digits to the left of
the decimal point, then I am not    quite certain what to do.

(I have not checked the Standard or the IBM documentation on this - but I
don't think that the "display-type" sign symbols can be used with the
"internal-type" decimal point)

Final question (if the previous doesn't help).
  Is the "B" actually in your PICTURE clause - or did you just put it in the
note to let us see where the PIC ended?  Again, I don't think you can use the
"B" (external format) symbol with the "V" symbol.
```

#### ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83vnh8$2613$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Not wishing to be accused of doing any of *my* work at the
moment, I decided to look at different ways to accomplish
what seems the intended goal: If the value is zero, show a
single "0" in the 18th position of a 19 position field.

Each of these options was checked in a program with ANS85
flagging active. The original field was unchanged, that is,
    PIC ----------------V--B.

Option 1: In the redefinition, insert a B between the V and
the 9, actually anywhere to the left of the 9.
    PIC ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZVB9B.
This could be confusing; but inserting the B is the smallest
change that could be made to the program to have the "0"
display in the correct position.

Option 2: In the redefintion, replace the picture clause with
something different.
    PIC BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB9B.
This shows that only one digit is to be presented; but it is
rather difficult to spot the 9.

Option 3: Use a conditional to set the value in the original
field.
    88 FORCE-2-ZERO VALUE "                 0 ".
Then use SET FORCE-2-ZERO TO TRUE in the procedure
division. No redefinition is required; but it is a most unusual
use of a conditional.

Option 4: Redefine the field as alpha-numeric.
    PIC XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX.
then, in the procedure division, MOVE "                 0 " to the
field. This, at least, clearly shows the intended result.

Reference modification could be used; but was not otherwise
considered.

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

Eileen Preston wrote in message ...
>We're still converting some VS COBOL programs to OS/390 COBOL
>and have run into a situation that isn't making any sense.   Of course
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any suggestions?
```

#### ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<840t9v$1d0_001@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
In article <s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>,
   "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
+We're still converting some VS COBOL programs to OS/390 COBOL and have run 
into a situation that isn't making any sense.   Of course looking up the 
documentation hasn't been a joy either.
+
+There is a field defined as:  PIC ----------------V--B (that's 16 hypens 
before the V and 2 after)
+
+It is redefined as:  PIC ZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZV9B (that's 16 Zs before the V).
+
+The error is that the '9' is out of place.  The redefine was changed to put 
in a VZ9 but it doesn't like that either, putting in a VZBB makes it happy but 
the user wants to see a zero if the field is zero.
+
+Any suggestions?
+
For readability, it is customary to use a repetition factor. e.g. -(16), 
instead of repeating the character, if more than two or three occur in a row. 
In my experience, most programmers will write PIC XX, PIC XXX, and PIC X(4), 
but never PIC XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX -- always PIC X(16) instead.

As has been pointed out by at least one other poster, the combination of - and 
V, or Z and V, in a PICture string is improper, and the V should be changed to 
 in each of the above strings. Even so, it's unlikely that you will achieve 
the results you desire -- a value of negative 0.01 will print as <18spaces>-1, 
for example.

I think what you're really after is just one PICture: "PIC -(16).99".
Using that, here's how various values should print:
+1.00	prints as                1.00
-1.00	prints as               -1.00
zero    prints as                 .00
+0.01	prints as                 .01
-0.01   prints as                -.01
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** "john doe" <someone@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<845quq$92d$1@news.smartworld.net>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <840t9v$1d0_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
Elaine ,
   Has any of this worked for you.
Im watching from the interest of learning from it.
thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Picture Clause Format - Redefinition Problem

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3868D82D.B417F9B1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<s8620306.029@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <840t9v$1d0_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
Doug Miller wrote:
> 
> For readability, it is customary to use a repetition factor. e.g. -(16),
> instead of repeating the character, if more than two or three occur in a row.
> In my experience, most programmers will write PIC XX, PIC XXX, and PIC X(4),
> but never PIC XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX -- always PIC X(16) instead.

One exception I have seen (and done):

05  my-field      pic $,$$$,$$$,$$$.99.
05  my-field-r    pic xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx  redefines my-field. 

With the visible alignment this can be readable.

It's nice to make pictures similar to make it easy to add up sizes when
appropriate:
05   my-field-1   pic x(01).
05   my-field-2   pic x(20).
05   my-field-3   pic 9(05).

It is easy to add these together.  Can't always do this, but it still is
a nice technique to put in the (01).
```

## Thread: Help converting COBOL data to ASCII text. — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help converting COBOL data to ASCII text.

- **From:** theo_kolettis@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83thqm$l24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello,

I'm wondering if anyone can offer some advice converting Cobol data
made by a COBOL program into an ascii text file.  Reading the file
through the DOS EDIT program, has mostly usable data and a little bit
of garbage.  If the file was in binary format, I wouldn't have expected
to find any information that I could read, but as it happens, the most
important information is in gibberish. Coincidence?

I wrote a COBOL program to read the data but I can't seem to get
readable information.  Is there a special way to declare the input or
output files?  I've looked at all the COBOL books that I own, and I
can't seem to find any information on this subject.

Thanks in advance for any help.

Theo


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help converting COBOL data to ASCII text.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m6u84.624$PI1.12931@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<83thqm$l24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You can download a free tutorial on COBOL data types from
www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html

Get file COBDATA.ZIP, explode (er, un-zip) and read the text and/or play
with the graphics.

Then, if you still need to convert the numeric data from 'COBOL  datatypes'
to 'IEEE datatypes', I have a tutorial on that, as well as a Windows 32-bit
DLL to handle it on the PC side. On a mainframe, you can actually unpack and
format the data using something like SyncSort, provided you get all the
field sizes and types correct.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help converting COBOL data to ASCII text.

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3862F181.727FE50@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<83thqm$l24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
theo_kolettis@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> can't seem to find any information on this subject.

Cobol data file is a sequence of records, possibly compressed,
possibly indexed. They are not supposed to be readable by
a human. Only Cobol programs are suposed to read them.

We developed a series of converters that can read Cobol data files
and convert them to text or DBF format, or even make them readable
to Crystal Reports.

All you need to have is your Cobol data file and at least one 
Cobol program in source that can read or write this file.
Then our converters would be able to read this file.

More on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: Help converting COBOL data to ASCII text.

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3863AAAB.915@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<83thqm$l24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
theo_kolettis@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to find any information that I could read,

You'll be able to read stuff like name and address information, even in a "binary" file.

> but as it happens, the most
> important information is in gibberish. Coincidence?No, the numeric data is likely to be binary.

You can probably unscramble this into a ASCII text (including the numbers in a way 
appropriate to import them into something else) by using the "binary input" input option 
of a generic parser/converter like ParseRat (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm).

It will assist you to identify header length (if any), record length, field positions 
and encoding method and will then permit you to export as fixed format, delimited, dBase 
or MailMerge formats.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help converting COBOL data to ASCII text.

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6hpch825k253@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<83thqm$l24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

> I'm wondering if anyone can offer some advice converting Cobol data
> made by a COBOL program into an ascii text file.

Give me the COBOL program that writes the COBOL data, I will give you
the COBOL program that converts the files into ASCII text you can read
in Notepad or Word or WordPerfect or DOS or VB or VC++ or Borland C
editors, or DOS editor, or Lotus 1-2-3 or Exel worksheet.

It takes one day to do that. I do for free. It is basic COBOL, but I
cannot give advice, for it is several pages in COBOL books first few
chapters, in DATA DIVISION and WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
```

## Thread: [OT] JCL Override Not... Overriding? — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [OT] JCL Override Not... Overriding?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jFr84.18223$W2.128257@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

Greets, all... I am banging my head against the wall with something I
encountered years back and have since solved and forgotten.

I have a proc which contains:

//QZLQSORT  EXEC PGM=SYNCSORT,COND=(0,NE,VSVRFY),PARM='CENTWIN=1950',
//            REGION=4096K                                           
//SORTIN   DD UNIT=CART,DISP=(OLD,KEEP),
//            DSN=UT.FR.&INDATA1        

... and I want to override this DSN with another one.  In the JCL I've
coded:

//QZLQSORT.SORTIN DD UNIT=CART,DISP=(OLD,KEEP), 
//       DSN=MYUID.OVERRIDE.DSN                 

... and it just doesn't 'take'; when I SUB the job the expansion shows:

XXQZLQSORT  EXEC PGM=SYNCSORT,COND=(0,NE,VSVRFY),PARM='CENTWIN=1950', 
XX            REGION=4096K                                            
//QZLQSORT.SORTIN DD UNIT=CART,DISP=(OLD,KEEP),                       
//       DSN=MYUID.OVERRIDE.DSN                                       
X/SORTIN   DD UNIT=CART,DISP=(OLD,KEEP),                              
X/            DSN=UT.FR.&INDATA1                                      
IEFC653I SUBSTITUTION JCL - UNIT=CART,DISP=(OLD,KEEP),DSN=UT.FR.DELETEME

(I coded symbolic INDATA1 to DELETEME for emphasis)

The substitution JCL and the X/ (instead of XX) shows me that the override
isn't... overriding.

Suggestions?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] JCL Override Not... Overriding?

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#B2L#6XT$GA.355@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<jFr84.18223$W2.128257@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Offhand, I can't see what's wrong.  Nightly JCL problems solving was a
couple of years back though . . .

However, there might be another problem.  If there are "other" overrides in
the runlib JCL for a specific step, and those overrides do not occur "ddname
order", then all those that are out of order "will not take".  However, to
hear this problem, it sounds like you are doing only a single override for
this step, so this isn't likely to be the problem.

Instead using an override of:

..//qzlqsort.sortin dd unit=cart,disp=(old,keep),
           dsn=myuid.override.dsn

try:

..//qzlqsort.sortin dd dsn=myuid.override.dsn


I don't think you need to override parms that aren't changed, and since the
first two remain the same, there isn't a need to override them.

I wish I had a mainframe (or emulator) handy to try this out on some test
JCL, I'm sure I could get it working in a couple of minutes.


My other suggestion is this: the DD before UNIT=CART appears to begin in
column 11 (unless it's my text editor screwing things up).  If you move that
over one to column 12, that may help, too.

> Greets, all... I am banging my head against the wall with something I
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Hi:

	First I want to wish you a "Joyous Holiday" and "Happy New Year."

	I've been following your quest for an answer. I've been a programmer
for the past 30 years. Most of it on IBM mainframe with different MVS
systems. Your override question is working. The overridden DD shows the
override by substituting the XX for X/. However, since the override was
through JCL and not the standard like INDATA1=XYZ, it won't show the
override in the list of overrides. Instead it's shown as the statement
coded the way you've put it into your JCL. If you've checked your
dataset you would have seen that the program read your file and not the
original one coded in the PROC.

Howard:

	There are two types of overrides. The first deals with typing in the
PROC override. These are coded at the top of the PROC and allow the
development of flexible JCL. The second is a JCL override which was used
by docdwarf. In this case the only way you know the override took place
is through the listing that can be printed by specifying MSGLEVEL=(1,1)
on the JOB card.

	Have a great New Year!

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> program using the same JCL to do a test.

#### ↳ Re: [OT] JCL Override Not... Overriding?

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)

- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12cc3e6a8c956c7989696@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<jFr84.18223$W2.128257@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In article <jFr84.18223$W2.128257@iad-read.news.verio.net>, 
docdwarf@clark.net says...
> 
> Greets, all... I am banging my head against the wall with something I
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The results you have posted here tell me you have overridden the JCL 
correctly.  "X/" means this line has been overridden by the "//" lines 
that preceed it.

Different subject, but it concerns me that you guys use a fixed date 
window of "1950".  We use CENTWIN=79, which applys a sliding date window 
instead of fixed.  It tells syncsort that the year must be from today/yy-
79 thru today/yy+20.   The only business case that would call for a fixed 
window of 1950, would be if you have masterfiles that must hold 1950 data 
forever, in which case full date expansion will be required by 2049, and 
you are only buying yourself time to retire.

Pete
```

## Thread: Visualage for Cobol or Micro Focus Mainframe Express — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Visualage for Cobol or Micro Focus Mainframe Express

- **From:** Herman <herros@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83tbcs$gh9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,

Can anybody tell me where to find some stuff about a compare being done
between IBM's Visualage for Cobol and Merant's (Micro Focus) Mainframe
Express. Maybe someone even did it and wants to share is results with
me ?

Thanks in advance


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Visualage for Cobol or Micro Focus Mainframe Express

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83tprk$c4l$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<83tbcs$gh9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
A couple of things that I can say (before others jump in)

1) Do you use OS/VS COBOL or CMPR2 on the mainframe?  If so, you need Micro
Focus to emulate these (IBM doesn't support these with VA).

2) Do you need to interface with Assembler (S/370 type) in your applications
on the PC? If so, you need Micro Focus (IBM doesn't support this).

3) Do you need a (fairly full) set of LE callable services?  If so, you need
Micro Focus - IBM doesn't support these on the PC.

4) Do you want a JCL emulator on the PC? If so, go with Micro Focus.

5) Do you want the SAME compiler (front end) on the PC as on the Mainframe
(i.e. messages will be IDENTICAL) between PC and mainframe?  Then use IBM
(Micro Focus "emulates" stuff on the mainframe, IBM uses the same front end).

5)Do you want a choice between IEEE and S/390 floating-point storage types?
If so, go with IBM.  Micro Focus *only* supports the S/390 format (when in
mainframe emulator mode).

6) If you are interested in IMS, CICS, DB2 or PL/I "interfaces", then both
products supply these, but you will need to look at the pros and cons of how
they do it.  (Again, in MOST cases, Micro Focus uses "emulators" while IBM
uses the "real thing").

7) Both products have "remote file access" (i.e. your PC product can access
"real" mainframe files and databases, not down-loaded copies) but the ease of
doing this and the resources on the mainframe will vary.

8) If you are interested in mainframe compatible OO syntax, then go with IBM.
Micro Focus does NOT provide syntax support for IBM's current OO syntax.

9) Are you trying to produce a "production" executable on the PC?  If so,
look strongly at IBM.  Micro Focus' current "mainframe development product"
does NOT produce executables (although their older products did).  Therefore,
you might need BOTH NetExpress and Mainframe Express if you want to do BOTH
mainframe development and PC development with COBOL.

   ***

Bottom-line is that both have PROs and CONs.
  You may ALSO want to look at CA-Realia if you are thinking about products
in this area.  It has its own PROs and CONs.

There are other "details" that you will need to look at, but I thought this
would get the discussion going.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Visualage for Cobol or Micro Focus Mainframe Express

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83ua3v$83l$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<83tbcs$gh9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <83tprk$c4l$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A few more PROs and CONs that I have thought about:

A) If you use the MLE (Millennium Language Extensions) that are "native" in
IBM Mainframe COBOLs, then you will need to use the IBM product on the PC.
(Micro Focus doesn't provide emulation for them.)

B) The "professional" (?) version of the IBM product includes a PC version of
the mainframe "CCCA" product can provide automated source code conversion
from the "older" IBM dialects to the newer ones.

C) The Micro Focus product includes (or at least used to include) both WFL
(the product for converting files from various formats to others - including
EBCDIC/ASCII "intelligent" conversions) and the data-file editor (lets you
edit data files based on their record descriptions).  I don't know for sure,
but I don't think that the IBM products include such facilities.

D) It may show my background (Xpediter and Micro Focus) but in my opinion the
IBM debugger verges on the "user-hostile" while the Micro Focus Animator
(both the older character mode one and newer GUI one) is QUITE user-friendly
and intuitive.  On the other hand, the IBM product provides "remote"
debugging of the actual mainframe object code (no "emulation" of machine
instructions) and does provide better inter-language capabilities (the same
interface for PL/I, C, *and* COBOL).

   ***

I may still add to this thread if I think of more items to consider.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

> <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Training needs always depend upon the software needs of your shop.

My shop writes new Mainframe/CICS/COBOL programs every time we write a 
new client/server application.  Our PowerBuilder programs on the network 
call these Mainframe/CICS/COBOL programs as data-retrieval/data-update 
subroutines so all corporate data is in one central location.

A study of pseudo-conversational programming for these new COBOL programs 
is moot, since they are not managing terminal communications.  But we do 
have a lot of pseudo-conversational COBOL/CICS programs running in 
production.

Sorry Greas, but I don't have any book recomendations.  :-(

Pete
```

## Thread: Reduce Initial CODE Cobol CICS — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Reduce Initial CODE Cobol CICS

- **From:** Andreas Lerch <andreaslerch@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991224.17011513@mis.configured.host>`

```
Hello,

My environment is:

        MVS - OS/390 2.4
        CICS-Transaction-Server
        COBOL-II - Compiler
        LE/370 Run-Time

I have the following problem. COBOL-II in CICS.

Cobol-Programms must be compiled with special options for a CICS
enviroment.

The load-module does not contain any space for the working-storage
section. Every time, the programm ist used for a transaktion, there
is a getmain and a LOT of instuctions to fill the value-fields from
litaral pool.

My question now. Is there any way to split a working-storage section
in two parts?

One for the modifyable variables and another one for read-only
variables, that can not modified.

My intention is, to reduce the proloque for any transaction.

In a batch-environment, i only have this Problem, when i code:
PROGRAMM-ID xyz IS INITIAL.

Sorry about my english, it's not my first language.

Thank you all, for answering.

- have a nice (xmas) day - Andreas Lerch - Germany
```

#### ↳ Re: Reduce Initial CODE Cobol CICS

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<07F25CBCECB50A3F.A4B25695C16763E1.13DA60B5466E3C30@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<19991224.17011513@mis.configured.host>`

```
On Fri, 24 Dec 1999 17:01:15 GMT, Andreas Lerch
<andreaslerch@t-online.de> enlightened us:

>Hello,
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I may not be understanding this completely, but if, as I suspect, your
GETMAIN is getting storage for an area in your Linkage Section, there
is no reason why you can't define your fixed variables in WORKING
STORAGE, i.e. 05 Field-1  PIC X Value '?'.  Whether they are
modifiable or not depends on how you code the program.

Regards and Happy Holidays (Frohe Weihnachten und glï¿½ckliches neues
Jahr)


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Two wrongs don't make a right, but two Wrights made an airplane.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Reduce Initial CODE Cobol CICS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<840ms1$ghg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19991224.17011513@mis.configured.host>`

```
Like the other posting a reply, I may be misunderstanding your problem.  I
*think* you are saying that you have defined Working-Storage Section with
both "modifiable" and "non-modifiable" data.  BECAUSE CICS requires COBOL
programs to be "re-entrant" - each time the transaction starts COBOL itself
does a "getmain" for the entire WS and initializes all the fields with VALUE
clauses in them.  You want to avoid the getmain and initialization each time
the transaction is started - for those items that are never modified (so
don't need a separate "copy" in order to be properly re-entrant).

*IF* that is the correct statement of your problem, what I would suggest is
that you create a program or part of your logic that is executed the first
time the transaction is invoked do the following:

  - use an EXEC CICS GETMAIN to get enough storage for your NON-modifiable
stuff
    (This will end-up in your Linkage Section, not WS)
  - Put the address (and length - if variable) in your com-area (or someplace
else that each iteration of the transaction can get)
  - Do "MOVEs" to initialize the values in that storage.

Then for the 2nd thru Nth iteration of the transaction, you can just do a SET
ADDRESS OF Linkage Section "item" and it will have the same values as the
last time (and you won't go thru any new GETMAINs or initialization)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reduce Initial CODE Cobol CICS

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3868DA1B.662A8082@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<19991224.17011513@mis.configured.host> <840ms1$ghg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 

I have worked in environments (Univac?) where we had large LINKAGE
SECTIONs, which contained Working-storage type data.   These were in
re-entrant programs.  I'm not sure which was which now, but either
WORKING-STORAGE or LINKAGE was initialized with each thread run, but not
the other.

The only COBOL programs I have ever cared about being multi-threaded are
on-line.  For most of my IBM career I have used ADS/O instead of COBOL
for on-line, with a few IDMS-DC called COBOL programs which we treat as
though they are not multi-threaded.
```

Hello,

thank you for answering.

I think there is no technique, to have static data that is only
loadable.

I dont talk about transactions, invoked 10 or 100 times a day. I want
to reduce the code at transaktions and submodules, invoked 100.000
times and more - a day.

The second is, that the programmers-quallification is
problem-orientated. Not specialized on performance facts.

The only way, i think, to place tables in assembler loadable modules;
load and point via Linkage-Section.

So on, thank you all.

- have a nice day - Andreas Lerch

#### ↳ Re: Reduce Initial CODE Cobol CICS

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38645F33.9F4312CB@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<19991224.17011513@mis.configured.host>`

```
Andreas Lerch wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> in two parts?

The simple answer is NO. W-S is a single entity to a COBOL program, and
is obtained by a single GETMAIN if you're re-entrant. I think the
compiler has already done (partly) what you're asking about. The
template for W-S which is part of the load module tells how to
initialize/populate W-S after it's obtained, using both the literal pool
& masks. Aside benefit to having programs RENT is that the load module
doesn't include W-S, just what's needed to initialize it after the
GETMAIN. We saved a lot of loadlib space when we switched our batch
programs from NORENT to RENT (check out something with some big tables
in W-S).

Bill Lynch

(Note: I used the terms I did above conceptually, "template", "mask",
etc. I don't know which terms are used by the compiler designers)
```

#### ↳ Re: Reduce Initial CODE Cobol CICS

- **From:** Jerry@zonko.co.uk (Jerry Ozaniec)
- **Date:** 1999-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<memo.19991225084508.185B@zonko.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19991224.17011513@mis.configured.host>`

```
In article <19991224.17011513@mis.configured.host>, 
andreaslerch@t-online.de (Andreas Lerch) wrote:

> The load-module does not contain any space for the working-storage
> section. Every time, the programm ist used for a transaktion, there
> is a getmain and a LOT of instuctions to fill the value-fields from
> litaral pool.

The only way you can reduce the overhead is:

1. Remove any unnecessary VALUE clauses.

2. Use literals rather than data names.

Jerry Ozaniec
Zonko Enterprises Limited
```

<a id="t-2fc7531e73"></a>
## Thread: Help - 'Source Format Free' — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help - 'Source Format Free'

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BRL84.157$Nj3.4228@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
12/24/99

Can anyone tell me the 'rules' used for "free format" source code?  What I
mean is, when NOT using 'free format' the rules state that columns 1-6 don't
count, col  7 may be D, *,C or - (continuation) , level indicators '01' or
'77' must appear in columns 8-12, other levels columns 13-71 (well, less if
you need a dataname and/or PICTURE clause), and a data name definition is
terminated only by a full stop.

For example, using 'free format'  could I have on one line something like:

 01  GROUP-ONE .     05  FIRST-ITEM   PIC X(02)    05 SECOND-ITEM PIC 9(04).

Or, does 'free format' really only mean that '01' and other level numbers
may appear as the first literal in a line of source? or that lines may
extend beyond column 72?

Can comment lines be included within  the elementary items of a group item
by using column 7, as in:

col 1-7

01   group-item.
    05  first-thing   pic x(01).
         *  comment            <<<  * is in column 7
   05   second-thing   pic x(012).


I am updating my record analyzer (yes, finally creating the long-awaited
Windows(r) version)  and wanted to include this option.

Also, although I know I could look it up, the in-line comment indicator for
the next standard is '*>' ? or is it '>*' ?
Are other comment indicators supported?

I am only interested in the DATA DIVISION, and any copybooks used therein.

And as if I haven't asked for enough already, could you indicate which brand
compiler and/or platform the rules apply to?

If there's a web site where I can look it up myself, that's fine.

(Hey, what comes out of this will be free, so it's not like I'm in this for
money!)

ALSO (and this is a REAL inquiry about your preference):  Should I store
user settings (e.g., last five files, COMP alignment, etc)  in the system
registry or in a (editable text) *.INI file?  The software will be a single
Windows 32-bit executable file, no extra DLL's required (I don't use
'Microsoft Visual anything' to produce it, so you don't need 2Mb of run-time
support files).

Regards
Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Help - 'Source Format Free'

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<840auh$5se$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<BRL84.157$Nj3.4228@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Concerning free format (as defined in the draft Standard -- and as I think
that Micro Focus has it implemented - mostly)  (Fujitsu and other vendors
have different rules, I think).

1) The full draft Standard can be reached from:
  http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

2) Now for the rules,

A) "data" can start in any column and go to any column and be continued in
any column.  Therefore, the following is "valid". "*>" is the inline comment
indicator and ">>D" is the new debugging line indicator.

1 Group1. 2
Elem Pic
X. 02 Elem2 pic X. 01 Another-Group. 05 another-elem *> a comment at end of
line
pic
*> a comment line
       x
>>D Value "A"  *> on a debugging line
    .
05 Elem2  Pic
    X(20) Value "New Type"-
   "of continuation line".

Now, I don't think many people would program that way, but it does show many
of the possible variations.

B) It is also "legal" to switch between the two types of format in the middle
of a data item, e.g.

01 Group. 05
Elem1
>>DIR Source format  is fixed
       Pic X. 05 Elem2  *> was always legal but not used much
>>Source free
      Pic 9.

C) in the draft of the next Standard (and several vendors already do this).
The entire A-margin/B-margin rules have been removed.  Therefore, an 01-level
can start in column 13 and an 05-level can start in margin 8.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help - 'Source Format Free'

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3868D46D.624A6441@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<BRL84.157$Nj3.4228@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <840auh$5se$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Oh goodie, now we won't be able to do column searches for paragraph
headers, and we will be able to disguise our data formats, increasing
our job security by making our code hard to read.

Still need to go a ways to match obscurity trends of the "in" languages.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help - 'Source Format Free'

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<840tmj$1d0_002@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<BRL84.157$Nj3.4228@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <BRL84.157$Nj3.4228@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
   "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
+12/24/99
+
+Can anyone tell me the 'rules' used for "free format" source code?  What I
+mean is, when NOT using 'free format' the rules state that columns 1-6 don't
+count, col  7 may be D, *,C or - (continuation) , level indicators '01' or
+'77' must appear in columns 8-12, other levels columns 13-71 (well, less if
+you need a dataname and/or PICTURE clause), and a data name definition is
+terminated only by a full stop.
+
+For example, using 'free format'  could I have on one line something like:
+
+ 01  GROUP-ONE .     05  FIRST-ITEM   PIC X(02)    05 SECOND-ITEM PIC 9(04).

If you write code like this, everyone who maintains it will curse your name 
and desire to commit physical violence upon your person. This is AWFUL. 
Whether you *can* do this or not is immaterial: this is bad code, and you 
should not do it.

Not to mention which, you omitted a period after the first 05-level item. 
That's part of the reason this is bad -- it makes it hard to spot mistakes 
like that.
```

<a id="t-64d5f45bf1"></a>
## Thread: mf Variable File Formats — 1 msgs

### Re: mf Variable File Formats

- **From:** "E. Johnson" <lizrj@utinet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s67lcg77h4583@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> Then MOVE the length of each file to be written to WS-File-Size before
doing
> your WRITE statement.  After each READ WS-File-Size will contain the size
of
> the record that was read.

> I have NOT changed the location of NOTE-LENGTH, but unless you really have
a
> system that can handle that, I *strongly* recommend that you put the
"object
> of the ODO" *before* the ODO itself - and not after it.

Bill, seriously, thanks for the input, I can completely understand now.
Really the record needs neither the array size nor the record size and I
will get rid of them and store them in working storage instead.  Big thanks
go to you for helping me in an non-judgemental way.

As to the other post ...

>  I can,
> however, tell when something works or not.

> Obviously not.  have you actually looked at the data file itself and
noticed the last 8 bytes of every record is rubbish left over from a
previous record ?

First of all, I just want to thank you for being so kind to me.  In a
profession I'm having to learn by myself as I go, it's very reassuring to
know there will be people out there willing to lend me a helpful hand.
Really Richard, you just went the extra mile to make me feel comfortable and
welcome.

However, I believe that yes, the program does work.  That is because I
define work to mean that the user can input and retrieve notes to and from
the file without losing any data and that the file is not storing the max
record every time it writes.  Both are important to me because we store info
such as cut size, pick up dates, installer info, etc and we store them in a
lot of places.  That I am storing an extra 8 bits of data out on the record
is annoying but not of a huge import.  Actually, when weighed against the
rest on my plate, it's small potatoes.

Yes, I do learn by trial and error.  But this is not by choice.  And I'd
hazard a guess to say many are in my same position:  programming for your
own company and not having the funds or the time for training.  So I've
learned as I've gone and programmed as fast as I could.  Anyone who is doing
MF Cobol, or really any sort of cobol, can tell you how very useful the
documentation is out there for teaching yourself.   So no, I'm not a
professional, but I'm working on it.  And the lesson for today is to treat
others as you would like to be treated.  I'm afraid my sarcastic post (then
and now) is in response to your attitude.  I guess in light of the lesson
for the day, I'll just try and be pleasant and say, 'thanks for the help'
and leave it at that.

~Elizabeth
```

---

<a id="t-6f3b897a46"></a>
## Thread: 2000 = millennium? — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### 2000 = millennium?

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3864F720.53FB16B2@worldnet.att.net>`

```
From CNN.online:

Pope rings in millennium in Vatican City

Pope John Paul II on Friday opened the Holy Door of St. Peter's Basilica
in a solemn Christmas Eve ceremony to begin the Roman Catholic Church's
millennium celebrations. The 79-year-old pontiff opened the door, which
remains closed except during Holy Years, and then prayed before it to
begin what he has proclaimed to be the Church's "Great Jubilee of the
Year 2000." Meanwhile, thousands of people turned out to celebrate 2000
years of Christianity at midnight Mass in Bethlehem. 

Since the millennium we're debating is of the CE and the calendar we're
using was originally developed for the Popes, I thought it of interest
that the current Pope regards next year (2000) as the millennium. Good
enough for me.

Bill Lynch
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84fnqs$1vk_003@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<3864F720.53FB16B2@worldnet.att.net> <386A15F3.EC3AC8D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <84diuk$659$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <84enr6$d28$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <84enr6$d28$1@news.igs.net>,
   "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
+
+Richard Plinston wrote in message <84diuk$659$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
+>:> Meanwhile, thousands of people turned out to celebrate 2000
+>:> years of Christianity at midnight Mass in Bethlehem.
+>
+>There seems to be a bug in their computer.  It has now been
+>only 1999 years since JC's birth (given it was 25/12/0).
+>
+>As he started his ministry when around 30 years old then there
+>has been around 196? years of 'Christianity'.
+
+
+Christ, he would have been appalled at the idea of "Christianity".  Christ
+taught "love thy neighbour".  "Christianity" teaches "love thy church", and
+if thy neighbour is not a member, fuck them.  That all started well after he
+died; don't blame the poor bugger for that.
+
Christianity teaches nothing of the sort. I'm sorry you've had such a 
negative experience with people *claiming* to be Christians, but if that's the 
attitude they display, I'd have to question whether they truly *are* 
Christians.

A few months ago, my church helped organize a large group of volunteers who 
spent a rainy Saturday weatherstripping, winterizing, and making general 
repairs to the homes of needy people in the inner city -- exactly *none* of 
whom are members of our church, or possibly of any church. We did this simply 
because it's the right thing to do, in accordance with Christ's admonition 
that "in as much as you did it for the least of my brethren, you did it for 
me." This is what Christianity is really about. 

Please remember that that hypocrites abound, and try to judge the value of any 
teaching (religious or otherwise) by the behavior of those who truly follow 
it, not those who merely give lip service to its ideals while living something 
entirely different. I once knew a young woman who carefully followed the 
guidelines of the latest weight-loss diet book -- except for a pint of ice 
cream every night -- and managed to gain weight anyway. Because the diet was 
worthless? No, because she didn't follow its teaching. The same holds true for 
religious teachings. We should no more judge Christianity by the behavior of 
those pseudo-Christians you describe, than we should judge Islam by the 
behavior of a handful of terrorists -- or a diet book by the actions of 
someone who eats a pint of ice cream every night.
```

<a id="t-55d5bd948b"></a>
## Thread: Using a Module in CICS and Batch with File I/O — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Using a Module in CICS and Batch with File I/O

- **From:** Andreas Lerch <andreaslerch@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 1999-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991225.14551742@mis.configured.host>`

```
Hello,

i think i have found a way to use the same module - subprogramm - in
CICS-Online and Batch - with file access.

This is no idea, i have created one module like the following, to make
file-access in batch and online. The data-access is different, the
data-modifikation is the same.

In Batch and Online, i need a base-date, which is stored in a
vsam-file. The submodule reads a record, incrementes a date und
rewrite the record.


Main-module - Batch:

                SET             C-INVOKE-BATCH          TO TRUE
                CALL            C-MODULE        USING           C-AREA
                END-CALL

Main-module - CICS-Online:

                SET             C-INVOKE-CICS           TO TRUE
                SET             C-DFHEIBLK      TO ADDRESS OF 
DFHEIBLK.
                CALL            C-MODULE        USING           C-AREA
                END-CALL

Submodule: (must be compiled for CICS)

        File-Secion.
                ASSIGN...
                FD
                ... and anything else you want.

        Linkage Section.
                COPY            DFHEIBLK.               (Inserted by 
translator)
                DFHCOMMAREA.
                05              C-Module                PIC X(08)      
 VALUE 'SUBPGM'.
                05              C-RELEASE               PIC X(02)      
 VALUE '01'.
                05              C-INVOKE                PIC X.
                        88      C-INVOKE-BATCH                  VALUE 
'B'.
                        88      C-INVOKE-CICS                   VALUE 
'C'.
                05              C-DFHEIBLK              USAGE POINTER.

        PROCEDURE DIVISION USING DFHEIBLK DFHCOMMAREA.

The first instruktion in Procedure-Division must be the following.
This must be done, to access the first parameter. Because the
CICS-translator inserts a modified 'PROCEDURE DIVISION'.

                SET ADDRESS OF DFHCOMMAREA TO ADDRESS OF DFHEIBLK.

                EVALUATE TRUE
                 WHEN           C-INVOKE-CICS
                        SET ADDRESS OF DFHEIBLK TO C-DFHEIBLK
        now i can use every components from CICS - like EXEC-CICS 
READ...
                 WHEN           C-INVOKE-BATCH
        and here i can use OPEN CLOSE READ and so on for BATCH...
                END-EVALUATE.

There are several other ways to do this job. This was a test and it
works fine.

Reference: CICS Application Programming Guide: Nesting - what the
application programmer must do.


- have a nice day - Andreas Lerch
```

#### ↳ Re: Using a Module in CICS and Batch with File I/O

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<842p7j$qg9$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19991225.14551742@mis.configured.host>`

```
FYI,
  Depending on your COBOL compiler, you may not even be able to TRANSLATE and
COMPILE a program with SELECT/ASSIGN and/or FDs.  If your site has and uses
the
    WORD(CICS)
compiler option, trying to use this "stuff" will cause a compile-time error.
```

```

Hello,

yes that is true, it's an idea to use complex logic in different 
environments.

If you use DB/2 in CICS and Batch you need different Loadmodules 
because DSNHLI? is part of the runtime. DL/I (ie ASMTDLI / CBLTDLI) 
you can invoke dynamic.

few month - will solve all y2000 problems....
