<a id="t-b4599bb00c"></a>
## Thread: Windows - OR is it me ? — 25 this month / 56 total

### Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```
The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is the
Windowing, not the OO.

Those that use the various forms of windowing, Flexus, APIs, GUIs,
Dialog System, Dialog Editor, Panels etc.  - care to share your
experiences on how you got up and running on Windows ? Even at this
stage of the game, I keep my windowing techniques to a minimum , not
using many features that are available, and only selecting from them as
I specifically need one.

So - how did you start, what phases if any, did you progress through -
Ken Mullins care to tell me about you API learning curve, Thane, Judson,
Cheesle, Ib and others - your path to Windows ? Simon, my young friend
in Norfolk, your learning curve ? (And other than those texts which only
quote C examples, any suggested 'Windows in 24 Hours' books ?).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3866c242.23157804@allnews.nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```
When we decided to convert all our DOS applications to GUI we looked at
several tools on the market.  Our goal was to convert to GUI with
minimal training on our part and be able to move our application  from
one platform to another with minimal changes.

We ended up choosing Flexus because of minimal training and we don't
have to worry of APIs, etc.  We started with a small project and once we
got familiarize with the tool, we started converting our character based
system to GUI.

Since then we have converted several of our mainframe applications to
GUI and still in the process of converting some of the systems.  Our
goal is to convert everything to GUI   Depending on your application or
your needs, you may need several tools to accomplish your task.   

Tony M. Mina


On Sun, 26 Dec 1999 20:34:05 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is the
>Windowing, not the OO.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<846p5s$l66$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```
I started out coding OS/2 GUI apps about 7 or 8 years ago...Started with a
simple windowed application and kept expanding...One of the first apps I
developed under OS/2 was the CNN Headline News SportsTicker (at the bottom
of the TV screen)...All native OS/2 API code in mf cobol...It is still being
used today...CNN Sports Illustrated also uses the same app...

I looked at the dialog system, but it is / was lacking a lot of the required
functionality for a true GUI application...It didn't process a lot of the
standard windows messages that you have access too with native API coding...

I also looked at using panels...Way too slow...And missing a lot of
functionality...

I don't do much OS/2 coding now, but I do a lot of Windows development...All
native APIs, except for 3rd party DLL's like "tab pro" and "list
pro"...Actually using these 3rd party controls is basically the same as
calling a MS API...

One thing I have found over the years, is that an application uses only a
very small portion of the available API calls...You create a window,
enable/disable a window, add an item to a listbox, process push buttons,
etc...Once you learn how to do these things, it's relatively simple to code
a new program...

I use the dialog editor (when it's not screwing up a resource file...:-)) to
create dialogs which become the clientarea of my application (modeless
dialogs)...I usually have a separate .cbl member per dialog and one for the
main window...It is even possible to code up an entire application without
even a messageloop...

Initially, the most confusing part to learning windows development is
getting away from batch oriented thinking to message oriented and reentrant
code thinking...The application program is no longer start here and end
there processing...the user is totally in control...The app must allow the
user to change his mind, etc...

Also, it's kind of difficult getting use to the fact that you can do a
windows function that will result in your code being reentered...The
'SendMessage' API for example...Or an API that results in other windows
messages being generated while you are still processing a message...

I'm not into OO, so I can't really compare native coding to windowing in
OO...I will say though that the OO examples I have seen to date are probably
more complex to me than calling API's...I have used my own developed
vocabulary for windows functions...for example

cmEnableWindow handle is hWindow

This verb issues a "EnableWindow" API call...Probably easier to follow and
code than the API itself...The problem I have with the vocab feature is the
slow compile times...Mostly because of the large windows copybooks
required...For that reason, I no longer use the vocab feature...

       Program-ID. "cmEnableWindow" is external.
       Special-Names.
       Call-Convention 74 is WinAPI.
       Linkage Section.
       copy "W003SD.CPY".
       01 hWindow                      handle.
       Procedure Division WinAPI using by value hWindow
                 invoked as == cmEnableWindow
                               [Windowhandle is] <hWindow> ==.
       End Program "cmEnableWindow".

kenmullins
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T5B94.398$_3.4880@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com>

> The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is
the
> Windowing, not the OO.
>

James -
After about a zillion years of COBOL batch and batch-oriented screen
work,
we made the switch to Fujitsu. The OO part is mostly invisible to us
as
FJ fences that off quite nicely. To our old, tired way of thinking,
we've had
two areas of interest to force to the front of our brains:

1. Keep as few balls in the air as possible; and
2. Remember the user can make some really crazy choices
under Windows (like clicking the 'Close Program' button in the middle
of entering a name).

The hardest parts - honestly - was learning the eccentricities of a
new
compiler and how to move stuff back and forth between a screen and
a program, at the right time, in the right place, with the right
values.

There are fun parts:
1. Designing a screen with a painter and toolkit is way better than
columns and rows.
2. Neat, ready-made stuff like progress indicators, graphs, text and
list boxes that can hold any amount of data (with the scroll bars
built in), loadable graphic images, complete control over fonts,
their sizes, and renditions, sound effects (I found a really great
bagpipe web site).
3. Thought-free linkage editting and really smart debugging.

So, what we did, was start with a basic form. As the programming
progressed we would reach a point where some feature we'd seen
in a Windows application would be useful and we'd figure out how to
implement that feature in our code. The experience with other
Windows programs told us what was doable - all we needed, then,
was to figure out how we could do it.

Our outstanding mysteries are:
1. User types something in a field and, under certain - but not rare -
conditions we don't get a chance to check it.
2. How to control the application-installation procedure.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-xqg8HYFGZ1lS@localhost>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```
On Sun, 26 Dec 1999 20:34:05, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> 
wrote:

> The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is the
> Windowing, not the OO.

> So - how did you start, what phases if any, did you progress through -
> Ken Mullins care to tell me about you API learning curve, Thane, Judson,
> Cheesle, Ib and others - your path to Windows ? Simon, my young friend
> in Norfolk, your learning curve ? (And other than those texts which only
> quote C examples, any suggested 'Windows in 24 Hours' books ?).

Well, you ask a loaded question.

I have a split work personality.  

Where I worked full time the tool of choice was MicroFocus COBOL.  
Where I worked at home, my tool of choice was CA-Realia COBOL.  Where 
I worked the history was - RM/COBOL  and CA-Realia concurrently, them 
move to MF totally.

At any rate, I have experience with 3 of these - API, Dialog System, 
and Sp2.

At Home:

When Realia released their 4.0 product, they also showed examples of 
GUI programs using the Windows API and OS/2 Presentation Manager.  I 
wanted to create GUI programs, but the API method was very very 
tedious.  It took quite some time to understand it.  The whole message
queue thing makes porting a DOS based text mode application a 
nightmare.  I had been developing text based applications using 
SCREENIO (From Realia, actually created by Norcom).  I had my user 
interface largely separated from my code - a big plus in the 
conversion game.  However, my main complaint was that I could easily 
get bogged down in User Interface development and my productivity went
way way down.  No reasonable solution in site.  (1992-1996).

At Work: 

Finally we got the impetus to move to Windows.  I had played with 1.0 
Dialog System (henceforth called DS) - and was encouraged by the fact 
that one could create text or gui applications with the same code - in
fact it would run on OS/2, Windows or Unix.  Pretty nice -- I thought.
 The tool was patently unstable.  It was virtually unusable as far as 
cross platform development.  We initially shelved it.
(1992-1996).  

When MF released 2.0 and rapidly followed with 2.5 DS we decided to 
try it again.  The boss was leaning, reluctantly, toward learning 
Visual C++, but did not like the prospects of a rewrite and struggled 
with OO.  At this same time, our West Coast parent company (from which
I had trasnfered) was developing a "replacement" for the primary 
Mainframe COBOL application.  This was in an attempt to re-license the
system to NT network users instead of Mainframe users (as we had done 
previously), and to eliminate the mainframe.  Initially the project 
was subcontracted to MicroSoft - who's team of consultants (mostly 
H1-B Visa holders) used Visual Basic.  They were fired after a year 
and a new team was hired.  The new team  used OO and MFC.  The staff 
was made up of EXPERTS in the fields.  2 of the main people had PHD's 
in OO design.  The project eventually collapsed under it's own weight 
- no one working on it under stood it.
The mainframe still runs the company.  

With these failures in sight, the decision was made to stick with 
COBOL - but what to use?  I was given the task of converting one of 
our systems from Text mode to Dialog System GUI - then with 2.5 DS.  I
did so and learned several things.

1 - I hate Dialog System.
   a.  Alien Scripting language.
   b.  Restrictive rules about order of processing.
   c.  Still unstable.

2 - Any conversion is a rewrite.

Our code was field by field accepts.  I had taught them to use the 
Screen Section, but it was only used in new systems, and not in 
existing.  It would not have helped with DS anyway.  

I learned to hate event driven programming with DS.  It's a nightmare 
of dependancies.  It's a step up from the API development method, but 
not easy.  Bottom line:  My boss asked me if it was possible to 
convert our systems using this.  My prototype showed it was, but I 
said deployment would be a nightmare, and stability was just not 
there.  (The thing never worked on 2 different machines the same 
way!).

Back at home:  

I had some demand to get my text mode applications to GUI.  I don't 
remember how I heard about Flexus, but I fould them and downloaded the
evaluation version of sp2.  What a revelation.  A GUI solution that 
worked - and worked well.  It was easy to use.  Porting my existing 
DOS mode applications was a breeze.  Having used SCREENIO, all I had 
to do was convert my screenio screens to Sp2 panels, replace the 
calls, and off I went.  SIMPLE.  I was thrilled.  Things that took me 
days to accomplish at work with DS, took minutes at home with sp2. I 
bought it.   I was very excited to tell my boss about sp2 and how well
it worked.

At work, the applications would still need to be rewritten, however.  
Field by field accepts are the problem, no matter what your solution. 
It would STILL be loads faster than DS and more stable.  But they 
turned their back on Sp2 for one simple and terrible reason - it was 
not from MicroFocus.  

I fought for another year with DS before leaving the company for 
another position.  They have yet to have the system fully converted, 
and deployment has been a nightmare, even though they have moved to 
NetExpress and the current release of DS.

Back at home, I have been happily developing Windows applications with
sp2.  I have also been able to easily switch from 16 bit Realia to 32 
bit Fujitsu because of using sp2!  Conversion is easy.

Now some general comments about Windows development.  

It's still slower than my DOS text mode development by about 1/3.  
Printing was a bear until I switched to Flexus Form Print - now it's a
breeze.
The details of the GUI are not daunting, because sp2 handles the 
tedium for me, letting me concentrate on the code.  Sp2 does not limit
my flexibility.  It's a nice balance.  

I'm getting faster developing Windows applications now, but often 
Windows gets in the way.  I remember things happening like: Windows 
automatically searches the path for data files when the file is not in
the current directory.   Drove me crazy for a while. 

One final thought.  There is one more thing you get from using sp2 
that you do not get from the API or DS approach.  Transparant 
conversion to a Thin Client application.  This is where the program 
runs on a server, and the UI exists on the clients desktop.  This is 
very cool.  
  
-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84829k$7ce$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com...
> The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is the
> Windowing, not the OO.

Which should be. OO and Windowing is two different areas, although Windowing
may be solved using OO, it is not related at all.

> So - how did you start, what phases if any, did you progress through -

Our first step was to move to the graphical environment, in that sense the
application behaved like a native windows application and not being run in a
dos session. That was fairly easy due to the portability of Acucobol.

This phase included core functionality as printer support and keyboard
management.

The further road has pretty much been built as we were walking. graphical
elements been taken into the application as it felt natural more than
anything else. The message box for instance, had been there for years,
although character based, because it was one common source, it was easily
replaced with a gui one.

More sophisticated controls has been added as the maintenance has touched
the applications.

But your question isn't really about how we did it, but with what we did it.
I don't know MF or Fujitsu or any of the others too good. They may support
it the same way that Acucobol does, I get the impression however, that both
Fujitsu and MF has moved away from Cobol, toward the structure of C++ and
pascal. And i ask myself, for what good?

You saw my earlier example code of how to do a message box in Acucobol,
DISPLAY MESSAGE BOX
compare this to the examples that there has been of how to do a message box
in MF. What is most Cobol alike?

Whean dealing with the API on the level of which is provided in the sample I
have seen of MF here in this NG, obviously, you have to do a lot of
housekeeping yourself. Freedom of course, anyone would claim freedom, as
would I, to choose to do whatever I want. Well. The price of this, is that
you yourself have to deal with the baby, when it is born, raised and at its
death.

You would probably try to avoid implementing too much new stuff at one time.
That is one of the benefits of the Acucobol approach, and which we have
used. Because the code is portable, we can move it around and apply changes
as we go. We may, but we don't have to; have concerns for messages, handling
callbacks and whatever.

I enjoy using Acucobol because I still have the feeling that I am
programming in Cobol, although I show fancy listboxes and grids and you name
it. DISPLAY and ACCEPT is what I used yesterday, and I still use it. The
most serious impact the gui stuff has had to applications that I have done
is that the exception procedures has been growing, to deal with the events,
which I of own choice has wanted to deal with.

At the same time, if I want access to the API, I can do it, like the file
functions I sent in as a demo app to the NG a while ago. From my humble
point of view, that is what Cobol should do, it should protect me from the
low end API and provide me with the tools to accomplish my everyday tasks
without forcing me into complicated construction work for even the simplest
task.

It should be no secret that I consider OO Cobol to be somewhat of a paradox,
I must admit though, that I have not been playing around with OO Cobol so
much, my experience with OO Cobol is with the code samples that has been
floating around here in this NG.

However, I am positive there is no one single answer to this. Some people
give flexibility priority above anything else, and I understand that point
of view. For myself it was more important not to be bugged with the lowlevel
stuff, if I wanted that, I could use C++ or Delphi. For me, with experience
from C++, Delphi and Acucobol. The choice is Acucobol because of the
simplicity. When I need to do advanced stuff, I got the tool for that as
well. Now, someone will point out the benefit of being able to do it all in
one language, and they definitely got a point. That is why I am happy to say
that there has been two things I have had to do externally, that is serial
communication and of all things, clipboard communication. Considering the
simplicity of the tasks that is the real reason we chose Cobol in the first
place, I feel that it is to live with.

Oh, I am talking way too much.

Listen, James, I know these things are difficult to express writing,
however, why don't you give it a try, and I will see if there is anything I
can explain. Although I don't know MF or Fujitsu too good, I feel that I
have a good idea of what Windows is all about.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** mojo@chesapeake.net (Mike Madara)
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6k286k5k290@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <84829k$7ce$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Hi all,

Per the COBOL/Windows GUI issue, my situation duplicates a lot of 
what Thane has experienced.

We are a Micro Focus COBOL shop with a large PC-based, character
app that our 'users' wanted converted to GUI.  Since we're MF based,
we gave Dialog a hard look a couple of years ago, hoping it would be
our solution.  But like Thane, the product for us was too unstable, 
especially in the development environment.  My fingers got tired
from rebooting 'cause of all the GPFs we were experiencing.
Now, these problems might be solved in the later releases of
NetExpress, but we already started the conversion using
Flexus' sp2.

Couldn't be happier.  The product is stable and the support from
Flexus is outstanding.  We did a 'proof of concept' conversion
from character to gui last year for one of the modules.  The users
loved the new gui version.  We're converting the other modules
this year.

Mojo
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

Well now.  This should be interesting, because I have been playing around
with a whole bunch of ideas, and have not come up with something that I
really like yet.

Here is the way that I am currently thinking.

Is it possible to read all the screen sections of a system (or CICs files,
or whatever), and construct a data-base of object attributes?  In effect, by
using the data base, I should be able to dynamically build a screen object,
and place on it Cobol Screen Objects. Each item in a source code screen
section has well defined attributes.  If we standardize a few methods
(accept and display come to mind), then we can test using existing systems.

SP2 looks like it could handle the actual screen methods, and has the
advantage of being multi-platform.

James J. Gavan wrote in message <38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>...
>The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is the
>Windowing, not the OO.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3867FED1.B18E90F4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <848den$i1n$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> Well now.  This should be interesting, because I have been playing around
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Many thanks to all who have responded to my original question to date.
Thane, no it wasn't meant to be a tricky question and I was interested
in your progression through different compilers. I'll comment to all
later, but I thought I should comment on Don's message at this stage.

Yes Don. It is possible! Using Dialog Editor coupled with OO/GUI I was
mulling in my mind for shortcuts. This was further strengthened by a
comment made by Ken Foskey. I'm trying to design a generic Dialog
Template, and the final link hit me about 2 hours ago. Bearing in mind
the Dialog Editor is parallel to C and C++ Dialogs with a Resource File,
if I hold W/S tables for Resource IDs, my own field numbers, and the
length of data allowed to be entered for a field. I can pass these as
parameters, (a separate group for entryfields, pushbuttons,
radiobuttons, droplists, listboxes etc.), to a class MakeDialog - bingo
! One class will generate dialogs - no details at this stage 'cos I'm
still working on it - but I'll get back. (And I'm willing to bet you can
use a similar technique with Screen Section).

I'm not suggesting OO is the panacea; with planning, I'm sure the same
approach is possible from 'Structured' - but this OO - I like, I like it
- for its flexibility. Every turn I take, I'm finding something 'new'
that I can do with it.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84968q$fd$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <848den$i1n$1@news.igs.net> <3867FED1.B18E90F4@home.com>`

```
take it a step further and include the type of control and the address of
the database field associated with the control in your ws array, length of
the data...If you do, you'll have enough info to call a generic module and
let it do the work for you...Sort of like binding a field in your record to
a control in the dialog...Similar to say visual basic...

Thats what I do...I have a "Default Dialogbox Procedure" that does most of
the windowing work...The actual code for a normal screen is simply building
the array with elements for each of the controls and pointers to their
associated database fields...No GUI code in the screen modules at all...The
"Default Dialogbox Procedure" takes care of accessing the array and placing
the data into the controls, etc...

kenmullins

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00

```
forgot to mention...the default dialog procedure also takes care of updating
your record fields when the user changes the contents of a control...It
knows from the array, what kind of control it is, where the data is stored,
it's length, etc...Then there is a callback when the user selects the OK
pushbutton for example...At that point, the record is in working storage
updated with the data the user added or changed, and simply needs to be
rewritten to your dataset...each windows message (wm-command, InitDialog,
etc) received in the screen's .cbl module is simply passed on to the default
dialogbox procedure, with a fifth field attached, that being the address of
the array...

confused yet? :-)

kenmullins

On Tue, 28 Dec 1999 02:24:08, "Ken Mullins" 
<KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote:

Sp2 works in a similar manner to what you folks have been describing -
except you don't have to worry about the API interface, the call 
interface is totally Ansi standard COBOL.  Sp2 has "panels" that are 
designed either in the panel editor or dynamically within your 
program.  You determine which functions, buttons, fields etc, should 
return control to your program after the sp2 call to "converse" the 
panel (window).   Working-Storage is updated with the data from the 
window.  Message boxes are handled with a simple call.  You also have 
easy access to the clipboard and other normal windows features.  All 
via Working-Storage and simple calls.  

Donald's idea is very easy to accomplish with Sp2.  I have a friend 
that had his own home grown ASM routines for user input for DOS.  He 
took his files and wrote a pogram to create sp2 panels from his screen
definitions.  He has a very extensive system, but he was done in a 
very short time.

Now, he's going back and adding things like tool bars, windows based 
help, etc.  This seems to be the common progression, regardless of the
tool used. 


> forgot to mention...the default dialog procedure also takes care of updating
> your record fields when the user changes the contents of a control...It
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> rewritten to your dataset...

kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FmB94.67781$eh2.1460219@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com...
> The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is the
> Windowing, not the OO.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I was involved in the development of some COBOL GUI development tools so I
had to learn the native APIs. The trickiest part was learning enough C to be
able to translate a call. Once I had that skill, the rest is an exercise of
struggling through the various references for samples that come close to
what you're trying to do, until you build up a repertoire.

A book that was a great help, and dates me, is 'The COBOL Presentation
Manager Programming Guide' by David M. Dill published by Van Nostrand
Reinhold. I suspect there's a Windows 3.x version but I doubt if there's
many other references that cover the Win32 API for COBOL programmers.

The biggest weakness of COBOL GUI programming is the lack of available
reference material. You can choose to use a third party development tool
like NetExpress, Power COBOL, Flexus, et al but they require you to learn a
particular vendor's solution to the problem, not the native API. You're also
limited to whatever they produce in the way of documentation. More
sophisticated interfaces, fancy controls like calendar functions, and
state-of-the-art stuff is usually written and documented for C++ or Visual
Basic programmers.

While I like COBOL for a lot of things and can make it do just about
anything with the Win32 API, it would not be my first choice for developing
a Win32 application. The reasons are many, but the most critical reason is
the pool of people that could maintain it in the future is quite small.
Consider a multi-tier architecture using COBOL for the business and I/O and
develop the UI using whatever tools get the job done. It's easy to call
COBOL from C++, VB or Java. A COBOL CGI front-end is also pretty
straightforward.

Karl Wagner
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386b6a1f.4953219@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <0352f008.f5eb3021@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
Lucky.Tex <lucky.texNOluSPAM@groupmail.com.invalid> wrote:

>In article <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>, "Jerry P"
><bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>front with VSAM on the back.

Howdy Tex!

Just wanted to comment that a COBOL-oriented GUI doesn't necessarily
imply a "scraped" front end.  I'm not just speaking of the sp2
solution, but also, Dialog System, Fujitsu Power COBOL, COBOL WOW from
RM, Acu's GT syntax, etc.

All of the above solutions are real programming tools which allow you
to have a completely interactive graphical user interface for your
COBOL application.  These are by no means merely "screen scrapers."

I'm sure that all of these COBOL GUI products would allow you to write
an intermediate program to emulate your existing screen handling code.
In this case, then you would be effectively "screen scraping."  But
this is an implementation issue, not a required method by any of the
above products.

Just wanted to throw in my 2 cents.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84g5vj$sfh$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <0352f008.f5eb3021@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
"Lucky.Tex" <lucky.texNOluSPAM@groupmail.com.invalid> wrote in message
> earth lasts that long, Judson).  I have progs written in the '70s that
> are still running.  But when the users start wanting GUI, I want more
> of a solution than just to scrape it.  Right now, seems like a GUI
> COBOL front end with a sql on the back.  A far cry from text on the
> front with VSAM on the back.

I guess Acucobol could give you a quickstart, as the Acu4GL package lets you
use Cobol file access syntax on SQL C/S databases. The runtime itself
provides you the opportunity to run in a gui environment with a character
dialog, or with a mixed dialog. So, when you are ready you gradually move
over to pure SQL and gui module by module.

And I am most positive this is doable with other Cobol vendors as well.

The task is doable, it is just a matter of attitude.

Cheesle
```

As you say Jerry - COBOL's demise - we now know of hundreds PLUS ONE.
Still if he does prove to be right, anticipate that for the next 90
years switching that old-fangled stuff to the new esoteric languages,
will keep current COBOL programmers nicely endowed with lots of loot.

I've had some reassurance out of this thread so far - I'm not off track
and others have indicated a gradual transition from DOS to Windows; the
problem being to become acquainted with the new animal. Arrive at a
standard methodology that suits your needs and the "beast", (Windows)
can be tamed. (I was interested reading about the other SP2 users, apart
from Thane, coming out of the woodwork. This must be a 'beautiful' day
for a certain 'Bob' <G>). 

While the Windows environment offers many 'gimmicky' controls, I get the
impression that others keep it simple - not just using gizmos because
they are there.

Essentially my problem has been lack of examples, not just current
compiler. Working at home is very different from chewing the fat with
co-workers - a quick glance at their coding and you are off to the
races. I keep plugging this one - BUT that is why we need a site with
COBOL example source code. As indicated in his message, Ken Mullins
using APIs is doing 'parallel' to what I'm proposing - I'm spending my
time re-inventing the wheel when there are probably numerous examples
out there in programmers personal 'archives'.

Thank goodness, at least, that there is access to this News Group.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386b62aa.3043819@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <38695649.85AE8B53@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>Jerry P wrote:
>> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>for a certain 'Bob' <G>). 

Jimmy:

I hope that you have noticed that I have been politely quiet and
purposely unresponsive on this issue.  This is because I believe that
it would be inappropriate for me to comment.  That would be akin to
using the newsgroup as a sales platform, which I am vehemently
against.

...but, Thane has done a wonderful job of explaining sp2 and, you are
indeed correct that it is a wonderful day....it's just too bad that
the world is going to explode in less than 48 hours.

;-)

>While the Windows environment offers many 'gimmicky' controls, I get the
>impression that others keep it simple - not just using gizmos because
>they are there.

Our viewpoint on this issue is that Windows gizmos should be used only
if they add *real* value to the application.  That is a judgement call
on the part of the programmer and end user.  In other words, "value"
is in the eye of the beholder.  If they serve no real purpose other
than to look cool, then it may be best to leave them out of the
application.

However, if the primary function of the application IS to look cool,
or if you are selling your software and "looking cool" helps you sell
a significantly greater volume of your product, then by all means,
include the gizmo.

>Essentially my problem has been lack of examples, not just current
>compiler. Working at home is very different from chewing the fat with
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thank goodness, at least, that there is access to this News Group.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-43177859ac"></a>
## Thread: Numeric-Edited in the Draft Standard (CD 1.7 w/CCP 00-100) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Numeric-Edited in the Draft Standard (CD 1.7 w/CCP 00-100)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8453nk$1sfb$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Page 334, 13.16.41.3, PICTURE clause, general rule 11a,
requires that "at least one symbol '9' and ...".

This appears to be a substantive change potentially affecting
existing programs; but is not shown, as such, in Appendix D.

The PICTURE clause -(16)V-- shows as valid when checked
with Micro Focus 3.2 and ANS85 flagging. It also appears to
be valid based upon text in both Micro Focus and IBM LRMs.
However, not having a copy of the ANS 85 standard, I cannot
compare against the previous standard.

We know from a previous thread, "Picture Clause Format -
Redefinition Problem", that at least one program uses a similar
PICTURE clause and, therefore, will be affected.

Under the draft standard, PICTURE -(16)V--, would no longer
be valid because it does not have "at least one symbol '9' and ..."
in the PICTURE clause.

Could others, in the newsgroup, verify that -(16)V-- is valid in
other compilers with ANS85 standard flagging.

Do others agree with my conclusion that this constitutes a
"substantive change potentially affecting existing programs"
and that it should be noted in Appendix D of the draft?

A copy of this post was NOT sent to the J4 chair.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric-Edited in the Draft Standard (CD 1.7 w/CCP 00-100)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<845mlk$lfm$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8453nk$1sfb$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
FYI,
  The original "report" of a problem was in going from a '74 to an '85
compiler.  (The program compiled with OS/VS COBOL which was a '74 compiler
but NOT with VS COBOL II which is an '85 compiler.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric-Edited in the Draft Standard (CD 1.7 w/CCP 00-100)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<847e1d$316p$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8453nk$1sfb$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
I submitted a separate document to J4 noting the problem
with the floating-insertion characters, "+" and "-", with respect
to general rule 11a. It was given document number J4/00-0138
for those wishing to read and comment further on the problem.
------------------
Rick Smith

Rick Smith wrote in message <8453nk$1sfb$1@news.hitter.net>...
>Page 334, 13.16.41.3, PICTURE clause, general rule 11a,
>requires that "at least one symbol '9' and ...".
>
>This appears to be a substantive change potentially affecting
>existing programs; but is not shown, as such, in Appendix D.
[...]
>A copy of this post was NOT sent to the J4 chair.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric-Edited in the Draft Standard (CD 1.7 w/CCP 00-100)

- **From:** Rosa Fischer <rosa.fischer@mch.sni.de>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38675C07.92B93C07@mch.sni.de>`
- **References:** `<8453nk$1sfb$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
I agree with you that this is a change potentially affecting existing
programs, but what is worse this rule is inconsistent with the
description of general rule 7 b floating insertion editing, general rule
8 b zero suppression and replacement editing when using "*" and table 9
floating insertion symbols 2nd row rsp column of *, +, - and cs.
"-(16)V--" is a valid picture character string in ANS85. Thank you for
this hint.

Sincerely Rosa Fischer

Rick Smith schrieb:
> 
> Page 334, 13.16.41.3, PICTURE clause, general rule 11a,
> requires that "at least one symbol '9' and ...".
```

<a id="t-b268024beb"></a>
## Thread: Observation on Using // as the COBOL Inline Comment Indicator — 1 msgs

### Observation on Using // as the COBOL Inline Comment Indicator

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<845ar1$207h$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
A previous discussion concerning the COBOL inline comment
indicator concluded by suggesting the // might be a better
indicator that *> because // is easier to type.

It is possible that // could appear in a valid picture clause.

Given:

    77 NE-ITEM PICTURE 99//99.

and

    MOVE 1234 TO NE-ITEM

the result is 12//34. Were // an inline comment, the definition
would be a syntax error. Though the above PIC may be unusual,
adopting // could change existing progams and would require
additional rules. Of course, my early suggestion of -- would
have affected many more programs.

I am still in favor of changing the inline comment indicator; but,
as usual, there is much more to the COBOL standard than
"meets the eye." As I dig ever deeper into the draft standard,
the more I appreciate the work being done by J4 and WG4.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

---

<a id="t-ef556a6515"></a>
## Thread: Merant (MF) Fileshare Crash Problem — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Merant (MF) Fileshare Crash Problem

- **From:** "Joshua Seltzer" <jseltzer@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C9330548D5C30640.70AFAF752F0BF02B.C218D8DD8F129D79@lp.airnews.net>`

```
We are running Merant  (Micro Focus) Object COBOL ver 4.1.10e (PRN 13.03) on
a SCO open server ver 5.04 system.  This server 320mg of RAM and is running
at about 11% of memory utilization.  We use this server primarily for
Fileshare and Client Server Bindings (very limited) .
We have had the application (retail POS) up and running juft fine for over 6
weeks.  On Thursday December 23 5:09pm and Friday December 24 2:00pm
(yes -peak load periods) file share crashed and left the folowing message:

Execution error: file 'cobkeymp'
error code 162, pc=0, call=0,seg=0
162 Arithmetic overflow or underflow

Fileshare Server closedown invoked
  by Stop Run processing


We were able to restart fileshare and it is running okay.  What gives?  This
is the standard Fileshare object supplied with the application server.  All
access is by TCP/IP and there are about 40 users with max file opens at
about 220.   I know this is not the very latest rev of SCO MF COBOL.  We
could not change our version in the production system untill after the
holidays and New Years.

Has anyone heard of this problem.  Merant tech support was closed for the
Holiday!
Thanks for any help.

Joshua Seltzer
Larich Distributors, Inc.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Almost, CoinCap Spam

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84akif$1sk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-uxtEDjLSQYjB@localhost> <848tps$mtm$1@news.hitter.net>`

```

> I don't even know what ERP is or why I should care.

Here's what our management says it is ( in a newsletter to us).....
'Enterprise Resource Planning' refers to a type of commercial off-the-
shelf business software (COTS).
'Enterprise' refers to the fact that the software is provided across
the entire business. The types of 'resources' are those used by the
business - people, money, contracts, services and supplies, fixed
assets, etc. 'Planning' refers to the strategic element. Executives and
managers are given the information they need to do their jobs, and to
do strategic planning quickly and accurately. The system will benefit
the whole organization, not just the departments labeled 'Financial
Office' or 'Human Resources'.

This was in the context of rationalizing why they need to install
PeopleSoft HR, Oracle Financials, and some other packages, at
considerable costs. E.g., to replace our 30-year old systems.

The reason it matters to us (I guess sorta maybe) is that (once we get
the old data converted) we won't be programming much anymore, we'll be
installing/monitoring/versioning the ERP. And we'll have happy
Executives and managers, to boot.
John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: NEWBEE ?? how can I get at the Esc key in cobol — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### NEWBEE ?? how can I get at the Esc key in cobol

- **From:** "michael vasmatics" <vasmati@snet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8487g7$c77@news1.snet.net>`

```
On a pc, using micro focus compiler,
how can I define the Esc key, for that mater, any of the function keys,  so
that its available for use when writing an accept statement.
Not to worry you would not be doing my homework. I was just wondering, I
cant find anything beyond the standard ASCII stuff.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBEE ?? how can I get at the Esc key in cobol

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<848c6u$3s9g$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8487g7$c77@news1.snet.net>`

```

michael vasmatics <vasmati@snet.net> wrote in message
news:8487g7$c77@news1.snet.net...
> On a pc, using micro focus compiler,
> how can I define the Esc key, for that mater, any of the function keys,
so
> that its available for use when writing an accept statement.
> Not to worry you would not be doing my homework. I was just wondering, I
> cant find anything beyond the standard ASCII stuff.
>
Not sure if this is what you want, but the ESC character is stored as
decimal 27 or hex 1B in ASCII and decimal 39 or hex 27 in EBCDIC.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBEE ?? how can I get at the Esc key in cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<848j7p$i20$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<8487g7$c77@news1.snet.net>`

```
michael vasmatics <vasmati@snet.net> wrote:
: On a pc, using micro focus compiler,
: how can I define the Esc key, for that mater, any of the function keys,  so
: that its available for use when writing an accept statement.
: Not to worry you would not be doing my homework. I was just wondering, I
: cant find anything beyond the standard ASCII stuff.


u need to do some CALLs to the x"AF" function to set the ADIS
routines to enable function keys.  Then you need to have 
CRT STATUS IS variable-name to receive the status informaion
with each ACCEPT.

There are other ways of doing it such as running ADISCF.

It is all in the manuals - somewhere.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWBEE ?? how can I get at the Esc key in cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3867FA20.36D691C8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8487g7$c77@news1.snet.net>`

```


michael vasmatics wrote:
> 
> On a pc, using micro focus compiler,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> cant find anything beyond the standard ASCII stuff.

I think the following should put you on the right track :-

*****************************
The CRT STATUS clause of the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph provides a data
item composed as follows:

Status Key 1 - indicates the condition that caused the termination of
the ACCEPT operation
	Status Key 2 - further distinguishes between ACCEPT termination actions
	Status Key 3 - contains the raw keyboard code for the key that
terminated the ACCEPT operation.

The following examples show how the CRT status-key should be coded and
referenced.

************************************************************

*                                                          *
*  The following shows how the special-names paragraph     *
*  sets up both a cursor position field and a CRT status   *
*  key field.                                              *
*                                                          *
************************************************************

special names.
   cursor is cursor-position
   crt status is crt-status.

...

working-storage section.

 01 cursor-position                 pic 9(4).

************************************************************
*  The following group item defines the CRT status key     *
*  field and establishes certain 78-level condition-names  *
*  associated with key fields.                             *
*                                                          *
*  Programs using these definitions should be compiled     *
*  with NOIBMCOMP and MF to function as expected.          *

*                                                          *
************************************************************

 01 crt-status.
    05 crt-status-1                 pic 9.
       88 terminate-key                     value 0.
       88 function-key                      value 1.
       88 adis-key                          value 2.
       88 status-1-error                    value 9.
    05 crt-status-2                 pic 99   comp-x.
       88 esc-key                           value 0.

       88 f1-key                            value 1.
       88 enter-key                         value 1.
       88 fun-key-num-user                  values 0 thru 127.
       88 fun-key-num-system                values 0 thru 26.
    05 crt-status-3                 pic 99   comp-x.
       88 raw-key-code                      values 0 thru 255.
             ...
 procedure-division.
             ...

************************************************************
*                                                          *

*  The following shows the procedural code that would      *
*  evaluate the CRT status keys and direct processing      *
*  accordingly.                                            *
************************************************************

   evaluate terminate-key also function-key also adis-key
    when true also any also any
       if esc-key
           evaluate crt-status-3
             when 0  perform raw-key-0
             when 1  perform raw-key-1
             when 2  perform raw-key-2

             when 3  perform raw-key-3
               ...
           end-evaluate
       else
           perform logic-for-terminator-key
       end-if

    when any also true also any
      evaluate crt-status-2
        when 1  perform user-function-1
        when 2  perform user-function-2
        when 3  perform user-function-3
        when 4  perform user-function-4
        when 5  perform user-function-5
          ...
      end-evaluate

    when any also any also true

      evaluate crt-status-2
         when 1  perform sys-function-1
         when 2  perform sys-function-2
         when 3  perform sys-function-3
         when 4  perform sys-function-4
         when 5  perform sys-function-5
           ...
      end-evaluate
 end-evaluate

*********************************************************************

Also checkout Screen Section, ADIS and ACCEPT and DISPLAY formats.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Foodman, JJ Gavan,

Thanks for your solutions.  I've pasted you solutions into my home cobol etc.
manual.  I've been using COBOL since '66.  Haven't done much COBOL in the '90's,
Mostly C stuff, including  a parsers using yacc for compilers, CICS BMS map
generator, etc.

Started on a Burroughs B280 Improved. It had 4k of memory, 4 tape drives a 1,000
cpm card reader and a 450 cpm card punch.  Someone was always trying to rewind the
card punch.  The punch would would try to punch all the holes, causing it to
crunch, a distinct sound.  When heard it was an immediate call to the C.E.  We
could and did enter the machine language instructions pressing the lights on the
console.

Have fun with the Y2K bugs that I helped create.  Any organization that has not
updated the software long ago deserves their problems.  How 'bout the guy that has
a patent on the comparison & it's assumptions?  Used a similar technique in '69 for
driver lincences for folks born before '00.

Thanks again.

Cheers
Ralph Jones

#### ↳ Re: NEWBEE ?? how can I get at the Esc key in cobol

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84aage$r2c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8487g7$c77@news1.snet.net>`

```
Just define a two-position field in working-storage.

01  WS-KEY  PIC X(02) value space.

After an ACCEPT, ACCEPT WS-KEY from ESCAPE KEY.

WS-KEY will have '01' for ESC, '02' for F1,  '03' for F2, etc.





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: cpp migration MicroFocus Workbench 2 NetExpress — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### cpp migration MicroFocus Workbench 2 NetExpress

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-12-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7VdaZpDGOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```

Hello i have i great problem with netexpress from merant.
the merant german support told me my problem is out of range from the  
hotline.

This is my environment:
=======================
Microsoft windows nt 4.0 service pack 3.0
Merant    netexpress 3.0 service pack 1.0
Microsoft visual c++ 5.x
IBM       universal database 5.2

In the past we use cobol workbench 4.0.26 with no problems.

But now i get this error-code:
173 program not found "checkfil"

a little bit closer:
--------------------
A cpp executable is started, maybe in the netexpress dosbox or direct by
clicking the icon (it dosen't matter).

This cpp executable starts some more dlls (system dlls and some own cpp  
dlls).

I can follow the loadlist by using the c++ debugger and catch the process.

A cobol dll (not static) will be loaded, the runtime cblrtss.dll will be  
loaded.

A few more cpp dlls will be loaded.
The last cpp dll loads a cobol dll (not static).
This cobol dll should load one more cobol dll (not static).
This step fails with the error "173 ... CHECKFIL"

This are the steps i done with no solution:
-------------------------------------------
Start the application from the netexpress dos prompt.
Check the path-environment is less than 256 characters.
Include the netexpress\base\bin and application-path in the path
environment.
Check cobdir and include also the application-path.
Only using must-have directives like CASE and MAKESYN.
Naming the second dll with fullpath does also not solute the problem.

So i think the problem must be between the calling and called program.

I think it's an environment-problem. But what ist the problem?
We build all our cobolsources (more then 750 dlls) as a massmake from the  
commandline.

I didn't understand following results:
--------------------------------------
If i link externl.obj to my cobol-dlls the error 173 changes to 119, which  
means that checkfil is not unique.

So what? CHECKFIL not found or CHECKFIL not unique?

If i use a cobol executable instead of the cpp appication all works fine.

I think it must be my environment, but where is the error or what can i do?

Some more informations:
-----------------------
We do not prefer to build one big dll.
We can't use the CANCEL-Command after calling a dynamic cobol-subprogram,  
because our programs should also run in a mainframe environment.

What can i do?
Our problem is the Y2K-problem. The cobol workbench 4.0.26 is imho not Y2K  
compatible

At Last:
--------
I'm sorry about my terrible english - i hope you can understand this text.
This posting is my last hope
```

## Thread: Hasp problem — 8 msgs

### Hasp problem

- **From:** "Yiannis Patrikiadis" <yiannis@arion.gr>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84agvo$8l6$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
I do have a damaged hasp that is not making the Cobol run properly. Early
version 3.1.41. What should I do to make it work again? any Idea?
```

#### ↳ Re: Hasp problem

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OmlLh9XU$GA.284@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<84agvo$8l6$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
HASP stands for Houston Automatic Spooling Program (or something close to
that).  It is the predecessor to IBM's MVS component JES2 (Job Entry
Subsystem2).  HASP has not been the official name of the JES2 product for
something like 30 years.  How old is the machine you're running?

And if we are talking about JES2 being somehow damaged or corrupted, that's
a red, white, and blue warning signal to contact both your site's sysprogs
immediately (and they may soon be on the line to IBM technical support).

If we're not talking about JES2 being damaged or corrupted, then I also
can't see how it would specifically prevent COBOL from working without
affecting anything else.  JES2 is such a major component of MVS that if
anything happened to it, the Console Operators would be on the line to their
sysprogs right away.  Something as innocent as typing "$P" at the system
console would pretty much lock the mainframe in short order (depending on
overall system load), and that command does nothing more than tell JES2 to
stop accepting new work.

Please, can you more clearly explain your problem?  Please post the sysout
of the error messages (or extract of the syslog or SMF records).


Yiannis Patrikiadis <yiannis@arion.gr> wrote in message
news:84agvo$8l6$1@newssrv.otenet.gr...
> I do have a damaged hasp that is not making the Cobol run properly. Early
> version 3.1.41. What should I do to make it work again? any Idea?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

we do need to know what the poster means by both "HASP" and the COBOL release
in question.

## Thread: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12 — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

- **From:** "michael vasmatics" <vasmati@snet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84bihn$ane@news1.snet.net>`

```
How would I code to get a specific random number range into a table.
DATA DIVISION.
 01 MULTIPLIER        PIC 99  VALUE 0  OCCURS 13 TIMES.
 01 SUB                      PIC 99 .

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
   PERFORM 200-FILL-MULTIPLIER
        VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB  > 13.

200-FILL- MULTIPLIER.
        MOVE FUNCTION RANDOM 0 THRU 12 TO MULTIPLIER (SUB)

Ok will this fill the table with 13 random number between 0 and 12.
I would love to ask how to avoid duplicates, but 1 step at a time will do
just fine.
```

#### ↳ Re: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

- **From:** PAL3000 <pauldub*REMOVETHIS*@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<50lqOCIeuxGcl45j4DAjC4Qjy60M@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<84bihn$ane@news1.snet.net>`

```
Keep a table of flags that indicate whether you used the number yet or
not:

Loop:

      Load rand with random number between 0 and 12

      If flag_table(rand) = "X"
          go to Loop
      else
          move "X" to flag_table(rand)
          move rand to multiplier(Sub)
      end-if

end-Loop


On Tue, 28 Dec 1999 19:09:31 -0800, "michael vasmatics"
<vasmati@snet.net> wrote:

>How would I code to get a specific random number range into a table.
>DATA DIVISION.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>just fine.

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out 
*REMOVETHIS* from my email address
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84e1bm$9vm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<84bihn$ane@news1.snet.net> <50lqOCIeuxGcl45j4DAjC4Qjy60M@4ax.com>`

```
PAL3000 <pauldub*REMOVETHIS*@home.com> wrote:
: Keep a table of flags that indicate whether you used the number yet or
: not:

This may not be the correct way to do it.  In fact the problem 
is not really that of generating '13 random numbers between
0 and 12 with no duplicates', but is really putting the
non-random, fixed numbers in a random order.

This is best done by having a table of 13 items that hold the
numbers 0 to 12 (in order) and a sequence.  Generate 13
random numbers into the sequences and then sort the array
using the sequence as a key.  This will put the numbers 0-12
in a random order.

:>Ok will this fill the table with 13 random number between 0 and 12.
:>I would love to ask how to avoid duplicates, but 1 step at a time will do
:>just fine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

#### ↳ Re: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84dim5$7g8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<84bihn$ane@news1.snet.net>`

```
I would change your code "slightly" as follows:

 DATA DIVISION.
  01 MULTIPLIER        PIC 99  VALUE 0  OCCURS 13 TIMES.
  01 SUB                      PIC 99 .

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    PERFORM 200-FILL-MULTIPLIER
         VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB  > 13.

 200-FILL- MULTIPLIER.
         Compute MULTIPLIER (SUB) = (FUNCTION RANDOM) * 13
            .

This will take the FUNCTION RANDOM output (which is between 0 and 1 - with
lots of decimal points) and convert it into two-digits between 0 and 13.

NOTE: Although some compiler do allow it as an extension, you may NOT use
FUNCTION RANDOM in a MOVE statement in "Standard" COBOL.

NOTE2:  The COBOL "FUNCTION RANDOM" is "pseudo-random" - which means that if
you repeat this same program over and over, you will always get the same
series of "random numbers".  (There is a long explanation of that - and if
you perform that same paragraph from multiple places in your program - you
will NOT get the same numbers each time.)  If you want a "more" random
series, then before your first use of RANDOM (without a seed-value) use one
reference to RANDOM with the some portion of the time-seconds as a seed
value.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

- **From:** "alan russell" <russelah@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84dkmq$9ki@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<84bihn$ane@news1.snet.net> <84dim5$7g8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I've run across this problem before, namely how to not have duplicates in
the selections.  The specific problem was to shuffle a deck of 52 cards
(this problem with the number 13 sounds suspeciously like it).  The trick is
to not look at the problem of generating 52 random numbers between 1 and 52
(or 0 and 51), and having to worry about whether that number has been
generated before.  After all, it could be several hundred iterations before
all 52 possible results are generated.

My solution was to build an array of the results, assign a random number to
each, then sort the random numbers.

01  TABLE-OF-RESULTS.
    05  ONE-RESULT OCCURS 52.
        10  RESULT-VALUE PIC 99.
        10  RESULT-RANDOM PIC S9V9(9).

Put the values 1-52 in RESULT-VALUE (1) - (52), put 52 random numbers into
RESULT-RANDOM (1) - (52).  Sort ONE-RESULT (1) - (52) in ascending order by
RESULT-RANDOM (1) - (52).  In the process, the values in RESULT-VALUE (1) -
(52) will be reordered in random order, and or course each will only occur
once.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

William M. Klein wrote in message <84dim5$7g8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
>I would change your code "slightly" as follows:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>NOTE2:  The COBOL "FUNCTION RANDOM" is "pseudo-random" - which means that
if
>you repeat this same program over and over, you will always get the same
>series of "random numbers".  (There is a long explanation of that - and if
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386AE38A.7A4CA9C1@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<84bihn$ane@news1.snet.net> <84dim5$7g8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <84dkmq$9ki@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
This method for creating a shuffled deck avoids duplicates:
...
  05 DECK OCCURS 52    PIC 99.
  05 SHUFFLE OCCURS 52 PIC 99.
...
PERFORM VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > 52
  MOVE X1 TO DECK(X1)
END-PERFORM
PERFORM VARYING X1 FROM 52 BY -1 UNTIL X1 < 1
  R1 = //Code to pick an random integer from 1 to X1 goes here//
  SHUFFLE(X1) = DECK(R1)
  DECK(R1)=DECK(X1)
END-PERFORM
...
Whenever a card from the deck is chosen, whatever is at the current end of the
deck is moved to replace the chosen card.  HTH

> I've run across this problem before, namely how to not have duplicates in
> the selections.  The specific problem was to shuffle a deck of 52 cards
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> all 52 possible results are generated.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbee ?? generata a random number between 0 and 12

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "alan russell" <russelah@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84dnil$ccn@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<84bihn$ane@news1.snet.net> <84dim5$7g8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <84dkmq$9ki@netnews1.apci.com> <386A5E82.4956C99B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
No, with enough precision in the pseudo-random numbers being generated, it
is highly unlikely that there were will be 2 consecutive identical numbers.
Even if they were stored in such a way that truncation made them identical,
the fact that they are only being used as a way of +ACI-shuffling+ACI- the array of
results means that it will still be a good shuffle.

## Thread: How COPY statement is handled by COBOL complier — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: How COPY statement is handled by COBOL complier

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6hst5pd5k2122@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<83200k$gsv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

sujayg wrote:
> 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> REPLACING.


To my understanding, COPY is a statement that takes a single parameter
which is a variable name, not a file name. That makes sense to me.
When the compiler meets the statements, it inserts the proper variables
and their format into the exact codes.
```

#### ↳ Re: How COPY statement is handled by COBOL complier

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84b22k$hq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<83200k$gsv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <s6hst5pd5k2122@corp.supernews.com>`

```
AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:s6hst5pd5k2122@corp.supernews.com...
>
> sujayg wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.help.com/

Just not true.  The names referenced in the COPY member are *not* variables
(although I think some compilers allow this as an extension).  Now how the
implementor "translates" a name in the COPY statement to a physical file is
implementor-defined. (For example, IBM mainframe compilers use the "of
library-name" to point to a DDName - other vendors allow you to automatically
add a ".cpy" or ".cbl" extension to the file-name) - but no Standard
conforming implementation treats these names as "variables" - when running in
Standard conforming mode.
```

#### ↳ Re: How COPY statement is handled by COBOL complier

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lxxa4.320$zp.4764@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<83200k$gsv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <s6hst5pd5k2122@corp.supernews.com>`

```
the COPY statement can be continued on multiple lines:  I've done
10 lines.  You MUST NOT break any words or text

AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:s6hst5pd5k2122@corp.supernews.com...
>
> sujayg wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How COPY statement is handled by COBOL complier

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84egck$ok4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<83200k$gsv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <s6hst5pd5k2122@corp.supernews.com> <Lxxa4.320$zp.4764@news3.mia>`

```
James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:Lxxa4.320$zp.4764@news3.mia...
> the COPY statement can be continued on multiple lines:  I've done
> 10 lines.  You MUST NOT break any words or text
>

Sorry, this is another incorrect statement.  The following is perfectly valid
in any ANSI'85 conforming compiler:

       Co
-         py   aMemb
-                 er of what
-                  ever Replacing  =="ABC
-                                                "def"==    b
-                                                             y a-COBOL-
-                                                                word .

Now, I am *NOT* recommending anyone to code ANY source statement like that -
but the (existing) Standard requires that a conforming implementation MUST
support such code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How COPY statement is handled by COBOL complier

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-12-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386B56E2.58759FE6@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<83200k$gsv$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <s6hst5pd5k2122@corp.supernews.com> <Lxxa4.320$zp.4764@news3.mia> <84egck$ok4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Yes, the committee were even asked for a ruling on this and said
"Bill is Right". See Cobol Information Bulletin CIB-25,
clarification A-52. The example they considered was 

7   12
    C
-   O
-   P
-   Y text1. 

And another where text1 was continued.

Both were ruled valid.

Tim Josling

William M. Klein wrote:
> > 10 lines.  You MUST NOT break any words or text
> Sorry, this is another incorrect statement.  The following is perfectly valid
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> -                                                                word .
```

```
After reading Chapter 24 of Thane's COBOL in 24 hours book, about using sp2
as a GUI for a program, I compiled the provided source and linked the obj
with sp2.lib and copied sp2.dll into the same directory as the binary.  When
I run the executable, the program dumps.  This is an excerpt of the error --
minus the hex.

CHAPT24A caused an invalid page fault in
module SP2.DLL at 0177:1001b150.

Does anyone have a solution??

Compiler is Fujitsu v3
Platform is x86 w/ Win98
Source can be provided if necessary

-Steve Kalemkiewicz
```

>After reading Chapter 24 of Thane's COBOL in 24 hours book, about using sp2
>as a GUI for a program, I compiled the provided source and linked the obj
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Steve:

The CD included with the book actually has 2 different versions of
sp2...one for the 16 bit compiler and one for the 32 bit compiler.

If you want to send a bit more detail to me via private e-mail
message, we can figure out what the problem is and then post the final
results here so that everyone knows what the problem was.

Which version of the Fujitsu compiler are you using and which version
of sp2 are you using?  Any additional details sent to
rtwolfe@flexus.com would help me pinpoint the problem.

Thanks!



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com

#### ↳ Re: sp2 page fault when run

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84apu7$7ob$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3MT94.59$r6.10244@news.flash.net>`

```
Thane hasn't seen this note yet in his news reader, but sent the following
reply,

"Steven - sorry for the problem!

Go here:

http://www.mcp.com/info/0-672/0-672-31453-3/

The .PAN file shipped on the CD for chapter 24 was corrupt.  A
corrected version can be downloaded from there.

Also, you cannot move the installation to another machine or
directory.  This is the reason that for Chapter 24 I have you
executing from the sp2 install directory.  The evaluation version of
sp2 will not allow you to move the .dll.  This is to prevent
unauthorized distribution of applications developed with the trial
version."
```

#### ↳ Re: sp2 page fault when run

- **From:** "Steven Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@flash.net>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cQ7a4.546$r6.69735@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<3MT94.59$r6.10244@news.flash.net>`

```
Thanks everyone!  I downloaded the *.pan file, re-compiled, re-linked, and
executed the program in the sp2 directory and everything worked well.

Once again Thank you.

-Steve Kalemkiewicz
```

#### ↳ Re: sp2 page fault when run

- **From:** ThaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-NeWESjWfybY9@localhost>`

```
On Tue, 28 Dec 1999 00:46:55, "Steven Kalemkiewicz" 
<senseisk@flash.net> wrote:

> After reading Chapter 24 of Thane's COBOL in 24 hours book, about using sp2
> as a GUI for a program, I compiled the provided source and linked the obj
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Sure!  Two things.  First you might want to visit this web site:
http://www.mcp.com/info/0-672/0-672-31453-3/

It contains the eratta from the book and the sp2 panel file for 
chapter 24 (It was corrupt on the first few pressings of the CD).

Second - you need to compile and run in the sp2 install directory.  
The evaluation version is tied to that directory to prevent 
unauthorized distribution of the software developed with the 
evaluation version.

There is a screen cam movie on the CD that shows the entire compile 
and link process, with the proper paths.  If you have any other 
problems, don't hesitate to email me.  I'll be glad to help.
```

## Thread: COBOL Data Type — 6 msgs

### COBOL Data Type

- **From:** "Olaf Deutsch" <ODeutsch@T-ONLINE.DE>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<849kia$hmp$1@news07.btx.dtag.de>`

```
I'd like to write a VB program that reads data files created with COBOL.
 Could you please explain how to interpret a data file if it was created
with the comp keyword (comp-3).

PiC Statement:

PIC 9(7)v99 comp-3
PIC 9(7)v99 comp-3
PIC 9(7)v99 comp-3
....

Olaf
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Data Type

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6hpcflv5k295@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<849kia$hmp$1@news07.btx.dtag.de>`

```

Olaf Deutsch wrote:
> 
> I'd like to write a VB program that reads data files created with COBOL.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> PIC 9(7)v99 comp-3

In COBOL, there are data format for variables that have numeric values
that can be used in Mathematic Calculations. They are called COMPUTABLE.
They are COMP-1, COMP-2, COMP-3, and COMP-4.

Programmers use COMP-3 most, for it is COBOL packed-decimal-format.
I do not think the other computer languages can read COBOL COMP-3 format.
The number 123 is 3 bytes but it can be in compressed format as 2 bytes.
The number 12345 is 3 bytes in compressed format instead of 5, for example.
To determin the size of a number in compressed format, just add 1 to the
size of the number and then devide by 2. COBOL programmers use this
format to save the room to store variables.

In the past, I have done experiments with VB binary files and VC++
binary files. They are not compatible. I have never done experiments with
VB reading COBOL COMP-3 format, but I have done experiments with VB
reading COBOL ASCII format and they are compatible.

If you want to use VB with COBOL files, use COBOL to convert COMP-3 (packed
decimal format) into its display format (ASCII) and they will work
together. When you use VB to read COBOL output files, you should use
Open statement for random, and the fields all are String, including numeric
fields, and do not forget to add the last 2 bytes for the NewLine and
Carridge Return for each line.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Data Type

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-12-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

compilers - and even there, it really is talking about OLD (pre '85)
compilers.

is not (usually) correct or relevant.
```

<a id="t-eb21fb6809"></a>
## Thread: invalid key clause.... — 3 this month / 11 total

### invalid key clause....

- **From:** morella3253@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-12-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84bsiq$ume$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi to all,

I need help in the following problems:

1) I have a program that reads an indexed file. The last record in
   the file has zero values in the key. When this record is read
   the program should close the file and exit, however, this is not
   happening:

   if mobile-num = zeros
   then
      write file-name invalid key display "error with key"
      display "last record read"
      go to aa-close-files
   end-if.

It appears that the invalid key clause seems to be grouping all the
statements below it. This means that the section aa-close-files and
the display "last record read" statement are not processed. Control
returns after the end-if since no invalid key problem occurs. Why
is this happening?

2) I have an indexed file with the key:

   05 mobile-no pic x(10).
   05 end-date  pic 9(6) COMP-3.
   05 end-time  pic 9(6) COMP-3.

I want to read each record and then write it with a new valued
for end-date and end-time in the same file. For example, first record
read and put end-date = '990506', end-time = '170501', second record
read and put end-date = '990506', end-time = '170502' etc...

Since the key is being updated, I read the record, saved it, then
deleted it and then wrote it again with the new values.

When I ran the program for the whole file, it updated the first and
second record but then looped continuously between the first and second
record.
I know this is a problem with the indexes but why is it happening?
Should I just write to another file rather than trying to update the
records in the same file by reading a record and then deleting and then
writing it again.

3) I have some old programs that seem to compile without using
   the invalid key clause (note that the compilation is done through
   utility programs which I do not have access to).
   However, any new programs I have written do not compile without
   the invalid key clause. Is this due a compile flag I am missing?

Thanks in advance
Morella.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
See below

#### ↳ Re: invalid key clause....
